Cortés answered through our interpreters — Doña Marina above all — and told him that he did not know with what he, nor any of us, could repay the great favors we received each day; that we truly came from where the sun rises; and that we were vassals and servants of a great lord called Emperor Don Carlos, who had under him many great princes as subjects. Having heard of him and of what a great lord he was, he had sent us to these parts to see him and to beg that they become Christians, as our Emperor and all of us were, and that he and all his vassals would save their souls; and that hereafter he would declare more fully how and in what manner that was to be, and how we worshiped one true God alone, and who He is, and many other good things they would hear — as he had already told his ambassadors Tendile, Pitalpitoque, and Quintalvor when we were in the sand hills.

When this speech was finished, the great Moctezuma had ready very rich jewels of gold and many cast pieces, which he gave to our captain; and likewise to each of our captains he gave little things of gold and three loads of rich feather-work cloaks; and among all the soldiers he gave each of us two loads of cloaks as well, with great joy. In everything he looked every inch a great lord. And when he had finished distributing these gifts, he asked Cortés whether we were all brothers and vassals of our great Emperor. Cortés said yes — that we were brothers in love and friendship, and very principal persons, and servants of our great king and lord. And because other exchanges of good courtesies passed between Moctezuma and Cortés, and because this was the first time he came to visit us, and so as not to weary him, they broke off the talk. Moctezuma had ordered his stewards that we should be supplied, in our own manner and custom, with maize, grinding stones, and Indian women to make bread, chickens and fruit, and much fodder for the horses. Moctezuma took his leave of our captain and all of us with great courtesy, and we went out with him to the street. Cortés ordered us that for the present we should not go very far from our quarters, until we understood better what was fitting. And here I leave off, and I will tell what happened next —</english>
<spanish>CAPÍTULO LXXXIX — Cómo el gran Moctezuma vino a nuestros aposentos, con muchos caciques que le acompañaban, y la plática que tuvo con nuestro capitán —

Como el gran Moctezuma había comido y supo que nuestro capitán y todos nosotros así mismo había buen rato que habíamos hecho lo mismo, vino a nuestro aposento con gran copia de principales y todos debidos suyos, y con gran pompa. Y como a Cortés le dijeron que venía, le salió a mitad de la sala a recibir, y el Moctezuma le tomó por la mano. Trajeron unos como asentaderos, hechos a su usanza, y muy ricos, y labrados de muchas maneras con oro, y el Moctezuma dijo a nuestro capitán que se sentase; y sentáronse entrambos, cada uno en el suyo. Y luego comenzó el Moctezuma un muy buen parlamento, y dijo que en gran manera se holgaba de tener en su casa y reino unos caballeros tan esforzados como era el capitán Cortés y todos nosotros. Y que había dos años que tuvo noticia de otro capitán que vino a lo de Champotón, y también el año pasado le trajeron nuevas de otro capitán que vino con cuatro navíos, y que siempre los deseó ver; y que agora que nos tiene ya consigo, para servirnos y darnos de todo lo que tuviese; y que verdaderamente debe de ser cierto que somos los que sus antecesores, muchos tiempos pasados, habían dicho que vendrían hombres de donde sale el sol, a señorear estas tierras, y que debemos ser nosotros, pues tan valientemente peleamos en lo de Potonchán y Tabasco, y con los tascaltecas, porque todas las batallas se las trajeron pintadas al natural.

Y Cortés le respondió, con nuestras lenguas, especial la doña Marina, y le dijo que no sabe con qué pagar él ni todos nosotros las grandes mercedes recibidas de cada día; y que ciertamente veníamos de donde sale el sol, y somos vasallos y criados de un gran señor, que se dice el emperador don Carlos, que tiene sujetos a sí muchos y grandes príncipes; y que teniendo noticia de él, y de cuán gran señor es, nos envió a estas partes a le ver, y a rogar que sean cristianos, como es nuestro emperador y todos nosotros, y que salvarán sus ánimas, él y todos sus vasallos; y que adelante les declararía más, cómo y de qué manera ha de ser, y cómo adoramos a un solo Dios verdadero, y quién es, y otras muchas buenas cosas que oirán, como les había dicho a sus embajadores Tendile, Pitalpitoque y Quintalvor, cuando estábamos en los arenales.

Y acabado este parlamento, tenía apercibido el gran Moctezuma muy ricas joyas de oro, y de muchas echuras que dio a nuestro capitán; y así mismo a cada uno de nuestros capitanes dio cositas de oro y tres cargas de mantas de labores ricas de pluma; y entre todos los soldados también nos dio a cada uno dos cargas de mantas, con una alegría. Y en todo bien parecía gran señor. Y desque lo hubo repartido, preguntó a Cortés si éramos todos hermanos y vasallos de nuestro gran emperador. Y dijo que sí, que éramos hermanos en el amor y amistad, y personas muy principales, y criados de nuestro gran rey y señor. Y porque pasaron otras pláticas de buenos comedimientos entre Moctezuma y Cortés, y por ser esta la primera vez que nos venía a visitar, y por no le ser pesado, cesaron los razonamientos. Y había mandado el Moctezuma a sus mayordomos que a nuestro modo y usanza de todo estuviésemos proveídos: ques mayíz, y piedras, e indias para hacer pan, y gallinas y fruta, y mucha yerba para los caballos. Y el Moctezuma se despidió con gran cortesía de nuestro capitán y de todos nosotros, y salimos con él hasta la calle; y Cortés nos mandó que al presente no fuésemos muy lejos de los aposentos, hasta entender más lo que conviniese. Y quédese aquí, y diré lo que adelante pasó —</spanish>
<flags>voice: Bernal Díaz del Castillo — blunt, garrulous old soldier, plain and concrete, quietly aggrieved, proud eyewitness (held). ⚠ NO REFERENCE —. Colonial spellings modernized throughout Spanish (monteçuma→Moctezuma, ç→c/z, avn→aún, prinçipales, rreçebir, esforçados, antecesores, xpianos→cristianos, mds→mercedes, aperçebido, pareçer, çesaron, yndias→indias, yerva, cavallos→caballos, etc.). Folio headers, page numbers, and signature line omitted. "debidos suyos" kept as vassals/subjects owing him tribute (Bernal Díaz's term). "echuras" rendered as cast pieces (gold castings). Ambiguity retained: "le tomó por la mano" rendered literally (Moctezuma took Cortés by the hand); manuscript syntax also allows the reversed gesture — standard alternate reading not imposed. Diplomatic/hearsay layer (prophecy of men from the east, painted battle reports) is Moctezuma's reported speech, not Bernal Díaz's independent claim. Closing formula "quédese aquí, y diré lo que adelante pasó" kept as chapter break.</flags>

=== CAPITULO LXXXX como luego, otro dia, — Section 91 ===
<english>Chapter XC — How, a few days later, our captain went to see the great Moctezuma, and of certain talks they had.

Three days later, Cortés agreed to go to Moctezuma’s palaces. First he sent word to find out what he was doing and that we were on our way. He took with him four captains — Pedro de Alvarado, Juan Velázquez de León, Diego de Ordaz, and Gonzalo de Sandoval — and five of us soldiers went as well. When Moctezuma learned of it, he came out to receive us halfway through the hall, very well attended by his nephews, because other lords did not enter or go where Moctezuma was unless the business was important. With great ceremony, which he showed Cortés and Cortés showed him, they took each other by the hand. Where his dais stood, he had Cortés sit on his right hand, and likewise he had all of us sit on seats that he ordered brought there.

Cortés began to address them through our interpreters Doña Marina and Aguilar. He said that now that he had come to see and speak to so great a lord as he was, and was rested, and all of us having fulfilled the journey and the command that our great king and lord had given him — and to what matters most for him to say, on behalf of our Lord God — that by now His Majesty would have understood from his ambassadors Tendile, Pitalpitoque, and Quintalvor, when he granted us the favor of sending us the gold sun and moon at the sandbar, as we told them we were Christians and worshipped one true God called Jesus Christ, who suffered death and passion to save us, and we told them that a cross, which they asked why we worshipped, was the sign of another where our Lord God was crucified for our salvation, and that this death and passion, which he permitted to be so, was to save by it the whole human lineage that stood lost, and that this our God rose on the third day and is in the heavens, and is he who made heaven and earth and sea and sands, and created all things that are in the world, and gives the waters and the rains, and nothing is done in the world without his holy will, and that in him we believe and worship, and that those they hold for gods are not so but devils, very evil things, and as bad as their figures look, worse are their deeds, and that they should see how evil and worthless they are, and that where we have crosses set up, like those their ambassadors saw, they do not dare show themselves before them for fear of them, and that in time they would see it. And what he now asked of him by His Majesty was that he attend to the words he now wished to tell him.

Then he told him, very plainly made to understand, of the creation of the world, and how we are all brothers, children of one father and one mother called Adam and Eve, and how as such a brother our great Emperor, grieving the perdition of the souls — and they are many — that those idols of his carry down to hell, where they burn in living flames, sent us for this that he had heard, to remedy it and not worship those idols, nor sacrifice Indians to them any more, men or women, since we are all brothers, nor permit sodomies or robberies. And he told them more: that in time our king and lord would send men who live among us very saintly, better than we are, to make it understood to him, because for the present we came only to notify him of it, and so he asked it of him by His Majesty, that he do and fulfill it.

And because it seemed Moctezuma wished to answer this talk of Cortés, Cortés said to all of us who went with him that with this we had fulfilled our part, as the first approach, and Moctezuma answered:

“Lord Malinche, I have very well understood your talks and reasonings before now, which you told my servants before this at the sandbar — that of three gods and of the cross, and all the things you have preached in the towns through which you have come. We have not answered you to any of them, because from the beginning until now we have worshipped our gods and hold them for good; yours ought to be so likewise. Do not trouble yourselves, for the present, to speak to us of them. And as for the creation of the world, we have held it believed thus for many ages past. And for this cause we hold it certain that you are those whom our ancestors told us would come from where the sun rises. And to that your great king I am in charge, and I will give him of what I may have. Because, as I have said before, scarcely two years ago I had notice of captains who came with ships by the way you came, and they said they were servants of that your great king. I wished to know whether you are all one. And Cortés told him yes, that we were all brothers and servants of our Emperor, and that those came to see the road and seas and ports, to know them very well, and for us to come as we came.”

And Moctezuma said it — meaning Francisco Hernández de Córdoba and Grijalva, when we came to discover the land the first time — and said that from then he had thought to have some of those men who were coming, to keep them in his realms and city to honor them, and that since his gods had fulfilled their good desires and we were already in his house, which may be called ours, that we should take pleasure and rest, that there we would be his subjects, and that if sometimes he sent to tell us not to enter his city, it was not of his will but because his vassals were afraid, for they were told we cast lightning and thunderbolts, and with the horses killed many Indians, and that we were fierce teules [gods], and other childish things; and that now he had seen our persons, and that we are of bone and flesh and of much reason, and knows we are very stout men, and for these causes holds us in much greater esteem than they had told him, and that he would give us of what he had. And Cortés and all of us answered that we held it in great favor, so overflowing a will.

Then Moctezuma said, laughing — for in everything he was very merry in his speech as a great lord — “Malinche, I know well they have told you, those of Tlaxcala with whom you have taken such friendship, that I, who am as a god or teotl [god], and that all there is in my houses is all gold and silver and rich stones. I have well known that, as you are understanding men, you would not believe it, and you would take it for mockery what you now see, lord Malinche — my body of bone and flesh like yours, my houses and palaces of stone and wood and lime, as befits a lord; I am a great king if I am one, and I have the riches of my ancestors if I have them — but not the follies and lies they have told you of me. So you will likewise take for mockery, as I take yours, your thunder and lightning.”

And Cortés answered him likewise laughing, and said that enemies always say evil and untrue things of those they wish ill, and that he had well known that another lord in these parts, more magnificent, he does not expect to see, and that not without cause is he so named before our Emperor.

And while they were in these talks, Moctezuma secretly ordered a great cacique, a nephew of his, of those who were in his company, to tell his stewards to bring certain pieces of gold that seemed they ought to have been set apart to give to Cortés, and ten loads of fine cloth; which he divided, the gold and mantles, among Cortés and the four captains, and to us soldiers he gave each two collars of gold, each collar worth ten pesos, and two loads of mantles. All the gold he then gave amounted to more than a thousand pesos, and he gave it with a joy and bearing of a great and valorous lord.

And because the hour was past midday, and so as not to be more burdensome to him, Cortés told him: “Lord Moctezuma, you always have the custom of heaping one favor upon another in doing us more each day; now it is time that you eat.” And Moctezuma answered that rather, for having gone to visit him, we had done him more. And so we took leave with great courtesies on his part, and we went to our quarters, and we went talking of the good manner and breeding he showed in everything, and that we would show him much respect in everything, and with our quilted morion caps removed when we passed before him — and so we did — and let us leave it here, and pass on to what came after.</english>
<spanish>Capítulo XC — Cómo, poco después, otro día, fue nuestro capitán a ver al gran Moctezuma, y de ciertas pláticas que tuvieron.

A los tres días, acordó Cortés de ir a los palacios de Moctezuma. Primero envió a saber qué hacía, y que supiese cómo íbamos, y llevó consigo cuatro capitanes, que fueron Pedro de Alvarado, Juan Velázquez de León, Diego de Ordaz y Gonzalo de Sandoval, y también fuimos cinco soldados. Y como Moctezuma lo supo, salió a recibirnos a mitad de la sala, muy acompañado de sus sobrinos, porque otros señores no entraban ni comunicaban adonde Moctezuma estaba, si no eran en negocios importantes. Y con gran acato, que hizo a Cortés y Cortés a él, se tomaron por las manos. Y adonde estaba su estrado le hizo sentar a la mano derecha, y así mismo nos mandó sentar a todos nosotros en asientos que allí mandó traer.

Y Cortés les comenzó a hacer un razonamiento, con nuestras lenguas doña Marina y Aguilar, y dijo que ahora que había venido a ver y hablar a un tan gran señor como era, y estaba descansado, y todos nosotros, pues habíamos cumplido el viaje y mandado que nuestro gran rey y señor le mandó, y a lo que más le viene a decir, de parte de nuestro señor Dios, es que ya su majestad habrá entendido de sus embajadores Tendile, Pitalpitoque y Quintalvor, cuando nos hizo las mercedes de enviarnos la luna y el sol de oro al arenal, como les dijimos que éramos cristianos y adoramos a un solo Dios verdadero, que se dice Jesucristo, el cual padeció muerte y pasión por salvarnos, y les dijimos que una cruz, que nos preguntaron por qué la adorábamos, que fue señal de otra donde nuestro señor Dios fue crucificado por nuestra salvación, y que esta muerte y pasión, que permitió que así fuese, por salvar por ella todo el linaje humano que estaba perdido, y que este nuestro Dios resucitó al tercero día y está en los cielos, y es el que hizo el cielo y tierra y la mar y arenas, y crió todas las cosas que hay en el mundo, y da las aguas y rocíos, y ninguna cosa se hace en el mundo sin su santa voluntad, y que en él creemos y adoramos, y que aquellos que ellos tienen por dioses no lo son, sino diablos, que son cosas muy malas, y cuales tienen las figuras, peores tienen los hechos, y que mirasen cuán malos son y de poca valía, que adonde tenemos puestas cruces, como las que vieron sus embajadores, con temor de ellas no osan parecer delante, y que el tiempo andando lo verán. Y lo que ahora le pide por majestad es que esté atento a las palabras que ahora le quiere decir.

Y luego le dijo, muy bien dado a entender, de la creación del mundo, y cómo todos somos hermanos, hijos de un padre y de una madre que se decían Adán y Eva, y cómo tal hermano nuestro gran Emperador, doliéndose de la perdición de las ánimas que son muchas, las que aquellos sus ídolos llevan al infierno, donde arden a vivas llamas, nos envió para esto que haya oído lo remedie y no adore aquellos ídolos, ni les sacrifiquen más indios ni indias, pues todos somos hermanos, ni consienta sodomías ni robos. Y más les dijo que el tiempo andando enviaría nuestro rey y señor unos hombres que entre nosotros viven muy santamente, mejores que nosotros, para que se lo den a entender, porque al presente no venimos más que a se lo notificar, y así se lo pide por majestad que lo haga y cumpla.

Y porque pareció que Moctezuma quería responder a eso Cortés la plática, dijo a todos nosotros que con él fuimos, con esto cumplimos por ser el primer toque, y Moctezuma respondió:

—Señor Malinche, muy bien tengo entendidas vuestras pláticas y razonamientos, antes de ahora, que a mis criados antes de esto les dijisteis en el arenal: eso de tres dioses y de la cruz, y todas las cosas que en los pueblos por donde habéis venido habéis predicado; no os hemos respondido a cosa ninguna de ellas, porque desde el principio acá adoramos nuestros dioses y los tenemos por buenos; así deben ser los vuestros. No curéis, al presente, de hablarnos de ellos. Y en eso de la creación del mundo, así lo tenemos nosotros creído muchos tiempos a pasados. Y a esta causa tenemos por cierto que sois los que nuestros antecesores nos dijeron que vendrían de adonde sale el sol. Y a ese vuestro gran rey yo le soy en cargo, y le daré de lo que tuviere, porque, como dicho tengo, otra vez, bien a dos años tengo noticia de capitanes que vinieron con navíos por donde vosotros vinisteis, y decían que eran criados de ese vuestro gran rey; quería saber si sois todos unos. Y Cortés le dijo que sí, que todos éramos hermanos y criados de nuestro Emperador, y que aquellos vinieron a ver el camino y mares y puertos, para lo saber muy bien y venir nosotros como venimos.

Y decíalo Moctezuma por lo de Francisco Hernández de Córdoba y Grijalva, cuando vinimos a descubrir la primera vez, y dijo que desde entonces tuvo pensamiento de haber algunos de aquellos hombres que venían, para tener en sus reinos y ciudad para honrarlos, y que pues sus dioses les habían cumplido sus buenos deseos y ya estábamos en su casa, las cuales que se pueden llamar nuestras, que holgásemos y tuviésemos descanso, que allí seríamos suyos, y que si algunas veces nos enviaba a decir que no entrásemos en su ciudad, no era de su voluntad, sino porque sus vasallos tenían temor, que les decían que echábamos rayos y relámpagos, y con los caballos matábamos muchos indios, y que éramos teules bravos, y otras cosas de niñerías; y que ahora que había visto nuestras personas, y que somos de hueso y de carne y de mucha razón, y sabe que somos muy esforzados, y por estas causas nos tiene en mucha más estima que le habían dicho, y que nos daría de lo que tuviese. Y Cortés y todos nosotros respondimos que se lo teníamos en gran merced, tan sobrada voluntad.

Y luego Moctezuma dijo, riendo — porque en todo era muy regocijado en su hablar de gran señor —: «Malinche, bien sé que te han dicho esos de Tlaxcala, con quien tanta amistad habéis tomado, que yo, que soy como dios o teotl, y que cuanto hay en mis casas es todo oro y plata y piedras ricas; bien tengo conocido que, como sois entendidos, no lo creheríades, y lo tendríais por burla lo que ahora, señor Malinche, veis: mi cuerpo de hueso y de carne, como los vuestros, mis casas y palacios de piedra y madera y cal, de señor; yo gran rey, si soy, y tener riquezas de mis antecesores, si tengo; mas no las locuras y mentiras que de mí os han dicho. Así que también lo tendréis por burla, como yo tengo de vuestros truenos y relámpagos.»

Y Cortés le respondió también riendo, y dijo que los contrarios, enemigos, siempre dicen cosas malas y sin verdad de los que quieren mal, y que bien había conocido que otro señor en estas partes, más magnífico, no le espera ver, y que no sin causa es tan nombrado delante nuestro Emperador.

Y estando en estas pláticas, mandó secretamente Moctezuma a un gran cacique sobrino suyo, de los que estaban en su compañía, que mandasen a sus mayordomos que trajesen ciertas piezas de oro que parece ser debieran estar apartadas para dar a Cortés, y diez cargas de ropa fina; lo cual repartió, el oro y mantas, entre Cortés y los cuatro capitanes, y a nosotros los soldados nos dio a cada uno dos collares de oro, que valdría cada collar diez pesos, y dos cargas de mantas. Valía todo el oro que entonces dio sobre mil pesos, y esto daba con una alegría y semblante de grande y valeroso señor.

Y porque pasaba la hora más de medio día, y por no serle más importuno, le dijo Cortés: «Señor Moctezuma, siempre tiene por costumbre de echarnos un cargo sobre otro en hacernos cada día más; ya es hora que vuestra merced coma.» Y Moctezuma respondió que antes, por haberle ido a visitar, le hicimos más. Y así nos despedimos, con grandes cortesías de él, y nos fuimos a nuestros aposentos, y íbamos platicando de la buena manera y crianza que en todo tenía, y que nosotros en todo le tuviésemos mucho acato, y con las gorras de armas acolchadas quitadas cuando delante de él pasásemos, y así lo hacíamos; y déjese aquí, y pasemos adelante.</spanish>
<flags>voice: Bernal Díaz del Castillo — blunt, garrulous old soldier, plain and concrete, quietly aggrieved, proud eyewitness (held). ⚠ NO REFERENCE —. Colonial spellings modernized throughout Spanish (monteçuma→Moctezuma, ç→c/z, avn→aún, prinçipales, rreçebir, esforçados, antecesores, xpianos→cristianos, mds→mercedes, aperçebido, pareçer, çesaron, yndias→indias, yerva, cavallos→caballos, etc.). Folio headers, page numbers, signature line, and "Digitized by Google" omitted. "se tomaron por las manos" kept reciprocal (they took each other by the hand); manuscript also allows reversed gesture — alternate reading not imposed. Moctezuma's reported speech (ancestors from the east, painted battle reports, teotl/god comparison, Tlaxcalan gossip) rendered as diplomatic/hearsay layer, not Bernal Díaz's independent claim. "debidos suyos" / "suyos" rendered as his subjects/vassals. OCR/damage: "ṕaticando" read as platicando; closing "con grandes cortesias del" completed as "de él" from context [supplied]. "tres dioses" kept as Moctezuma's summary of prior preaching. teotl [god] glossed once in English. Closing formula "déjese aquí, y pasemos adelante" kept as chapter break.</flags>

=== CAPITULO XCI—de la manera, E persona — Section 92 ===
<english>CHAPTER XCI — On the manner and person of the great Moctezuma, and what a great lord he was.

The great Moctezuma was about forty years old, of good stature and well proportioned, slender, with little flesh, and his color neither very dark nor light, but the proper hue and complexion of an Indian. He wore his hair not very long, only as far as his ears, and had few black hairs for a beard, well set and thin, and his face somewhat long and cheerful, and his eyes of good bearing. He showed in his person, in the way he looked at people, love on one side, and when gravity was needed he was very polished and clean. He bathed once a day in the afternoon, near the Angelus.¹ He had many women as mistresses, daughters of lords, although he had two great chieftainesses as his legitimate wives; and when he lay with them he did it so secretly that none could know of it except some of those who served him. He was very clean of sodomies. The cloaks and garments he put on one day he did not put on again until four days later. He had more than two hundred leading men of his guard in other halls next to his own, and these were not so that all might speak with him, but one by one; and when they went to speak with him they had to take off their rich cloaks and put on others of little value, but they had to be clean, and they had to enter barefoot with their eyes cast down to the ground and not look him in the face; and with three reverences that they made to him, saying in them, “Lord, my lord, my great lord,” before they reached him, and after they gave him their report of what they had come for, he dismissed them in few words. They did not turn their backs when taking leave of him, but their face and eyes down to the ground toward where he sat, and not with their backs turned, until they left the hall.

And another thing I saw: when other great lords came from distant lands for lawsuits or business, when they reached the lodgings of the great Moctezuma they had to come barefoot and in poor cloaks, and they were not to enter straight into the palaces but to go around a little to one side of the palace door — for to enter headlong they took as disrespect. At mealtimes his cooks had more than thirty kinds of stews made in his manner and custom, and they kept them on small earthen braziers underneath so they would not grow cold; and of what the great Moctezuma was to eat they prepared more than three hundred dishes, without counting a thousand more for the guard; and when he was to eat, Moctezuma would sometimes come out with his leading men and stewards, and they would point out which stew was better and of what birds and things it was made, and of what they told him he was to eat; and when he came out to see it, it was few times and as if for pastime. I hear tell that they used to cook him the flesh of boys of little age, and because he had so many different stews and so many kinds of things, we did not trouble to see whether it was human flesh or other things, because daily they cooked for him hens, wattled cocks, pheasants, land partridges, quail, tame and wild ducks, venison, pork of the land, little reed birds, and doves, and hares, and rabbits, and many kinds of birds and things bred in these lands, which are so many that I cannot name them so quickly, and so we did not look into it; but I know that certainly, after our captain reproved him for the sacrifice and eating of human flesh, from then on he ordered that such fare not be cooked for him — let us leave off speaking of this, and return to the manner he kept in his service at

mealtime || and it is in this manner: that if it was cold, they had made for him a great fire of chips from a wood of tree bark that made no smoke; the odor of the bark from which they made those fires was very fragrant, and so that they would not give him more heat than he wanted they placed before him a kind of carved board with gold and other figures of idols, and he sat on a low seat, rich and soft, and the table likewise low, made in the same fashion as the seats, and there they laid his tablecloths of white cloaks and certain napkins, somewhat long, of the same; and four very beautiful and clean women gave him water for his hands in deep vessels like hand-basins, which they call xicales [gourds], placing others beneath to catch the water, others like plates, and they gave him his towels; and two other women brought him bread of tortillas, and when he began to eat they set before him a kind of wooden screen, very painted in gold, so that he would not be seen eating; and the four women stood apart, and four great old lords of rank and years stood at his sides, with whom Moctezuma from time to time conversed and asked things; and as a great favor he gave each of these old men a plate of what he liked best, and they said that those old men were his very close subjects and counselors and judges of lawsuits, and the plate and fare that Moctezuma gave them they ate standing and with great ceremony, and all without looking him in the face. He was served with Cholula pottery, one red and one black; while he ate, not for a moment were those of his guard to make uproar or speak loudly, who stood in the halls near Moctezuma’s. They brought him fruit of all that there was in the land, but he ate very little of it, only now and then. They brought in vessels like cups of fine gold a certain drink made from the same cacao, which they said was to give access to women, and then we did not look into it; but what I saw was that they brought more than fifty great jars of good cacao with its foam, and of that he drank, and the women served him in drinking with great ceremony; and sometimes at mealtime there were hunchbacked Indians, very ugly, because

they were small of body and broken in the middle of their bodies. Among them were buffoons, and other Indians who must have been tricksters, who spoke witty things to him, and others who leaped and danced for him, because Moctezuma was fond of pleasures and songs; and those he ordered to be given the leftovers and jars of cacao, and the same four women took up the tablecloths and again gave him water for his hands, and with the great ceremony they showed him Moctezuma spoke with those four old leading men of things that concerned him, and they took leave of him with the great reverence they owed him, and he remained resting; and after the great Moctezuma had eaten, straightway all of his guard ate, and many others of his household servants, and it seems to me they took out more than a thousand dishes of those fares I have spoken of, besides jars of cacao with its foam, as is made among Mexicans, more than two thousand, and fruit without number; then for his women and maidservants, and bakers, and cacao-makers, what great expense he had — let us leave off speaking of the cost and food of his household, and speak of the stewards, and treasurers, and pantries and cellars, and of those who had charge of the houses where they kept the maize; I say that there was so much to write, each thing by itself, that I do not know where to begin, save that we stood astonished at the great order and supply he had in everything; and I say further that I had forgotten, and it is well to tell it again, that they served Moctezuma at table when he ate, as I have said, two other very graceful women to bring tortillas kneaded with eggs and other substantial things, and the tortillas were very white and they brought them on plates covered with clean cloths; and they also brought him another kind of bread, which are like long loaves made and kneaded with another sort of substantial things, and pachol bread, as it is called in this land, which is like wafers; they also placed on the table three tubes, very painted and gilded, and inside they had liquid amber stirred with certain herbs called tobacco, and when he had finished eating, after they had danced and sung for him and cleared the table, he took the smoke from one of those tubes, very little, and with it he fell asleep.—

Let us now leave off speaking of the service of his table, and return to our account. I remember that at that time his chief steward was a great chieftain whom we named Tapia, and he kept account of all the revenues brought to Moctezuma, with his books made of their paper called amatl [paper], and he had a great house full of these books. Let us leave off speaking of the books and accounts, since that goes outside our account, and let us say how Moctezuma had two houses full of every kind of arms, and many of them rich, with gold and jewels, where there were round shields large and small, and some like clubs and others like two-handed swords, set in them blades of flint that cut much better than our swords, and other spears longer than ours, with a forearm’s length of blade, set in them many blades that, although they strike with them on a buckler or round shield, do not spring loose, and in the end cut like razors with which they shave heads; and he had very good bows and arrows, and two-piece staves and others of one piece, with their throwing-sticks, and many slings and hand-rounded stones, and certain shields that are of such workmanship that they can roll them up when they do not fight, so they do not hinder them, and at the time of fighting when they are needed they let them fall and remain covered from head to foot. He also had many quilted cotton armor richly worked on the outside with feathers of many colors, like devices and inventions, and they had others like helmets and wooden and bone casques, likewise very worked in feathers on the outside, and they had other arms of other fashions which, to avoid prolixity, I leave off telling, and his officials who always worked and busied themselves in it, and stewards who had charge of the arms. Let us leave this, and go to the house of birds; and since I must stop to count each kind and of what quality they were, I say that from royal eagles and other smaller eagles and many other kinds of large-bodied birds, down to very small birds painted in diverse colors, likewise where they make those rich featherworks they work from green feathers — and the birds of these feathers have bodies like the woodpeckers

we have in our Spain, called in this land quetzals, and other birds that have feathers of five colors, which are green and red and white and yellow and blue, I do not know what these are called — then parrots of other differentiated colors, he had so many that I do not remember their names; let us leave ducks of good plumage and others larger that he wished to be shown, and of all these birds they plucked the feathers in seasons when it was fitting, and they grew back again; and nearly all the birds I have spoken of they bred in that house, and at nesting time certain Indians and Indian women had charge of putting their eggs for them, and of looking after all the birds, and of cleaning their nests and feeding them, and this to each kind of bird what was its keep; and in that house I have spoken of there was a great pond of fresh water, and in it another kind of very tall long-legged birds, colored all over body and wings and tail; I do not know their name, but on the island of Cuba they called them ipiris, and others like them; and likewise in that pond there were many other kinds of birds that always stayed in the water — let us leave this, and go to another great house where they had many idols, and they said they were their fierce gods, and with them kinds of beasts: tigers, and lions of two sorts, some that are made like wolves, which in this land are called adives and foxes, and other small beasts, and all these carnivores were kept on meat, and most of them were bred in that house, and they fed them venison, hens, little dogs, and other things they hunted; and I still hear tell that bodies of Indians whom they sacrificed, and it is in this manner — that, as I have already heard tell, when they sacrificed some wretched Indian they sawed him with flint knives across the chest and, while he boiled, they took out his heart and blood and presented it to their idols, in whose name they made that sacrifice, and then they cut off his thighs and arms and head, and that they ate at feasts and banquets, and the head they hung from beams, and the body of the sacrificed one they did not approach to eat themselves, but gave it to those fierce animals; then they had more in that accursed house: many vipers and snakes, venomous, that carry in the tail one that sounds like rattles — these are the worst vipers of all — and they kept them in jars and in great pitchers, and in them much feather, and there they laid their eggs and bred their little vipers, and gave them to eat of the bodies of the Indians they sacrificed, and other flesh of dogs that they used to breed; and we even took for certain that when they drove us out of Mexico and killed more than eight hundred of our soldiers, those fierce beasts and snakes were kept many days on the bodies of the dead, as I shall tell in its time and season; and these snakes and beasts were offered to those fierce idols of theirs, so that they might stay in their company. Now let us speak of the infernal things: when the tigers and lions roared and the adives and foxes howled and the serpents hissed, it was grim to hear and seemed hell. Let us pass on and speak of the great officials he had of each craft practiced among them; let us begin with lapidaries and gold- and silversmiths of hollow work, at which in our Spain the great silversmiths must look; and of these he had so many and so fine in a town called Escapuzalco, a league from Mexico. Then workers of fine stones and chalchihuitl [greenstone], which are like emeralds, and many other great masters. Let us go on to the great officials of feather-mosaic, and painters and carvers very sublime, who for what we have now seen of the work they do we may judge what they then wrought — that there are now three Indians in the city of Mexico so fine in their craft of carvers and painters that they are called Marcos de Aquino, and Joan de la Cruz, and El Crespillo, who if they had been in the time of that ancient and famed Apelles, or of Michelangelo or Berruguete, who are of our times, would likewise have been numbered among them. Let us pass on and go to the Indian weavers or washerwomen who made him such a multitude of fine garments, with very great feather labors, which most commonly they brought him from certain towns and a province on the north coast near Veracruz, called Cotaxtla, very near San Juan de Ulúa, where we disembarked when we came with Cortés. And in the house of the same great Moctezuma, all the daughters of lords whom he kept as mistresses always wove very fine things, and many other daughters of Mexican citizens who lived as in a kind of retreat, wishing to seem nuns, also wove, and all in feathers. These nuns had their houses near the great cue [temple] of Huitzilopochtli, and out of devotion to him or to another female idol they said was their advocate for marriages, their parents put them into that way of life until they married, and from there they took them out to marry them. Let us pass on and speak of the great number the great Moctezuma had of dancers and performers, and others who carry a pole with their feet, and others who fly high when they dance, and others who seem like matachines, and these were to give him pleasure; I say he had a whole quarter of these who understood nothing else. Let us pass on and speak of the officials he had of stonemasons, and bricklayers, carpenters, who all busied themselves in the works of his houses. I also say that he had as many as he wished. Let us not forget the gardens of flowers and fragrant trees, and the many kinds he had of them, and the order and walks of them and his pools and ponds of fresh water, as the water enters on one side and goes out on another, and the baths he had within them, and the diversity of little birds bred in the trees, and what medicinal and useful herbs he had in them — it was a thing to see; and for all this, many gardeners, and everything worked in stone and very whitewashed, likewise arbors and walks and other retreats and apartments like summer houses, and also where they danced and sang; and there was so much to look at in these gardens, as in everything else, that we could not have our fill of seeing his great power; and so accordingly he had as many Indian masters of every craft practiced among them as he wished; and because I am weary of writing on this matter, and curious readers will be more so, I shall leave off telling and shall say how our Cortés went with many of our captains and soldiers to see the Tlatelolco, which is the great marketplace of Mexico, and we climbed up a high cue [temple] where their idols Tezcatlipoca and Huitzilopochtli stood; and this was the first time our captain went out to see the city, and what happened therein most of all.</english>
<spanish>CAPÍTULO XCI — De la manera y persona del gran Moctezuma, y de cuán grande señor era.

Era el gran Moctezuma de edad de hasta cuarenta años y de buena estatura y bien proporcionado, y delgado, y pocas carnes, y la color ni muy moreno, sino propia color y matiz de indio, y traía los cabellos no muy largos, sino cuanto le cubrían las orejas, y pocas barbas prietas y bien puestas y ralas, y el rostro algo largo y alegre y los ojos de buena manera, y mostraba en su persona, en el mirar, por un cabo amor, y cuando era menester gravedad, era muy pulido y limpio. Se bañaba cada día una vez a la tarde, cerca del avemaría.¹ Tenía muchas mujeres por amigas, hijas de señores, puesto que tenía dos grandes cacicas por sus legítimas mujeres, que cuando usaba con ellas era tan secretamente, que no lo alcanzaban a saber sino alguno de los que le servían. Era muy limpio de sodomías. Las mantas y ropas que se ponía un día, no se las ponía sino después de cuatro días. Tenía sobre doscientos principales de su guarda en otras salas junto a la suya, y estos no para que hablasen todos con él, sino cual y cual; y cuando le iban a hablar se habían de quitar las mantas ricas y ponerse otras de poca valía, mas habían de ser limpias, y habían de entrar descalzos y los ojos bajos puestos en tierra y no mirarle a la cara; y con tres reverencias que le hacían y le decían en ellas señor, mi señor, mi gran señor, primero que a él llegasen, y después que le daban relación a lo que iban, con pocas palabras les despachaba. No le volvían las espaldas al despedirse de él, sino la cara y ojos bajos en tierra hacia donde estaba, y no vueltas las espaldas, hasta que salían de la sala.

Y otra cosa vi, que cuando otros grandes señores venían de lejanas tierras a pleitos o negocios, cuando llegaban a los aposentos del gran Moctezuma, habían de venir descalzos y con pobres mantas, y no habían de entrar derecho en los palacios, sino rodear un poco por un lado de la puerta del palacio, que entrar de rota batida teníanlo por desacato. En el comer, le tenían sus cocineros sobre treinta maneras de guisados, hechos a su manera y usanza, y los tenían puestos en braseros de barro chicos debajo porque no se enfriasen, y de aquello que el gran Moctezuma había de comer guisaban más de trescientos platos, sin más de mil para la gente de guarda; y cuando había de comer, salía el Moctezuma algunas veces con sus principales y mayordomos, y le señalaban cuál guisado era mejor y de qué aves y cosas estaba guisado, y de lo que le decían de aquello había de comer; y cuando salía a lo ver, eran pocas veces, y como por pasatiempo. Oí decir que le solían guisar carnes de muchachos de poca edad, y como tenía tantas diversidades de guisados y de tantas cosas, no lo echábamos de ver si era de carne humana o de otras cosas, porque cotidianamente le guisaban gallinas, gallos de papada, faisanes, perdices de la tierra, codornices, patos mansos y bravos, venado, puerco de la tierra, pajaritos de caña y palomas, y liebres y conejos, y muchas maneras de aves y cosas que se crían en estas tierras, que son tantas que no las acabaré de nombrar tan presto, y así no miramos en ello; mas sé que ciertamente, desde que nuestro capitán le reprendía el sacrificio y comer de carne humana, que desde entonces mandó que no le guisasen tal manjar — dejemos de hablar en esto, y volvamos a la manera que tenía en su servicio, al

tiempo del comer || y es desta manera, que si hacía frío, teníanle hecha mucha lumbre de ascuas de una leña de cortezas de árboles que no hacían humo; el olor de las cortezas de que hacían aquellos fuegos, muy oloroso, y porque no le diesen más calor de lo que quería ponían delante una como tabla labrada con oro y otras figuras de ídolos, y él sentado en un asentadero bajo, rico y blando, y la mesa también baja, hecha de la misma manera de los sentaderos, y allí le ponían sus manteles de mantas blancas y unos pañuelos, algo largos, de lo mismo, y cuatro mujeres muy hermosas y limpias le daban agua a manos en unos como a manera de aguamaniles hondos que llaman xicales, ponían debajo para recoger el agua, otros a manera de platos, y le daban sus toallas; y otras dos mujeres le traían el pan de tortillas, y ya que comenzaba a comer echabanle delante una como puerta de madera muy pintada de oro porque no le viesen comer; y estaban apartadas las cuatro mujeres, y allí se le ponían a sus lados cuatro grandes señores viejos jóvenes y de edad en pie, con quien el Moctezuma de cuando en cuando platicaba y preguntaba cosas; y por mucho favor daba a cada uno de estos viejos un plato de lo que más le sabía, y decían que aquellos viejos eran sus debidos muy cercanos y consejeros y jueces de pleitos, y el plato y manjar que les daba el Moctezuma comían en pie y con mucho acato, y todo sin mirarle a la cara. Servíase con barro de Cholula, de uno colorado y otro prieto; mientras comía, ni por pensamiento habían de hacer alboroto ni hablar alto los de su guarda, que estaban en las salas cerca de la del Moctezuma. Traíanle fruta de todas cuantas había en la tierra, mas no comía sino muy poca de cuando en cuando. Traían en unas como a manera de copas de oro fino cierta bebida hecha del mismo cacao, decían que era para tener acceso con mujeres, y entonces no mirábamos en ello; mas lo que yo vi, que traían sobre cincuenta jarros grandes hechos de buen cacao, con su espuma, y de aquello bebía, y las mujeres le servían al beber con gran acato; y algunas veces al tiempo de comer, estaban unos indios corcobados, muy feos, porque

eran chicos de cuerpo y quebrados por medio los cuerpos. Entre ellos había chocarreros, y otros indios que debían de ser truhanes, que le decían gracias, y otros que saltitaban y bailaban, porque el Moctezuma era aficionado a placeres y cantares; y aquellos mandaba dar los relieves y jarros del cacao, y las mismas cuatro mujeres alzaban los manteles y le tornaban a dar aguamanos, y con mucho acato que le hacían hablaba el Moctezuma con aquellos cuatro principales viejos en cosas que le convenían, y se despedían de él con gran reverencia que le tenían, y él se quedaba reposando; y después que el gran Moctezuma había comido, luego comían todos los de su guarda y otros muchos de sus serviciales de casa, y me parece que sacaban sobre mil platos de aquellos manjares que dicho tengo, pues jarros de cacao con su espuma, como entre mexicanos se hace, más de dos mil, y fruta infinita; pues para sus mujeres y criadas y panaderas y cacahuateras, qué gran costa tenía — dejemos de hablar de la costa y comida de su casa, y digamos de los mayordomos y tesoreros y despensas y botellería, y de los que tenían cargo de las casas a donde tenían el maíz; digo, que había tanto que escribir, cada cosa por sí, que yo no sé por dónde comenzar, sino que estábamos admirados del gran concierto y abasto que en todo tenía; y más digo, que se me había olvidado, que es bien tornarlo a recitar, y es, que le servían al Moctezuma, estando a la mesa cuando comía, como dicho tengo, otras dos mujeres muy agraciadas, de traer tortillas amasadas con huevos y otras cosas sustanciosas, y eran muy blancas las tortillas y traíanlas en unos platos cubiertos con sus paños limpios; y también le traían otra manera de pan, que son como bollos largos hechos y amasados con otra manera de cosas sustanciales, y pan pachol que en esta tierra así se dice, que es a manera de unas obleas; también le ponían en la mesa tres cañutos, muy pintados y dorados, y dentro tenían líquido ámbar, revuelto con unas hierbas que se dice tabaco, y cuando acababa de comer, después que le habían bailado y cantado y alzado la mesa, tomaba el humo de uno de aquellos cañutos, muy poco, y con ello se adormecía.—

Dejemos ya de decir del servicio de su mesa, y volvamos a nuestra relación. Acuérdome que era en aquel tiempo su mayordomo mayor un gran cacique, que le pusimos por nombre Tapia, y tenía cuenta de todas las rentas que le traían al Moctezuma, con sus libros hechos de su papel que se dice amatl, y tenía destos libros una gran casa de ellos. Dejemos de hablar de los libros y cuentas, pues va fuera de nuestra relación, y digamos cómo tenía Moctezuma dos casas llenas de todo género de armas, y muchas de ellas ricas, con oro y pedrería, donde habían rodelas grandes y chicas, y unas como macanas y otras a manera de espadas de a dos manos, engastadas en ellas unas navajas de pedernal que cortan mucho mejor que nuestras espadas, y otras lanzas más largas que las nuestras, con una braza de cuchilla, engastadas en ellas muchas navajas que, aunque den con ellas en un broquel o rodela, no saltan, y cortan en fin como navajas con que se rapan con ellas las cabezas; y tenía muy buenos arcos y flechas, y varas de a dos gajos y otros de a uno, con sus tiraderas, y muchas hondas y piedras rollizas hechas a mano, y unos como paveses que son de arte que las pueden arrollar arriba cuando no pelean, porque no les estorbe, y al tiempo del pelear, cuando son menester, las dejan caer y quedan cubiertos sus cuerpos de arriba abajo. También tenía muchas armas de algodón acolchadas y ricamente labradas por de fuera de plumas de muchos colores, a manera de divisas e invenciones, y tenían otros como capacetes y cascos de madera y de hueso, también muy labrados de pluma por de fuera, y tenían otras armas de otras hechuras que, por escusar prolixidad, lo dejo de decir, y sus oficiales que siempre labraban y entendían en ello, y mayordomos que tenían cargo de las armas. Dejemos esto, y vamos a la casa de aves; y por fuerza he de detenerme en contar cada género de qué calidad eran; digo que desde águilas reales y otras águilas más chicas y otras muchas maneras de aves de grandes cuerpos, hasta pajaritos muy chicos pintados de diversos colores, también donde hacen aquellos ricos plumajes que labran de plumas verdes, y las aves destas plumas son el cuerpo de ellas a manera de las picazas

que hay en nuestra España, llamanse en esta tierra quetzales, y otros pájaros que tienen la pluma de cinco colores, que es verde y colorado y blanco y amarillo y azul — estos no sé cómo se llaman —; pues papagayos de otras diferenciadas colores, tenía tantos que no se me acuerda los nombres de ellos; dejemos patos de buena pluma y otros mayores que les quería parecer, y de todas estas aves les pelaban las plumas en tiempos que para ello era conveniente y tornaban a pelechar, y todas las más aves que dicho tengo criaban en aquella casa, y al tiempo del encobar tenían cargo de echarles sus huevos ciertos indios e indias que miraban por todas las aves y de limpiarles sus nidos y darles de comer, y esto a cada género de aves lo que era su mantenimiento; y en aquella casa que dicho tengo había un gran estanque de agua dulce, y tenía en él otra manera de aves muy altas de zancas y colorado todo el cuerpo y alas y cola; no sé el nombre de ellas, mas en la isla de Cuba las llamaban ipiris, y otras como ellas; y también en aquel estanque había otras muchas razas de aves que siempre estaban en el agua — dejemos esto, y vamos a otra gran casa donde tenían muchos ídolos, y decían que eran sus dioses bravos, y con ellos género de alimañas: de tigres y leones de dos maneras, unos que son de hechura de lobos, que en esta tierra se llaman adives y zorros, y otras alimañas chicas, y todas estas carniceras se mantenían con carne, y las más de ellas criaban en aquella casa, y las daban de comer venados, gallinas, perrillos y otras cosas que cazaban; y aún oy decir que cuerpos de indios de los que sacrificaban, y es desta manera, que ya me habrán oído decir que cuando sacrificaban algún triste indio, que le aserraban con unos navajones de pedernal por los pechos y, bullendo, le sacaban el corazón y sangre y lo presentaban a sus ídolos, en cuyo nombre hacían aquel sacrificio, y luego les cortaban los muslos y brazos y cabeza, y aquello comían en fiestas y banquetes, y la cabeza colgaban de unas vigas, y el cuerpo del sacrificado no llegaban a él para comerlo, sino dábanlo a aquellos bravos animales; pues más tenían en aquella maldita casa muchas víboras y culebras emponzoñadas que traen en la

cola uno que suena como cascabeles — estas son las peores víboras de todas —, y teníanlas en unas tinajas y en cantaros grandes, y en ellas mucha pluma, y allí ponían sus huevos y criaban sus víboras, y les daban a comer de los cuerpos de los indios que sacrificaban, y otras carnes de perros de los que ellos solían criar; y aún tuvimos por cierto que, cuando nos echaron de México y nos mataron sobre ochocientos de nuestros soldados, que de los muertos mantuvieron muchos días aquellas fieras alimañas y culebras, según diré en su tiempo y sazón; y a estas culebras y alimañas tenían ofrecidos aquellos sus ídolos bravos, para que estuviesen en su compañía. Digamos ahora las cosas infernales: cuando bramaban los tigres y leones y aullaban los adives y zorros y silbaban las sierpes, era grima oírlo y parecía infierno. Pasemos adelante y digamos de los grandes oficiales que tenía de cada oficio que entre ellos se usaban; comencemos por lapidarios y plateros de oro y plata, y todo vaciadizo, que en nuestra España los grandes plateros tienen que mirar en ello, y destos tenía tantos y tan primos en un pueblo que se dice Escapuzalco, una legua de México. Pues labrar piedras finas y chalchihuis, que son como esmeraldas, otros muchos grandes maestros. Vamos adelante a los grandes oficiales de asentar de pluma, y pintores y entalladores muy sublimados, que por lo que ahora hemos visto la obra que hacen, ternemos consideración en lo que entonces labraban — que tres indios hay ahora en la ciudad de México tan primísimos en su oficio de entalladores y pintores, que se dicen Marcos de Aquino, y Joan de la Cruz, y el Crespillo, que si fueran en el tiempo de aquel antiguo y afamado Apeles, o de Miguel Ángel o Berruguete, que son de nuestros tiempos, también les pusieran en el número de ellos. Pasemos adelante y vamos a las indias tejedoras o lavanderas que le hacían tanta multitud de ropa fina, con muy grandes labores de plumas, de donde más cotidianamente le traían era de unos pueblos y provincia que está en la costa del norte, cerca de la Veracruz, que se decían Cotaxtla, muy cerca de San Juan de Ulúa, donde desembarcamos cuando venimos con Cortés. Y en su casa del mismo gran Moctezuma, todas las hijas de señores que tenía por amigas siempre tejían cosas muy primas, y otras muchas hijas de vecinos mexicanos que estaban como a manera de recogimiento, que querían parecer monjas, también tejían, y todo de pluma. Estas monjas tenían sus casas cerca del gran cue del Huitzilopochtli, y por devoción suya o de otro ídolo de mujer que decían que era su abogada para casamientos, las metían sus padres en aquella religión hasta que se casaban, y de allí las sacaban para casarlas. Pasemos adelante y digamos de la gran cantidad que tenía el gran Moctezuma de bailadores y danzadores, y otros que traen un palo con los pies, y de otros que vuelan cuando bailan por alto, y de otros que parecen como matachines, y estos eran para darle placer; digo que tenía un barrio de estos que no entendían en otra cosa. Pasemos adelante y digamos de los oficiales que tenía de canteros y albañiles y carpinteros, que todos entendían en las obras de sus casas. También digo que tenía tantas cuantas quería. No olvidemos las huertas de flores y árboles olorosos, y de los muchos géneros que de ellos tenía, y el concierto y paseaderos de ellas y de sus albercas y estanques de agua dulce, como viene el agua por un cabo y va por otro, y de los baños que dentro tenía, y de la diversidad de pajaritos chicos que en los árboles criaban, y de qué hierbas medicinales y de provecho que en ellas tenía — era cosa de ver; y para todo esto, muchos hortelanos, y todo labrado de cantería y muy encalado, así vanos como paseaderos y otros retretes y apartamentos como cenaderos, y también adonde bailaban y cantaban; y había tanto que mirar en esto de las huertas, como en todo lo demás, que no nos hartábamos de ver su gran poder; y así, por el consiguiente, tenía cuantos oficios entre ellos se usaban de todos gran cantidad de indios maestros de ellos; y porque yo estoy harto de escribir sobre esta materia, y más lo estarán los curiosos lectores, lo dejaré de decir, y diré cómo fue nuestro Cortés con muchos de nuestros capitanes y soldados a ver el Tlatelolco, que es la gran plaza de México, y subimos en alto cue, donde estaban sus ídolos Tezcatlipoca y Huitzilopochtli; y esta fue la primera vez que nuestro capitán salió a ver la ciudad, y lo que en ello más pasó.</spanish>
<flags>voice: Bernal Díaz del Castillo — blunt, garrulous old soldier, plain and concrete, quietly aggrieved, proud eyewitness (held). ⚠ NO REFERENCE —. Colonial spellings modernized throughout Spanish (monteçuma→Moctezuma, ç→c/z, avn→aún, prinçipales, rreçebir, esforçados, antecesores, xpianos→cristianos, mds→mercedes, aperçebido, pareçer, çesaron, yndias→indias, yerva→hierba, cavallos→caballos, hedad→edad, proporçionado→proporcionado, çenzeño→delgado, guisados, negoçios→negocios, etc.). Folio headers, page numbers, signature line, and "Digitized by Google" omitted. Footnote ¹ (testado: "cerca del avemaria") incorporated into main text at first bathing mention. OCR/damage repaired and flagged: "ṕaticando"→platicando; "ın chicos"/"Ʈntre"/".ıtavan"→"eran chicos"/"Entre ellos"/"saltitaban"; "quescrevir"→"que escribir"; "Encoclar"→encobar (nesting); "cacaguaterās"→cacahuateras; "liquidambar"→líquido ámbar; "debidos muy cercanos" rendered as close subjects/vassals; "conçierto"→order (not archaic "concert"); "tuteululco"→Tlatelolco [marketplace]; tezcatepuca/vichilobos→Tezcatlipoca/Huitzilopochtli; Escapuzalco, Cotaxtla, amatl [paper], xicales [gourds], cue [temple] glossed once in English. Hearsay layers (boy-meat cooking, human sacrifice at feasts, cacao as aphrodisiac, beasts fed on sacrificed bodies and later on dead Spaniards) kept as reported speech/hearsay, not Bernal Díaz's independent claim. Chapter continues across page break with "||" as in manuscript. Long descriptive inventory passage — interpretive only where bird/species names are uncertain ("estos no sé cómo se llaman"; ipiris from Cuba usage).</flags>

=== CAPITULO XCII—como nro capitan salio — Section 93 ===
<english>CHAPTER XCII — how our captain went out to see the city of Mexico and Tlatelolco, which is the main marketplace, and the great temple [cue] of their Huitzilopochtli, and what else happened.

Four days had already passed since we were in Mexico, and neither the captain nor any of us left our quarters, except to the houses and gardens. Cortés told us it would be well to go to the main marketplace and see the great temple of their Huitzilopochtli, and that he wished to send word to the great Moctezuma to take it well, and for that he sent as messenger Jerónimo de Aguilar and doña Marina, and with them a pageboy of our captain who already understood something of the language they called Orteguilla. And when Moctezuma learned of it, he sent to say that we should go in very good hour, and on the other hand he feared we might do some dishonor to his idols, and he resolved to go in person, with many of his chiefs, and in his rich litter he came out from his palaces as far as halfway along the road, beside certain temples, and he got down from the litter because he held it a great dishonor to his idols to go all the way to his house and temple in that manner; and great chiefs carried him by the arms, and lords of vassals went before him, and they carried before him two staffs like scepters raised on high, which was the sign that the great Moctezuma was going there. And when he went in the litter he carried a little rod half of gold and half of wood, raised up, like a rod of justice; and so he went, and went up into his great temple [cue] accompanied by many priests, and began to burn incense and perform other ceremonies to Huitzilopochtli. Let us leave Moctezuma, who had already gone ahead, as I have said, and return to Cortés and our captains and soldiers, who, as we always had for custom, night and day, kept ourselves armed; and so Moctezuma saw us when we went to see him, and he did not take it for anything new. I say this because on horseback our captain went, with all the others who had horses, and the greater part of our soldiers, very well prepared, and we went to Tlatelolco [marketplace], and many caciques whom Moctezuma sent went with us to accompany us. And when we reached the great marketplace called Tlatelolco, as we had never seen the like, we stood astonished at the multitude of people and goods that were in it, and at the great order and regulation they had in everything; and the chiefs who went with us were showing us each kind of merchandise, which stood apart by itself, each with its place set and marked. Let us begin with the merchants of gold and silver, and rich stones and feathers and mantles, and worked things, and other merchandise of Indian slaves — I say they brought as many of them to sell in that great marketplace as the Portuguese bring blacks from Guinea, and they brought them tied on long poles with collars on their necks so they would not run away, and others they left loose. Then there were other merchants who sold coarser cloth and cotton, and things of spun thread, and cacao sellers who sold cacao; and in this manner stood every kind of merchandise there is in all New Spain, set out in their order in the manner there is in my homeland, which is Medina del Campo, where the fairs are held — in each street its merchandise apart by itself — and so it was in this great marketplace. And those who sold mantles of nequen and ropes and cotaras, which are the shoes they wear and make from the same tree, and very sweet roots, cooked, and other sweets they draw from the same tree — all that stood in one part of the marketplace in its marked place. And skins of tigers and lions, and of otters and badgers and deer, and of other beasts, and raccoons and wildcats — some of them dressed and others undressed — stood in another part, and other kinds of things and merchandise. Let us pass on and speak of those who sold beans and chia and other legumes and herbs, in another part; let us go to those who sold hens, wattled cocks, rabbits, hares, deer, ducks, little dogs, and other things of that sort, in their part of the marketplace. Let us speak of the fruit sellers, and of those who sold cooked things, pozole, mulberries, and badly cooked food, likewise in their part; then every kind of pottery, made in a thousand ways, from great jars and little pitchers that stood apart by themselves; and likewise those who sold honey and molasses and other sweets they made like nougats; then those who sold wood, planks, cradles, beams, blocks and benches, each apart. Let us go to those who sold firewood, ocote pine, and other things of that sort. What more do they want me to say? Speaking with Acato, they also sold many canoes full of human excrement, which they had in the estuaries near the marketplace, and this was for making salt or for tanning hides, which they say could not be done well without it. Well do I understand that some lords may laugh at this; yet I say it is so, and I say further that they had for custom that along all the roads they had shelters made of cane or straw or grass, so that those who passed would not see them; there they would go in if they had need to purge their bowels, so as not to lose that filth. Why do I spend so many words on what was sold in that great marketplace? It is so as not to finish so soon telling in detail all the things; but paper, which in this land they call amatl [paper], and certain tubes of scents with liquid amber full of tobacco, and other yellow unguents and things of that sort, were sold apart; and they sold much cochineal beneath the arcades that stood in that great marketplace. There were many herb shops and merchandise of another sort, and they had there their houses where three judges judged, and others like constables and executors who inspected the goods. I had forgotten the salt, and those who made flint knives and how they drew them from the same stone; then fish sellers and others who sold certain little loaves they make from a kind of scum that forms on that great lagoon, which they cook, and make loaves of it that have a taste somewhat like cheese; and they sold axes of brass and copper and tin, and gourds [xicales] and certain very painted wooden jars. I would already have wished to have finished telling all the things that were sold there, for they were so many and of such variety that to finish seeing and inquiring into them — as the great marketplace was full of so many people and entirely ringed with arcades — in two days one could not have seen everything. And we went to the great temple [cue], and as we were already near its great courtyards, and before leaving the same marketplace, there stood many other merchants who, according to what they said, were of those who bring gold to sell in grains as they take it from the mines, with the gold put inside certain thin tubes like those of native geese, and likewise white so the gold would show through on the outside; and by the length and thickness of the tubes they kept account among themselves of how many mantles, or how many xiquipiles of cacao it was worth, or how many slaves or whatever other thing they traded it for. And so we left the great marketplace without seeing more of it, and reached the great courtyards and enclosures where the great temple [cue] stands; and before reaching it there was a great circuit of courtyards that seems to me were larger than the marketplace there is in Salamanca, with two enclosures of mortar all around, and the same courtyard and site all paved with great stones, white slabs, very smooth; and where there were none of those stones it was whitewashed and polished, and everything very clean, so that one would not find a straw nor dust in the whole of it. And when we reached near the great temple [cue], before we climbed any of its steps, the great Moctezuma sent from above, where he was performing sacrifices, six priests and two chiefs to accompany our captain; and as he climbed the steps, which were one hundred and fourteen, they went to take him by the arms to help him climb, believing he would grow tired, as they helped their lord Moctezuma; and Cortés would not allow them to come near him. And when we climbed to the top of the great temple [cue], to a little plaza that was made above, where they had a space like scaffolding, and on it set certain great stones where they placed the wretched Indians to sacrifice — there was a great bulk like a dragon, and other evil figures, and much blood shed that day — and as soon as we arrived Moctezuma came out of a temple where his accursed idols were, which was at the top of the great temple [cue], and two priests came with him; and with much reverence that they showed to Cortés and to all of us, he said: “You must be weary, lord Malinche, from climbing this our great temple.” And Cortés said to him, through our interpreters who went with us, that neither he nor we grew weary at anything; and then he took him by the hand and told him to look at his great city, and all the other cities that were within the water, and many other towns around the same lagoon on dry land; and that if he had not seen his great marketplace very well, from there he could see it much better. And so we stood looking, because from that great and accursed temple one was so high that everything lay well beneath one's view; and from there we saw the three causeways that enter Mexico — that of Iztapalapa, by which we had entered four days before, and that of Tacuba, by which afterward we went out fleeing on the night of our great rout when Cuitláhuac, newly made lord, drove us from the city, as we shall tell ahead — and that of Tepeaquilla; and we saw the fresh water that came from Chapultepec with which the city was supplied; and on those three causeways the bridges they had built at intervals, by which the water of the lagoon entered and left from one side to the other; and we saw on that great lagoon such a multitude of canoes, some coming with provisions and others returning with loads and merchandise; and we saw that from house to house in that great city and in all the other cities that were settled in the water one passed only by certain drawbridges of wood they had made, or in canoes; and we saw in those cities temples [cues] and shrines like towers and fortresses, all whitewashed, which was a thing of wonder; and the houses with flat roofs, and on the causeways other little towers and shrines that were like fortresses. And after we had well looked at and considered everything we had seen, we turned again to look at the great marketplace and the multitude of people in it, some buying and others selling, so that only the murmur and hum of the voices and words there sounded from more than a league away. And among us were soldiers who had been in many parts of the world, and in Constantinople and all Italy and Rome, and they said they had never seen a marketplace so well laid out, and with such order, and so large, and full of so many people. Let us leave this, and return to our captain, who said to fray Bartolomé de Olmedo — already mentioned by me at other times, who was there — “It seems to me, lord father, that it would be well to sound Moctezuma on letting us build our church here.” And the father said it would be well if it should avail, but it seemed to him it was not a fitting thing to speak of at such a time, for he did not see Moctezuma in such a mood that he would grant such a thing. And then our Cortés said to Moctezuma, through doña Marina the interpreter: “You are a very great lord, and much more deserving than you are; we have been glad to see your cities. What I ask of you by your mercy is that, since we are here in this your temple, you show us your gods and teules.” And Moctezuma said he would first speak with his great priests; and when he had spoken with them, he said we should enter a little tower and chamber like a hall, where there were two altars, with very rich paneling on the ceiling, and on each altar stood two bulks like giants of very tall bodies and very fat; and the first that stood on the right hand they said was that of Huitzilopochtli, their god of war, and he had the face and countenance very broad, and the eyes misshapen and dreadful; over the whole body so much precious stone, and gold and pearls and seed pearls stuck on with paste they make in this land from certain roots, that the whole body and head were full of it; and girding the body were certain things like great serpents made of gold and precious stones; and in one hand he held a bow, and in the other certain arrows. And another small idol that stood there they said was his page; he held a short lance and a very rich shield of gold and precious stones; and around the neck of Huitzilopochtli were set certain faces of Indians and others like hearts of the same Indians, some of gold and some of silver with much blue precious stone; and there were certain braziers with incense, which is their copal, and with three hearts of Indians that had been sacrificed that day, which were burning; and with the smoke and copal they had made that sacrifice to him. And all the walls of that shrine were so coated and black with crusts of blood, and likewise the floor, that everything stank very badly. Then we saw on the other side, on the left hand, the other great bulk on the altar of Huitzilopochtli; and he had a face like a bear and certain eyes that gleamed, made of their mirrors called tezcatl; and the body with rich stones stuck on in the manner of the other, their Huitzilopochtli, for they said both were brothers; and this Tezcatlipoca was the god of the infernos, and had charge of the souls of the Mexicans; and he had girded on the body certain figures like little devils, and their tails like serpents; and on the walls there were so many crusts of blood and the floor all bathed in it that in the slaughterhouses of Castile there was not so much stench; and there they had presented to him five hearts sacrificed that day. And at the very top of the whole temple [cue] stood another concavity very richly worked in wood, and in it another bulk like half man and half lizard, all full of rich stones and half mantled. They said its body was full of all the seeds there were in all the land, and they said it was the god of the fields and fruits — I do not remember the name — and everything was full of blood, walls as well as altar; and the stench was so great that we could not wait for the hour to get out. And there they had a drum very great beyond measure, which when they struck it the sound was so mournful and of such a kind, they say, as the instrument of the infernos, and more than two leagues from there it was heard. They said the skins of that drum were of very great serpents. And in that little plaza they had so many very devilish things to see — of horns and little trumpets and large knives, and many hearts of Indians they had burned with which they had censed those idols, and everything clotted with blood — they had so much of it that I give it to the curse; and as everything stank like a butcher shop, we could not wait for the hour to get ourselves away from so evil a stench and worse sight. And our captain said to Moctezuma, through our interpreter, as if half laughing: “Lord Moctezuma, I do not know how so great a lord and wise man as you are has not gathered in his understanding that these your idols are not gods but evil things that are called devils; and so that you may know it, and all your priests may see it clearly, do me a mercy: be pleased that on the top of this tower we set up a cross, and in one part of these shrines where your Huitzilopochtli and Tezcatlipoca stand we shall make a chamber where we place an image of our Lady” — whose image Moctezuma had already seen — “and you will see the fear they have of it, those idols that have you deceived.” And Moctezuma answered half angry, and two priests who were with him showed evil signs, and said: “Lord Malinche, if I had believed you would say such dishonor as you have said, I would not have shown you my gods. We hold these for very good, and they give us health and waters and good harvests and temporal goods and victories as many as we wish, and we are bound to adore them and sacrifice to them. What I beg of you is that no other words be spoken to their dishonor.” And when our captain heard that, so altered, he replied no more on the matter; and with a cheerful face he said: “Now it is time that you and we take our leave.” And Moctezuma answered that it was well, and that because he had to pray and perform a certain sacrifice in recompense for the great tatacul — which means sin — he had committed in letting us climb his great temple [cue] and being the cause of his letting us see his gods, and for the dishonor we did them in speaking ill of them, before he left he had to pray and adore; and Cortés told him: “Since it is so, forgive, lord.” And then we came down the steps below; and as they were one hundred and fourteen, some of our soldiers who were ill with buboes or humors felt pain in their thighs from coming down. I shall leave off speaking of his shrine and tell what seems to me of the circuit and manner it had; and if I do not tell it as naturally as it was, let them not marvel, for at that time I had another purpose in mind than to attend to what we carried in hand, which is the military and what my captain commanded me, and not to make relations. Let us return to our subject: it seems to me the circuit of the great temple [cue] would be six great solares, as they give them in this land, and from below to above, where there was a little tower and his idols stood, it narrows; and in the middle of the high temple [cue] up to the very top of it go five concavities like parapets, open and without breastworks. And because there are many temples [cues] painted on tapestries of conquerors, and on one I have, anyone who has seen them may infer the manner they had on the outside; but what I saw and understood, and of which there was fame in those times, is that they founded that great temple [cue] in the foundation of which all the inhabitants of that great city offered gold and silver and seed pearls and rich stones, and that they bathed it with much blood of Indians they sacrificed, taken in the wars, and with every kind and diversity of seeds there was in all the land, so that their idols would give them victories and riches and many fruits. Now some very curious readers will say: how could we come to know that in the foundation of that great temple [cue] they cast gold and silver and chalchihuitl stones and seeds and sprinkled them with human blood of Indians they sacrificed, there being more than a thousand years since it was built and made? To this I give for answer that after we won that fortress and great city, and the solares were divided, we at once proposed that in that great temple [cue] we should build the church of our patron and guide lord Santiago; and a great part of the solar of the high temple [cue] fell for the solar of the holy church of that temple [cue] of Huitzilopochtli; and when they opened the foundations to make them more firm, they found much gold and silver and chalchihuites and pearls and seed pearls and other stones; and likewise a inhabitant of Mexico to whom another part of the same solar fell found the same; and the officials of his Majesty's treasury demanded it as royal right due to him, and over it there was a lawsuit, and I do not remember what happened; but they informed themselves from the caciques and chiefs of Mexico and Guatemuz, who was then alive, and they said it was true that all the inhabitants of Mexico of that time cast into the foundations those jewels and all the rest, and that thus they held it in memory in their books and paintings of ancient things; and for this cause that wealth remained for the work of the holy church of St. Santiago. Let us leave this and speak of the great and sumptuous courtyards that stood before Huitzilopochtli, where lord Santiago now is, which is called Tlatelolco because it was so formerly called.
