=== CAPITULO XCV. de la prision del gran — Section 96 ===
<english>CHAPTER XCV. On the imprisonment of the great Montezuma, and what was done concerning it.

As we had agreed the day before to seize Montezuma, we spent the whole night in prayer, begging God that it might turn out in such a way as to redound to His holy service; and the next morning it was agreed how it was to be done. Cortés took with him five captains — Pedro de Alvarado and Gonzalo de Sandoval, Juan Velázquez de León and Francisco de Lugo, and Alonso de Ávila — and me, and our interpreters Doña Marina and Aguilar; and he ordered all of us to be very ready, and those on horseback saddled and bridled. As for arms, there is no need for me to set it down here from memory, because day and night we were always armed and shod in our alpargatas, which at that season was our footwear; and when we used to go speak with Montezuma he always saw us armed in that manner. I say this because, although Cortés went with all of us who accompanied him fully armed to seize him, Montezuma did not take it for anything new, nor was he disturbed by it. Once we were all ready, our captain sent word to let him know that he was coming to his palace — for that was his custom, so that he would not be alarmed seeing him come suddenly — and Montezuma understood well enough that he was coming angry over the Almería affair and feared him; he ordered that he come with all good speed. When Cortés entered, after paying his customary respects, he said through our interpreters, “Lord Montezuma, I am greatly astonished that you, being so valorous a prince and having declared yourself our friend, would order your captains whom you had on the coast near Tuxpan to take up arms against my Spaniards, and have the boldness to plunder the towns that stand under the guard and protection of our king and lord, and demand Indians, men and women, for sacrifice, and kill a Spaniard, my brother, and a horse.” He did not wish to tell him of the captain and the six soldiers who died as soon as they reached Veracruz, because Montezuma never came to know of it, nor did the Indian captains who waged war on them know either. Cortés told him further that, holding him as so close a friend, he had ordered his captains to serve and favor him in everything possible, “and you, on the contrary, have done this to us; and likewise in the matter of Cholula your captains had a great host of warriors, ordered by your command to kill us — I passed it over then because of how much regard I have for you; and likewise now your vassals and captains have grown shameless and hold secret councils that you wish to order us killed. For these reasons I would not wish to begin war nor destroy this city; it is fitting that, to excuse yourself from everything, you go at once, quietly and without any uproar, with us to our quarters, where you will be served and looked after very well as in your own house; and if you make uproar or cry out, you will at once be killed by these my captains, whom I do not bring for any other purpose.” When Montezuma heard this, he stood terrified and beside himself, and answered that he had never given such an order that they take up arms against us, and that he would at once send to summon his captains and the truth would be known and he would punish them; and then, in that very moment, he took from his arm and wrist the seal and sign of Vichilobos — for that was when he ordered some grave and weighty matter to be carried out, and it was at once fulfilled. As for going as prisoner and leaving his palaces against his will, there was no person worth his while to order him such a thing, and it was not his will to leave. Cortés replied with very good reasons, and Montezuma answered him with even better ones that he was not to leave his houses; and so they spent more than half an hour in these talks. When Juan Velázquez de León and the other captains saw that he was lingering with him and could not wait to have him taken from his houses and held prisoner, they spoke to Cortés somewhat heatedly and said, “Either you do it now, with all these words, or we carry him off prisoner, or we strike off his head.” For that reason Cortés told him again that if he cried out or made uproar they would kill him, because it was better that this time we secure our lives or lose them. As Juan Velázquez said it in a somewhat loud and frightening voice — for such was his manner of speaking — and Montezuma saw our captains angry, he asked Doña Marina what they were saying with those loud words; and as Doña Marina was very shrewd, she told him, “Lord Montezuma, what I advise you is to go at once with them to their quarters, without any noise, for I know they will do you great honor as the great lord you are, and otherwise you will remain here dead.” Then Montezuma said to Cortés, “Lord Malinche, since you wish it to be so, I have one son and two legitimate daughters — take them as hostages, and do not put this affront on me, for my lords will say, if they saw me carried off prisoner.” Cortés again said that his person had to go with them and it could not be otherwise; and in the end, after many more arguments that passed between them, he said he would go willingly. Then Cortés and our captains made him many courtesies and told him they begged him on their souls not to be angry, and that he should tell his captains and his guard that he went of his own will, because he had had consultation with his idol Vichilobos and with the priests who served him, that for his health and to preserve his life it was fitting to stay with us. Then they brought his rich litter, in which he was accustomed to go out, with all his captains who accompanied him, and he went to our quarters, where we placed guards and watches on him; and all the services and pleasures we could show him — Cortés as well as all of us — we showed him so many, and no fetters were put on him. Then all the greatest Mexican lords and his nephews came to see him, to speak with him and learn the cause of his imprisonment and whether he ordered that war be given us; and Montezuma answered them that he was glad to stay some days there with us of his own free will and not by force, and that when he wished something he would tell them, and that they should not stir themselves up nor the city, nor take grief from it, because what has happened — his staying there — his Vichilobos holds for good, and certain priests who know how to speak with his idol have told him so concerning it. And in this manner, as I have said, was the imprisonment of the great Montezuma; and there where he was held he had his service, and women, and baths in which he bathed, and twenty great lords and counselors and captains were always in his company at the curtain; and he made himself accustomed to being prisoner without showing distress in it. Ambassadors from distant lands came there with lawsuits, and brought him their tribute, and he dispatched matters of importance. I remember that when great caciques from distant lands came before him over boundaries, or towns, or other matters of that sort, however great a lord he might be, he removed his rich mantles and put on others of maguey fiber, of little value, and had to come barefoot; and when he reached the quarters he did not enter straight in, but by one side of them; and when he appeared before the great Montezuma, eyes down, on the ground, and before they reached him they made him three reverences and said, “Lord, my lord, and my great lord”; then they brought him the lawsuit or dispute for which they had come, painted and drawn on certain cloths and mantas of maguey fiber, and with very thin, polished little sticks they pointed out the cause of the lawsuit; and there beside Montezuma stood two old men, great caciques; and once they had well understood the lawsuit, those judges told Montezuma in few words the justice of the case, and he dispatched them, and ordered who was to take the lands or towns; and without further reply the litigants left, without turning their backs, and with the three reverences they went out as far as the hall; and once they were outside Montezuma’s presence they put on other rich mantles and walked about Mexico. I shall leave off for now speaking of this imprisonment, and let us tell how the messengers whom Montezuma sent with his sign and seal to summon his captains who killed our soldiers came before the prisoner; and what he spoke with them I do not know, only that he sent them to Cortés so that he might do justice on them. Once their confession was taken, without Montezuma present, they confessed to be true what I have already said above, and that their lord had commanded them to wage war and collect tribute, and that if any teules [idols, gods] were in their defense, they should also wage war on them or kill them. Once Cortés had seen this confession, he sent to let Montezuma know how they condemned him in that matter; and he excused himself as best he could, and our captain sent to tell him that he believed him thus — that although he deserved punishment according to what our king commands, that the person who orders others killed, whether without guilt or with guilt, should die for it, yet that he loves him so much and wishes him every good that, even if Montezuma had that guilt, Cortés would rather pay for it with his own person than see it fall upon Montezuma. And with all this that he sent to tell him, Montezuma remained fearful; and without spending more words, Cortés sentenced those captains to death, and that they be burned before Montezuma’s palaces; and so the sentence was at once carried out. And so that there might be no hindrance, while they were being burned he ordered shackles put on Montezuma himself; and once they were put on him he made uproar, and if before he was fearful, then he was much more so. After they were burned, our Cortés went with five of our captains to his quarters, and himself removed the shackles from him, and spoke such words to him, so affectionate, that his anger passed from him at once — because our Cortés told him that he not only held him as a brother but much more, and that as he is lord and king of so many towns and provinces, if he could, in time he would make him lord of more lands than he had not been able to conquer and that did not obey him, and that if he wished to go to his palaces he gave him license for it; and as Cortés was saying it to him through our interpreters, it seemed tears sprang from Montezuma’s eyes. He answered with great courtesy that he held it in his soul, but he understood well that all of Cortés’s words were words, and that for the present it was fitting to stay there prisoner, because perhaps, as his lords are many and his nephews and relatives come each day to tell him it would be well to give us war and take him from prison, once they saw him outside they would draw him to it, and he did not wish to see rebellions in his city; and that if he did not do their will they would perhaps wish to raise up another lord, and he took those thoughts from them by telling them his god Vichilobos had sent him word to be prisoner. And what is most certain, Cortés had told Aguilar to tell him secretly that although Malinche might order him to leave the prison, the rest of our captains and soldiers would not wish it. When Montezuma heard that, Cortés threw his arms around him and embraced him and said, “Not in vain, Lord Montezuma — I love you as much as myself.” Then Montezuma asked Cortés for a Spanish page who served him and already knew the language, who was called Orteguilla; and he was very useful both for Montezuma and for us, because through that page Montezuma inquired and learned many things about Castile, and we about what his captains told him; and truly the page was so good a servant that Montezuma loved him greatly. Let us leave off speaking of how Montezuma was already somewhat content with the great flattery, services, and conversation he had with all of us; because whenever we passed before him, even if it were Cortés, we removed our helmets or morions — for we were always armed — and he showed us great courtesy and honored us all. And let us give the names of those captains of Montezuma who were burned by justice: the chief was called Quetzalpopoca, and of the others one was called Coate and the other Quiavit; the other I do not remember the name — which matters little in knowing their names. And let us say that as this punishment became known throughout all the provinces of New Spain, they were afraid, and the towns of the coast where they killed our soldiers returned to serve very well the settlers who remained in Veracruz. And let the curious who read this consider such great deeds that we then did — putting the ships athwart, the other daring to enter so strong a city, having so many warnings that there they were to kill us once they had us inside, the other having such boldness as to dare seize the great Montezuma, who was king of that land, inside his great city and in his very palaces, with so great a number of warriors of his guard, and the other daring to burn his captains before his palaces and put shackles on him while the justice was being done. Many times now that I am old I stop to consider the heroic deeds we passed through in that time, that it seems to me I see them present; and I say that our deeds — we were not doing them ourselves, but they all came directed by God; for what men have there been in the world who would dare enter with four hundred soldiers — and we had not even reached them yet — into so strong a city as Mexico, which is greater than Venice, being apart from our Castile by more than fifteen hundred leagues, and seize so great a lord and do justice on his captains before him? For there is much to weigh in it, and not so plainly as I tell it. I shall pass on and tell how Cortés at once dispatched another captain to remain in Veracruz as Juan de Escalante was, whom they killed.</english>
<spanish>CAPÍTULO XCV. De la prisión del gran Montezuma, y lo que sobre ello se hizo.

Como teníamos acordado el día antes de prender al Montezuma, toda la noche estuvimos en oración, rogando a Dios que fuese de tal manera que redundase para su santo servicio; y otro día de mañana fue acordado de la manera que había de ser. Llevó consigo Cortés cinco capitanes, que fueron Pedro de Alvarado y Gonzalo de Sandoval, Juan Velázquez de León y Francisco de Lugo, y Alonso de Ávila, y a mí, y con nuestras lenguas doña Marina y Aguilar; y todos nosotros mandó que estuviésemos muy a punto, y los de a caballo ensillados y enfrenados. En lo de las armas no había necesidad de ponerlo yo aquí por memoria, porque siempre de día y de noche estábamos armados y calzados nuestros alpargates, que en aquella sazón era nuestro calzado; y cuando solíamos ir a hablar al Montezuma siempre nos veía armados de aquella manera. Esto digo, puesto que Cortés, con los que con él íbamos con todas sus armas para prenderle, no lo tenía el Montezuma por cosa nueva, ni se alteraba dello. Ya puestos a punto todos, envióle nuestro capitán a hacerle saber cómo iba a su palacio, porque así lo tenía por costumbre y no se alterase viéndolo ir de sobresalto; y el Montezuma bien entendió poco más o menos que iba enojado por lo de Almería y le temía, y mandó que fuese mucho en buen hora. Y como entró Cortés, después de haberle hecho sus acatos acostumbrados, le dijo, con nuestras lenguas: «Señor Montezuma, muy maravillado de vos estoy, que siendo tan valeroso príncipe y haberse dado por nuestro amigo, mandar a vuestros capitanes que teníades en la costa, cerca de Tuxpan, que tomasen armas contra mis españoles, y tener atrevimiento de robar los pueblos que están en guarda y amparo de nuestro rey y señor, y demandarles indios e indias para sacrificar, y matar un español, hermano mío, y un caballo». No le quiso decir del capitán ni de los seis soldados que murieron luego que llegaron a la Villa Rica, porque el Montezuma no lo alcanzó a saber, ni tampoco lo supieron los indios capitanes que les dieron la guerra. Más le dijo Cortés que, teniéndolo por tan su amigo, mandó a mis capitanes que en todo lo que posible fuese os sirviesen y favoreciesen, «y vos, por el contrario, nos lo habéis hecho; y así mismo en lo de Cholula tuvieron vuestros capitanes con gran copia de guerreros, ordenado por vuestro mandado que nos matasen — lo disimulé entonces por lo mucho que os quiero; y así mismo ahora vuestros vasallos y capitanes se han desvergonzado y tienen pláticas secretas de que nos queréis mandar matar. Por estas causas no querría encomenzar guerra ni destruir esta ciudad; conviene que, para todo excusar, luego, callando y sin hacer ningún alboroto, se vaya con nosotros a nuestro aposento, donde seréis servido y mirado muy bien como en vuestra propia casa; y si alboroto o voces daba, que luego sería muerto de estos mis capitanes, que no los traigo para otro efecto». Y cuando esto oyó el Montezuma, estuvo muy espantado y sin sentido, y respondió que nunca tal mandó que tomasen armas contra nosotros, y que enviaría luego a llamar sus capitanes y se sabría la verdad y los castigaría; y luego, en aquel instante, quitó de su brazo y muñeca el sello y señal de Vichilobos — que aquello era cuando mandaba alguna cosa grave y de peso para que se cumpliese, y luego se cumplía. Y en lo de ir preso y salir de sus palacios contra su voluntad, que no era persona la suya para que tal le mandase, y que no era su voluntad salir. Y Cortés le replicó muy buenas razones, y el Montezuma le respondía muy mejores, que no había de salir de sus casas; por manera que estuvieron más de media hora en estas pláticas. Y desde que Juan Velázquez de León y los demás capitanes vieron que se detenía con él y no veían la hora de haberlo sacado de sus casas y tenerle preso, hablaron a Cortés algo alterados y dijeron que: «Haced vos ya con tantas palabras, o le llevamos preso, o le daremos destocadas». Por eso tornóle a decir que, si daba voces o hacía alboroto, que le matarían, porque más vale que desta vez aseguremos nuestras vidas o las perdamos. Y como Juan Velázquez lo decía con voz algo alta y espantosa — porque así era su hablar — y el Montezuma vio a nuestros capitanes como enojados, preguntó a doña Marina qué decían con aquellas palabras altas; y como doña Marina era muy entendida, le dijo: «Señor Montezuma, lo que yo os aconsejo es que vayáis luego con ellos a su aposento, sin ruido ninguno, que yo sé que os harán mucha honra como gran señor que sois, y de otra manera aquí quedaréis muerto». Y entonces el Montezuma dijo a Cortés: «Señor Malinche, ya que eso queréis que sea, yo tengo un hijo y dos hijas legítimos; tomadlos en rehenes, y a mí no me hagáis esta afrenta, que dirán mis principales si me viesen llevar preso». Tornó a decir Cortés que su persona había de ir con ellos y no había de ser otra cosa; y en fin, de muchas más razones que pasaron, dijo que iría de buena voluntad. Y entonces Cortés y nuestros capitanes le hicieron muchas quiriçias y le dijeron que le pedían por ánima que no tuviese enojo, y que dijese a sus capitanes y a los de su guarda que iba de su voluntad, porque había tenido plática de su ídolo Vichilobos y de los papas que le servían, que convenía para su salud y guardar su vida estar con nosotros. Y luego le trajeron sus ricas andas, en que solía salir, con todos sus capitanes que le acompañaron; fue a nuestro aposento, donde le pusimos guardas y velas; y todos cuantos servicios y placeres que le podíamos hacer — así Cortés como todos nosotros — tantos le hacíamos, y no se le echaron prisiones ningunas. Y luego le vinieron a ver todos los mayores principales mexicanos y sus sobrinos, a hablar con él y a saber la causa de su prisión y si mandaba que nos diesen guerra; y el Montezuma les respondió que él holgaba de estar algunos días allí con nosotros de buena voluntad y no por fuerza, y que cuando él algo quisiese se lo diría, y que no se alborotasen ellos ni la ciudad ni tomasen pesar dello, porque esto que ha pasado de estar allí su Vichilobos lo tiene por bien, y se lo han dicho ciertos papas que lo saben, que hablaron con su ídolo sobre ello. Y desta manera, como dije, fue la prisión del gran Montezuma; y allí donde estaba tenía su servicio, y mujeres, y baños en que se bañaba, y siempre a la continua estaban en su compañía veinte grandes señores y consejeros y capitanes; y se hizo a estar preso sin mostrar pasión en ello. Y allí venían con pleitos embajadores de lejanas tierras y le traían sus tributos, y despachaba negocios de importancia. Acuérdome que, cuando venían ante él grandes caciques de lejanas tierras sobre términos, o pueblos, o otras cosas de aquel arte, que por muy gran señor que fuese se quitaba las mantas ricas y se ponía otras de enequen y de poca valía, y descalzo había de venir; y cuando llegaba a los aposentos no entraba derecho, sino por un lado de ellos; y cuando parecía delante del gran Montezuma, los ojos bajos, en tierra, y antes que a él llegasen le hacían tres reverencias y le decían: «Señor, mi señor, y mi gran señor»; entonces le traían pintado y dibujado el pleito o embarazo sobre que venían, en unos paños y mantas de enequen, y con unas varitas muy delgadas y pulidas le señalaban la causa del pleito; y estaban allí junto al Montezuma dos hombres viejos, grandes caciques; y desde que bien habían entendido el pleito, aquellos jueces se lo decían al Montezuma, la justicia que tenía; con pocas palabras los despachaba, y mandaba quién había de llevar las tierras o pueblos; y sin más replicar en ello se salían los pleiteantes, sin volver las espaldas, y con las tres reverencias se salían hasta la sala; y desde que se veían fuera de su presencia del Montezuma se ponían otras mantas ricas y se paseaban por México. Dejaré de decir al presente desta prisión, y digamos cómo los mensajeros que envió el Montezuma con su señal y sello a llamar sus capitanes que mataron nuestros soldados vinieron ante el preso; y lo que con ellos habló yo no lo sé, mas que se los envió a Cortés para que hiciese justicia de ellos. Y tomada su confesión, sin estar el Montezuma delante, confesaron ser verdad lo atrás ya por mí dicho, y que su señor se lo había mandado, que diesen guerra y cobrasen los tributos, y que si algunos teules fuesen en su defensa, que también les diesen guerra o matasen. Y vista esta confesión por Cortés, envióselo a hacer saber al Montezuma cómo le condenaban en aquella cosa; y él se excusó cuanto pudo, y nuestro capitán le envió a decir que así lo creía, que, puesto que merecía castigo conforme a lo que nuestro rey manda — que la persona que manda matar a otros, sin culpa o con culpa, que muera por ello —, mas que le quiere tanto y le desea todo bien, que ya que aquella culpa tuviese, que antes la pagaría Cortés por su persona que verla pasar al Montezuma. Y con todo esto que le envió a decir, estaba temeroso; y sin más gastar razones, Cortés sentenció aquellos capitanes a muerte, y que fuesen quemados delante los palacios del Montezuma; y así se ejecutó luego la sentencia. Y porque no hubiese algún embarazo, entre tanto que se quemaban mandó echar unos grillos al mismo Montezuma; y desde que se los echaron hacía bramuras, y si de antes estaba temeroso, entonces estuvo mucho más. Y después de quemados fue nuestro Cortés con cinco de nuestros capitanes a su aposento, y él mismo se los quitó los grillos, y tales palabras le dijeron y tan amorosas, que se le pasó luego el enojo — porque nuestro Cortés le dijo que no solamente le tenía por hermano, sino mucho más, y que, como es señor y rey de tantos pueblos y provincias, que si él podía, el tiempo andando, le haría que fuese señor de más tierras de las que no ha podido conquistar ni le obedecían, y que si quiere ir a sus palacios que le da licencia para ello; y decíaselo Cortés con nuestras lenguas, y cuando se lo estaba diciendo Cortés parecía que se le saltaban las lágrimas de los ojos al Montezuma. Y respondió con gran cortesía que se lo tenía en ánima, empero bien entendió que todo eran palabras las de Cortés, y que ahora al presente convenía estar allí preso, porque por ventura, como sus principales son muchos y sus sobrinos y parientes le vienen cada día a decir que será bien darnos guerra y sacarlo de prisión, que desde que le vean fuera le atraerán a ello, y que no quería ver en su ciudad rebeliones, y que si no hace su voluntad, por ventura querrán alzar a otro señor; y que él les quitaba aquellos pensamientos con decirles que su dios Vichilobos se lo ha enviado a decir que esté preso. Y lo más cierto, Cortés le había dicho a Aguilar que le dijese secreto que, aunque Malinche le mandase salir de la prisión, que los demás de nuestros capitanes y soldados no querríamos. Y desde que aquello lo oyó, Cortés le echó los brazos encima y le abrazó y dijo: «No en balde, señor Montezuma, os quiero tanto como a mí mismo». Y luego el Montezuma le demandó a Cortés un paje español que le servía, que sabía ya la lengua, que se decía Orteguilla; y fue harto provechoso, así para el Montezuma como para nosotros, porque de aquel paje inquiría y sabía muchas cosas de las de Castilla el Montezuma, y nosotros de lo que le decían sus capitanes; y verdaderamente le era tan buen servicial el paje, que lo quería mucho el Montezuma. Dejemos de hablar de cómo estaba ya el Montezuma algo contento con los grandes halagos, servicios y conversación que con todos nosotros tenía, porque siempre que ante él pasábamos — y aunque fuese Cortés — le quitábamos los bonetes de armas o cascos, que siempre estábamos armados, y él nos hacía gran mesura y honraba a todos. Y digamos los nombres de aquellos capitanes de Montezuma que se quemaron por justicia: el principal se decía Quetzalpopoca, y los otros se decían el uno Coate y el otro Quiavit; el otro no me acuerdo el nombre, que poco va en saber sus nombres. Y digamos que, como este castigo se supo en todas las provincias de la Nueva España, temieron, y los pueblos de la costa adonde mataron nuestros soldados volvieron a servir muy bien a los vecinos que quedaban en la Villa Rica. Y han de considerar los curiosos que esto leyeren tan grandes hechos que entonces hicimos — dar con los navíos al través, lo otro osar entrar en tan fuerte ciudad, teniendo tantos avisos de que allí nos habían de matar desde que dentro nos tuviesen, lo otro tener tanta osadía de osar prender al gran Montezuma, que era rey de aquella tierra, dentro en su gran ciudad y en sus mismos palacios, teniendo tan gran número de guerreros de su guarda, y lo otro osar quemar sus capitanes delante sus palacios y echarle grillos entre tanto que se hacía la justicia. Muchas veces, ahora que soy viejo, me paro a considerar las cosas heroicas que en aquel tiempo pasamos, que me parece las veo presentes; y digo que nuestros hechos — que no los hacíamos nosotros, sino que venían todos encaminados por Dios —, porque ¿qué hombres ha habido en el mundo que osasen entrar cuatrocientos soldados — y aún no llegábamos a ellos — en una fuerte ciudad como es México, que es mayor que Venecia, estando apartados de nuestra Castilla sobre más de mil y quinientas leguas, y prender a un tan gran señor y hacer justicia de sus capitanes delante de él? Porque hay mucho que ponderar en ello, y no así secamente como yo lo digo. Pasaré adelante y diré cómo Cortés despachó luego otro capitán, que estuviese en la Villa Rica como estaba Juan de Escalante, que mataron.</spanish>
<flags>voice: Bernal Díaz del Castillo — blunt, garrulous old soldier, plain and concrete, quietly aggrieved, proud eyewitness (held). ⚠ NO REFERENCE —. Colonial spellings modernized throughout Spanish (ç→c/z, avn→aún, rre→re-, mxᵒ→México, juᵒ→Juan de Escalante, tuçapan→Tuxpan, villa rrica→Villa Rica, des- line breaks reunified). Folio headers, page numbers, signature line, and "Digitized by Google" omitted. teules glossed once in English [idols, gods]. Closing digression to curious readers and old-soldier meditation on deeds "encaminados por Dios" — Bernal's aggrieved rhetorical turn, interpretive but standard in his voice. === CAPITULO XCVII. Como estando el gran — Section 98 ===
<english>Chapter XCVII. How, while the great Moctezuma was prisoner, Cortés and all our soldiers always entertained him and cheered him up, and he was even given license to go hunting — and this license was for his own purpose.

Our captain was diligent in everything, and seeing that Moctezuma was a prisoner, and for fear he might grieve at being shut in and held, he took care every day, after we had prayed — for we had no wine then to say Mass — to go keep him company in the palace. Four captains went with him, especially Pedro de Alvarado and Juan Velázquez de León and Diego de Ordás, and they would ask Moctezuma, with much respect, how he was, and tell him to see that whatever he commanded would be done, and not to grieve over his imprisonment. And he would answer that he was rather glad to be a prisoner — and this because our gods gave us power for it, or his Huitzilopochtli permitted it.

And in talk after talk they gave him to understand, more at length, the things of our holy faith and the great power of our emperor our lord. And even sometimes Moctezuma would play with Cortés at totoloque [a ball game they call by that name], with some small smooth pellets they had made of gold for that game. They would throw the pellets a fair distance, and there were tiles too, also of gold, and at five lines they won or lost certain rich pieces and jewels they laid down. I remember that Pedro de Alvarado and the great Moctezuma were always cheating Cortés — a nephew of his, a great lord, and Pedro de Alvarado always cheating one line more than Cortés had — and Moctezuma, when he saw it, would say, with grace and laughter, that he did not want Tonatio — for so they called Pedro de Alvarado — to cheat Cortés, because he did much ixoxol [in their language, that he lied] in what he cheated, that he always threw one line more. And Cortés and all of us soldiers who at that season were on guard could not keep from laughing at what the great Moctezuma said. People will now say that we laughed at that remark because Pedro de Alvarado, though of gentle body and good manner, was vicious in talking too much, and because we knew his character from this we laughed so much. Let us return to the game: if Cortés won, the jewels went to those nephews and favorites of Moctezuma who served him; and if Moctezuma won, he distributed them among us soldiers who were guarding him. And even apart from what he gave us from the game, he never failed each day to give us presents of gold and clothing — both us and the captain of the guard, who then was Juan Velázquez de León — and in everything he showed himself a friend and servant of Moctezuma.

I also remember that on guard duty there was a soldier very tall of body, well built, and of very great strength, who was said to be a Hulano from Trujillo and was a man of the sea; and when his turn came for the night watch he was so ill-regarded that, speaking here with respect to you lordly readers, he would do dishonest things that Moctezuma heard. And as he was a king of these lands, so valorous, he took it as bad breeding and disrespect that in a place where he could hear it such a thing should be done without regard for his person. He asked his page Orteguilla who that ill-bred, filthy fellow was, and he said he was a man who was used to going on the sea and knew nothing of polish or good breeding; and he also gave him to understand the quality of each one of us soldiers who were there — which was gentleman and which was not — and he kept telling Moctezuma many things he wished to know. Let us return to our soldier from Trujillo: once it was day, Moctezuma had him summoned and told him that, being of that character, without regard for his person he had not shown the respect due him; he begged him not to do it again and ordered a gold jewel given him that weighed five pesos. And the man from Trujillo cared nothing for what he told him. Another night, on purpose, thinking Moctezuma would give him something else, and Moctezuma made it known to Juan Velázquez, captain of the guard, who straightway ordered the man from Trujillo taken off the watch, and they rebuked him with harsh words.

It also happened that another soldier, named Pero López, a great harquebusier, a man not easy to understand though well built, who was on guard over Moctezuma, had words one night with a fellow soldier over whether it was his turn or not, and said — or so it weighed on him — “With this dog, for keeping watch over him constantly, I am so sick in the stomach I could die.” Moctezuma heard that word, and it weighed on his soul; and when Cortés came to keep him company in the palace, Moctezuma let him know, and Cortés took such anger at it that, though Pero López was a very good soldier, he ordered him whipped inside our quarters. From then on, all the soldiers whose turn it was to stand watch over him kept great silence and good breeding — though many of us who guarded him did not need to be ordered — for the good comportment we had to show this great chieftain, who knew us all and knew our names and even our qualities, and was so good that he gave jewels to some of us, mantles to others, and beautiful Indian women to others. As at that time I was a young man and was always on his guard, or passed before him, I would remove my arms bonnet with very great respect; and his page Ortega had even told him that I had come twice to discover this New Spain before Cortés. I had spoken to Orteguilla that I wanted to ask Moctezuma to grant me the mercy of a very beautiful Indian woman; and when Moctezuma learned of it he had me summoned and said to me: “Bernal Díaz del Castillo, they tell me you lack a young woman for clothing and gold, and I shall order a good girl given you today; treat her well, for she is the daughter of a principal man, and they will also give you gold and mantles.” I answered him, with much respect, that I kissed his hands for so great a mercy, and that God our Lord might prosper him. It seems he then asked the page what I had answered, and he declared my reply; and Moctezuma is said to have said that I seemed to him of noble condition, because, as I have said, he knew all our names — and he ordered three gold tiles and two loads of mantles given me. Let us leave off speaking of this and say how, in the morning, after he had made his prayers and sacrifices to the idols, or breakfasted — a little, and it was not meat but axi [a maize-based meal] — he would spend an hour hearing lawsuits from many districts, from chieftains who came to him from far lands. I have already said elsewhere, in the chapter that speaks of it, the manner in which they entered to negotiate and the respect they showed him, and how they were always in his company. At that time, to dispatch business, twenty old men who were his judges; and since this has already been mentioned, I shall not repeat it. And then we came to know that of the many women he kept as mistresses he married some of them to his captains or to very principal persons, his close favorites, and he even gave some to our soldiers; and the one he gave me was one of those ladies, and it was plain in her that she was called doña Francisca. And so life passed — sometimes laughing, and sometimes thinking of his imprisonment.

I wish to say here, though it does not belong to our account, because some curious persons have asked me, why only the soldier I have named called Moctezuma “dog,” and Cortés did not order him whipped even though he was not in his presence, when we were so few soldiers and the Indians might have news of it. I say that at that time all of us, and even Cortés himself, when we stood before the great Moctezuma, showed him reverence with our arms bonnets, which we always carried removed; and he was so good and so well regarded that he did us all much honor — for besides being king of this New Spain, his person and character deserved it. And besides all this, if the matter is well considered — how our lives stood in the balance, so that he had only to command his vassals to take him from prison and at once make war on us — in truth, seeing his presence and royal frankness, and how he always had with him many lords who accompanied him and came from far lands, and many more lords from the great palace who served him, and the great number of people he constantly fed and gave drink to, no more and no less than when he was not a prisoner — all this considered, Cortés took great anger when he learned that such a word had been said to him; and, as he was wrathful over it, straightway ordered him punished, as I have said, and it was well spent on him. Let us pass on and say that at that very moment Indians arrived from Villa Rica laden with the two thick iron chains that Cortés had ordered the smiths to make; they also brought everything belonging to the brigantines, as I have said. And as soon as it was brought, he had the great Moctezuma told of it — and I leave him here, and shall tell what happened concerning it.</english>
<spanish>Capítulo XCVII. Cómo, estando el gran Moctezuma preso, siempre Cortés y todos nuestros soldados le festejábamos y regocijábamos, y aún se le dio licencia para ir a caza, y fue esta licencia para su intención.

Como nuestro capitán en todo era muy diligente, y vio que Moctezuma estaba preso, y por temor no se congojase con estar encerrado y detenido, procuraba cada día, después de haber rezado — que entonces no teníamos vino para decir misa — de irle a tener palacio, e iban con él cuatro capitanes, especialmente Pedro de Alvarado y Juan Velázquez de León y Diego de Ordás, y preguntaban a Moctezuma con mucho acato qué tal estaba, y que mirase que lo que mandaba todo se haría, y que no tuviese congoja de su prisión; y él respondía que antes se holgaba de estar preso, y esto porque nuestros dioses nos daban poder para ello, o su Huitzilopochtli lo permitía.

Y de plática en plática le dieron a entender más por extenso las cosas de nuestra santa fe, y el gran poder del emperador nuestro señor; y aún algunas veces jugaba Moctezuma con Cortés al totoloque, que es un juego que ellos así le llaman, con unos bodoquillos chicos muy lisos que tenían hechos de oro para aquel juego, y tiraban con los bodoquillos algo lejos, y unos tejuelos que también eran de oro, y a cinco rayas ganaban o perdían ciertas piezas y joyas ricas que ponían. Acuérdome que tanteaba a Cortés Pedro de Alvarado y al gran Moctezuma un sobrino suyo, gran señor, y el Pedro de Alvarado siempre tanteaba una raya de más de las que había Cortés; y Moctezuma, como lo vio, decía, con gracia y risa, que no quería que le tantease a Cortés el Tonatio — que así llamaban a Pedro de Alvarado — porque hacía mucho ixoxol en lo que tanteaba, que quiere decir en su lengua que mentía, que echaba siempre una raya de más. Y Cortés y todos nosotros los soldados que en aquella sazón hacíamos guarda no podíamos estar de risa por lo que dijo el gran Moctezuma. Dirán ahora que por qué nos reímos de aquella palabra es porque el Pedro de Alvarado, puesto que era de gentil cuerpo y buena manera, era vicioso en el hablar demasiado, y como le conocemos su condición por esto nos reímos tanto. Y volvamos al juego: y si ganaba Cortés daban las joyas a aquellos sus sobrinos y privados de Moctezuma que le servían, y si ganaba Moctezuma nos lo repartía a los soldados que le hacíamos guarda; y aún, no por lo que nos daba del juego, dejaba cada día de darnos presentes de oro y ropa, así a nosotros como al capitán de la guarda, que entonces era Juan Velázquez de León; y en todo se mostraba ser amigo y servidor de Moctezuma.

También me acuerdo que era de la vela un soldado muy alto de cuerpo y bien dispuesto y de muy grandes fuerzas, que se decía hulano de Trujillo, y era hombre de la mar; y cuando le cabía el cuarto de noche de la vela, era tan mal mirado que, hablando aquí con acato de los señores leyentes, hacía cosas deshonestas que lo oyó Moctezuma; y como era un rey destas tierras tan valeroso, tuvo a mala crianza y desacato que en parte que él lo oyese se hiciese tal cosa y sin miramiento de su persona. Y preguntó a su paje Orteguilla quién era aquel malcriado y sucio, y dijo que era hombre que solía andar en la mar y que no sabe de pulicia ni buena crianza; y también le dio a entender de la calidad de cada uno de los soldados que allí estábamos, cuál era caballero o cuál no, y le decía a la continua muchas cosas que Moctezuma deseaba saber. Volvamos a nuestro soldado de Trujillo: que desde que fue de día, Moctezuma lo mandó llamar y le dijo que, por qué era de aquella condición, que sin tener miramiento a su persona no tenía aquel acato debido, que le rogaba que otra vez no lo hiciese, y mandóle dar una joya de oro que pesaba cinco pesos; y el de Trujillo no se le dio nada por lo que le dijo. Y otra noche adrede, creyendo que le daría otra cosa, y Moctezuma lo hizo saber a Juan Velázquez, capitán de la guarda, y mandó luego el capitán quitar al de Trujillo que no velase más, y con palabras ásperas lo reprendieron.

También acaeció que otro soldado, que se decía Pero López, gran vallestero, y era hombre que no se le entendía mucho, y era bien dispuesto, y velaba a Moctezuma, y sobre si era ora de tomar el cuarto o no, de noche tuvo palabras con un cuadrillero, y dijo — o pesó tal —: «Con este perro, que por velalle a la continua estoy muy malo del estómago, para morirme»; y Moctezuma oyó aquella palabra, y pesóle en el alma; y cuando vino Cortés a tenelle palacio, lo alcanzó a saber, y tomó tanto enojo de ello que, al Pero López, con ser muy buen soldado, le mandó azotar dentro en nuestros aposentos; y desde allí adelante todos los soldados a quien cabía la vela, con mucho silencio y crianza, estaban velando, puesto que no había menester mandallo a muchos de nosotros que le velábamos, por este buen comedimiento que con aqueste gran cacique habíamos de tener; y él bien conocía a todos, y sabía nuestros nombres y aún calidades, y era tan bueno que a todos nos daba joyas, a otros mantas, e indias hermosas. Como en aquel tiempo yo era mozo, y siempre que estaba en su guarda, o pasaba delante de él, con muy gran acato le quitaba mi bonete de armas; y aún le habían dicho el paje Ortega que vine dos veces a descubrir esta Nueva España, primero que Cortés; y yo le había hablado al Orteguilla que le quería demandar a Moctezuma que me hiciese merced de una india muy hermosa; y como lo supo Moctezuma me mandó llamar, y me dijo: «Bernal Díaz del Castillo, hanme dicho que tenéis motolinía de ropa y oro, y os mandaré dar hoy una buena moza; tratadla muy bien, que es hija de hombre principal, y también os darán oro y mantas». Yo le respondí, con mucho acato, que le besaba las manos por tan gran merced, y que Dios nuestro señor le prosperase. Parece ser preguntó al paje qué había respondido, y le declaró la respuesta; y dizque le dijo Moctezuma que de noble condición me parecía Bernal Díaz, porque, como dije tengo, a todos nos sabía los nombres, y me mandó dar tres tejuelos de oro y dos cargas de mantas. Dejemos de hablar desto y digamos cómo, por la mañana, después que hacía sus oraciones y sacrificios a los ídolos, o almorzaba — poca cosa, y no era carne sino axi —, estaba empeñado una hora en oír pleitos de muchas partes de caciques que a él venían de lejos tierras. Ya he dicho otra vez, en el capítulo que dello habla, de la manera que entraban a negociar y el acato que le tenían, y cómo siempre estaban en su compañía. En aquel tiempo, para despachar negocios, veinte hombres ancianos que eran sus jueces; y por que esto ya memorado, no lo tornaré a recitar. Y entonces alcanzamos a saber que las muchas mujeres que tenía por amigas casaba dellas con sus capitanes, o personas principales muy privados, y aún dellas dio a nuestros soldados; y la que me dio a mí era una señora dellas, y bien se pareció en ella que se dijo doña Francisca. Y así se pasaba la vida, unas veces riendo y otras veces pensando en su prisión.

Quiero aquí decir, puesto que no vaya a propósito de nuestra relación, porque me lo han preguntado algunas personas curiosas, por qué solamente el soldado por mí nombrado llamó perro a Moctezuma, y aún no en su presencia le mandó Cortés azotar, siendo tan pocos soldados como éramos y que los indios tuviesen noticia de ello; y esto digo que en aquel tiempo todos nosotros, y aún el mismo Cortés, cuando estábamos delante del gran Moctezuma, le hacíamos reverencia con los bonetes de armas que siempre traíamos quitados; y él era tan bueno y tan bien mirado que a todos nos hacía mucha honra, que, demás de ser rey desta Nueva España, su persona y condición lo merecía. Y demás de todo esto, si bien se considera la cosa en que estaban nuestras vidas — sino solamente mandar a sus vasallos que le sacasen de la prisión y darnos luego guerra —, en ver su presencia y real franqueza, y cómo veíamos que tenía a la continua consigo muchos señores que le acompañaban y venían de lejos tierras, y otros muchos más señores del gran palacio que le hacían, y el gran número de gente que a la continua daba de comer y beber, ni más ni menos que cuando estaba sin prisión — y todo esto considerado —, Cortés tuvo mucho enojo desde que lo supo, que tal palabra le dijese; y como estaba airado de ello, de repente le mandó castigar, como dije tengo, y fue bien empleado en él. Pasemos adelante y digamos que en aquel instante llegaron de Villa Rica indios cargados con las dos cadenas de hierro gruesas que Cortés había mandado hacer a los herreros; también trajeron todas las cosas pertenecientes para los vergantines, como dije tengo; y así como fue traído, se lo hizo saber al gran Moctezuma, y déjolo aquí, y diré lo que sobre ello pasó.</spanish>
<flags>voice: Bernal Díaz del Castillo — blunt, garrulous old soldier, plain and concrete, quietly aggrieved, proud eyewitness (held). ⚠ NO REFERENCE —. === CAPITULO XCVIII. como Cortes mando — Section 99 ===
<english>CHAPTER XCVIII. How Cortés ordered two brigantines of great stoutness and sail-worth to be built for going about on the lagoon, and how the great Moctezuma told Cortés to give him leave to go make prayer at his temples, and what Cortés told him and how he gave him the leave—

since all the gear to build the brigantines had arrived, Cortés went right away to tell the great Moctezuma that he wanted two small vessels built so he could go about at his ease on the lagoon; that he should send his carpenters to cut the wood; and that they would go with our shipwrights, who were called Martín López and one Andrés Núñez. The oak timber stood about four leagues from there; it was brought quickly, the pitch for it was supplied, and since there were many Indian carpenters, the boats were soon built, caulked, and tarred, their yards and sails fitted to their size and measure, and an awning on each one. They came out as sound and as sail-worthy as if a month had been spent taking the pitch—for Martín López was a master of the first rank, and he was the man who later built the thirteen brigantines that helped us win Mexico, as I shall tell further on; and he was a good soldier in war too. Let us leave this aside and tell how Moctezuma told Cortés that he wanted to go out to his temples to make sacrifices and fulfill the devotions owed to his gods—so that his captains and lords might know, especially certain nephews of his who come every day telling him they want to set him free and make war on us, and that he answers them he is glad to stay with us because he believes, as he has been told and as his god Huitzilopochtli has commanded him, as he has been made to believe once before. As for the leave he asked for, Cortés told him to see to it that he did nothing by which he might lose his life; and that, to find out whether there was any misconduct—or whether he was ordering his captains or priests to set him free or make war on us—for that purpose he was sending captains and soldiers who would at once run him through with their swords if they sensed any disturbance concerning his person; and that he should go at a good hour, and that he must sacrifice no persons, which was a great sin against our true God, the one we had preached to him; and that there stood our altars and the image of Our Lady, before whom he might make prayer. Moctezuma said he would sacrifice no soul whatsoever. He went in his rich litter, very well attended by great chiefs, with great pomp as was his custom, and carried before him his insignia—a rod or staff, the sign that his royal person was passing, as they do for the viceroys of this New Spain. Four of our captains went with him to guard him: Juan Velázquez de León, Pedro de Alvarado, Alonso de Ávila, and Francisco de Lugo, with a hundred and fifty soldiers; and the father of the Merced [religious order] went with us too, to dissuade him from the sacrifice if he made one of men. Going as we were to the cue [temple] of Huitzilopochtli, when we were already near the accursed temple, he ordered them to take him down from the litter; he was borne on the shoulders of his nephews and other chiefs until he reached the temple. I have said before that along the streets where his person passed, all the lords had to keep their eyes on the ground and not look him in the face. At the steps of the high sanctuary many priests were waiting to help him up in their arms; they had already sacrificed four Indians the night before. However much our captain told him, and however much the friar of the Merced dissuaded him, it did no good at all—he had to kill men and boys for his sacrifice, and in that season we could do nothing else but dissemble with him, because Mexico and other great cities were very much in turmoil with Moctezuma's nephews, as I shall tell later. Once he had made his sacrifices—for he did not linger long over them—we returned with him to our quarters. He was very cheerful, and to the soldiers who had gone with him he soon gave merced [reward] in gold jewels. Let us leave it here, and tell what else happened—</english>
<spanish>CAPÍTULO XCVIII. Cómo Cortés mandó hacer dos bergantines de mucho sosten y veleros para andar en la laguna, y cómo el gran Moctezuma dijo a Cortés que le diese licencia para ir a hacer oración a sus templos, y lo que Cortés le dijo y cómo le dio la licencia—

pues, como hubo llegado todo el aparejo para hacer los bergantines, luego Cortés se lo fue a hacer saber al gran Moctezuma: que quería hacer dos navíos chicos para andar holgando en la laguna; que mandase a sus carpinteros que fuesen a cortar la madera; y que irían con nuestros maestros de hacer navíos, que se decían Martín López y un Andrés Núñez. Y como la madera de roble estaba a obra de cuatro leguas de allí, de presto fue traída y dado el gálago de ella, y como había muchos carpinteros de los indios, fueron de presto hechos y calafateados y breados, y puestas sus jarcias y velas a su tamaño y medida, y una tolda a cada uno; y salieron tan buenos y veleros como si estuvieran un mes en tomar los gálagos, porque el Martín López era muy extremado maestro, y este fue el que hizo los trece bergantines para ayuda a ganar a México, como adelante diré; y fue un buen soldado para la guerra. Déxemos aparte esto y diré cómo el Moctezuma dijo a Cortés que quería salir e ir a sus templos a hacer sacrificios y cumplir sus devociones para lo que a sus dioses era obligado—como para que conozcan sus capitanes y principales, especial ciertos sobrinos suyos que cada día le vienen a decir que le quieren soltar y darnos guerra, y que les da por respuesta que se huelga de estar con nosotros porque cree, como se lo ha dicho y se lo ha mandado su dios Huitzilopochtli, como ya otra vez se lo ha hecho creer. Y cuanto a la licencia que le demandaba, Cortés le dijo que mirase que no hiciese cosa con que perdiese la vida; y que, para ver si había algún descomedimiento, o mandaba a sus capitanes o papas que le soltasen o nos diesen guerra, que para aquel efecto enviaba capitanes y soldados para que luego le matasen a estocadas, en sintiendo alguna novedad de su persona; y que vaya mucho en buen hora, y que no sacrificase ningunas personas, que era gran pecado contra nuestro Dios verdadero, que es el que le hemos predicado; y que allí estaban nuestros altares y la imagen de nuestra Señora, ante quien podría hacer oración. Y el Moctezuma dijo que no sacrificaría ánima ninguna. Y fue en sus ricas andas, muy acompañado de grandes caciques, con gran pompa, como solía, y llevaba delante sus insignias, que era como vara o bastón, que era la señal de que iba allí su persona real, como hacen a los virreyes desta Nueva España; y con él iban, para guardarle, cuatro de nuestros capitanes que se decían Juan Velázquez de León, y Pedro de Alvarado, y Alonso de Ávila, y Francisco de Lugo, con ciento y cincuenta soldados; y también iba con nosotros el padre de la Merced, para retráerle el sacrificio si le hiciese de hombres. Y yendo como íbamos al cue de Huitzilopochtli, ya que llegábamos cerca del maldito templo, mandó que le sacasen de las andas, y fue arrimado a hombros de sus sobrinos y de otros caciques, hasta que llegó al templo. Ya he dicho otras veces que por las calles por donde iba su persona, todos los principales habían de llevar los ojos puestos en el suelo y no le miraban a la cara. Y llegado a las gradas de lo alto del adoratorio, estaban muchos papas aguardándole para ayudarle a subir de los brazos; y ya le tenían sacrificado de la noche antes cuatro indios. Y por más que nuestro capitán le decía, y se lo retrayese el fraile de la Merced, no aprovechaba cosa ninguna, sino que había de matar hombres y muchachos para hacer su sacrificio; y no podíamos en aquella sazón hacer otra cosa sino disimular con él, porque estaba muy revuelto México y otras grandes ciudades con los sobrinos del Moctezuma, como adelante diré. Y desque hubo hecho sus sacrificios—porque no tardó mucho en ellos—nos volvimos con él a nuestros aposentos, y estaba muy alegre; y a los soldados que con él fuimos, luego nos hizo merced de joyas de oro. Déxemoslo aquí, y diré lo que más pasó—</spanish>
<flags>voice: Bernal Díaz del Castillo — blunt, garrulous old soldier, plain and concrete, quietly aggrieved, proud eyewitness (held). ⚠ NO REFERENCE —.
