Page numbers and “Digitized by Google” omitted as apparatus.</flags>

=== CAPITULO CVlII. Como El gran montezu- — Section 109 ===
<english>CHAPTER CVIII. How the great Montezuma told our Captain Cortés to leave Mexico with all the soldiers, because all the caciques and priests wanted to rise up and make war on us until they killed us, as had been agreed and counseled by their idols, and what Cortés did about it.

As always, without cease, we never lacked sudden alarms—and of such a kind that they were enough to end our lives in them, if our Lord God had not remedied it. And it came about that, as we had placed in the great Cu [temple], on the altar we had made, the image of Our Lady and the cross, and the Holy Gospel was read and Mass said, it seems that Huitzilopochtli and Tezcatlipoca spoke with the priests and told them that they wished to leave their province, since they were so badly treated by the teules [the foreign gods], and that where those figures and the cross stood they did not wish to remain—or that they would not remain there unless we were killed; and that was the answer they gave them, and that they should not trouble themselves to seek another, and that they should tell Montezuma and all his captains to begin the war at once and kill us. And the idol told them to look—that all the gold they used to have to honor them, we had destroyed and made into bricks; and to look—that we were going about lording it over the land, and that we held prisoner five great caciques; and they told them other wicked things, to draw them into making war on us.

And so that Cortés and all of us might know it, the great Montezuma sent to summon Cortés, for he wished to speak with him on matters that went very deep. Orteguilla the page came and said that Montezuma was very agitated and sad, and that that night and part of the day many very principal priests and captains had been with him, speaking secretly in such a way that he could not understand. And when Cortés heard it he went at once to the palace where Montezuma was, and took with him Cristóbal de Olid, who was captain of the guard, and four other captains, and doña Marina, and Jerónimo de Aguilar. After they paid him much respect, Montezuma said: “O lord Malinche and lords captains, how it grieves me—the answer and command that our teules have given to our priests and to me and to all my captains—and it is that we make war on you and kill you and make you go forward by the sea. This I have gathered from it, and it seems to me that before they begin the war you should leave this city at once, and not one of you should remain here. And this, lord Malinche, I tell you: do in every way that suits you, for otherwise they will kill you; and look—your lives are at stake.”

And Cortés and our captains felt grief and even grew agitated, and it was no marvel at a thing so new and so settled—which was to put our lives in great peril over it at that instant, since they were warning us so determinedly. And Cortés told him he took the warning to heart, upon my life, and that at present two things grieved him: not having ships in which to go—for he had ordered broken the ones he brought—and the other, that Montezuma must by force go with us so that he may see our great Emperor; and he asks him, upon my life, to be so good as to hold back the priests and captains until three ships were made on the sandbank, because for them it is the better course if they begin the war—for all would die if they wished to give it. And moreover he said that, so Montezuma may see he wishes at once to do what he tells him, he should order his carpenters to go with two of our soldiers who are great masters at building ships to cut timber near the sandbank.

And Montezuma grew much sadder than before when Cortés told him he would have to go with us before the Emperor; and he said he would give the carpenters, and that he should dispatch at once and have no more words but deeds, and that meanwhile he would order the priests and captains not to trouble themselves to stir up the city, and that to his idols of Huitzilopochtli he would order them placated with sacrifices, and that it would not be with the death of men. And with this so troubled talk Cortés and the captains took leave of Montezuma, and we were all in great anguish, waiting for when they were to begin the war.

Then Cortés sent for Martín López, shipwright, and Andrés Núñez, and with the Indian carpenters the great Montezuma gave him, after they had talked over how the three ships could be built, he ordered him at once to set about making them and getting them ready—since in Veracruz there was every supply of iron and smiths, and oakum, and tow, and caulkers, and pitch—and so they went and cut the timber on the coast of Veracruz, and with full account and care of it and in good haste he began to build his ships. What Cortés told Martín López about it I do not know, and I say this because the chronicler Gómara in his history says he ordered him to make a show of it, as a joke—that he was building them so the great Montezuma would know; I leave it to what they may say, who thank God are alive at this time. But very secretly Martín López told me that in fact and in haste he was building them, and so he left three ships on the stocks.

Let them keep building the ships, and let us say what state we were all in in that great city—so thoughtful, fearing that from one hour to the next they would make war on us; and our Tlascalan allies, and doña Marina, said the same to the captain; and Orteguilla, Montezuma’s page, was always weeping; and all of us very much on the alert—and good guards on Montezuma; I mean of us being on the alert. There was no need to say it so many times, because by day and by night we never took off our arms, gorgets, and cuirasses, and with them we slept. And they will ask now where we slept, what our beds were—nothing but a little straw and a mat, and whoever had a little awning put it underneath; and shod and armed, and every kind of weapon very ready; and the horses saddled and bridled all day; and all so ready that at the touch of the alarm it was as if we stood placed and waiting for that moment—since each night there was no soldier who did not keep watch.

And another thing I say—and not to boast of it—that I grew so accustomed to going armed and sleeping in the manner I have said, that after New Spain was conquered I made it my habit to go to bed dressed and without a bed, and that I slept better than on mattresses. And now when I go to the towns of my encomienda I do not take a bed; and if sometimes I do take one, it is not by my will but for some knights who are present, so they do not see that for lack of a good bed I leave off taking one—but in truth I lie down dressed in it. And another thing I say: that I cannot sleep except a stretch of the night—I have to get up to see the sky and the stars, and I must walk a while in the open air, and this without putting anything on my head, bonnet or cloth; and thanks to God it does me no harm through the custom I had. And I say this so they may know in what fashion we true conquerors went about, and how accustomed we were to arms and to keeping watch.

And let us leave off speaking of it, since I am going outside our relation; and let us say how our Lord Jesus Christ always does us many favors—and it is that on the island of Cuba Diego Velázquez gave great haste to his fleet, as I shall tell ahead—and at that instant there came to New Spain a captain who called himself Pánfilo de Narváez.</english>
<spanish>CAPÍTULO CVIII. Cómo el gran Montezuma dijo a nuestro capitán Cortés que se saliese de México con todos los soldados, porque se querían levantar todos los caciques y papas y darnos guerra hasta matarnos, pues así estaba acordado y dado consejo por sus ídolos, y lo que Cortés sobre ello hizo.

Como siempre, a la continua, nunca nos faltaban sobresaltos — y de tal calidad que eran para acabar las vidas en ellos, si nuestro Señor Dios no lo remediara. Y fue que, como habíamos puesto en el gran Cu, en el altar que hicimos, la imagen de nuestra Señora y la cruz, y se dijo el santo Evangelio y misa, parece ser que los Huichilobos y el Tezcatepuca hablaron con los papas y les dijeron que se querían ir de su provincia, pues tan mal tratados son de los teules, y que adonde están aquellas figuras y cruz no quieren estar, o que ellos no estarían allí si no nos mataban; y aquello les daban por respuesta, y que no curasen de tener otra, y que se lo dijesen a Montezuma y a todos sus capitanes, que luego comenzasen la guerra y nos matasen. Y les dijo el ídolo que mirasen que todo el oro que solían tener para honrarlos lo habíamos deshecho y hecho ladrillos, y que mirasen que nos íbamos señoreando de la tierra, y que teníamos presos a cinco grandes caciques; y les dijeron otras maldades, para atraerlos a darnos guerra.

Y para que Cortés y todos nosotros lo supiésemos, el gran Montezuma envió a llamar a Cortés, porque le quería hablar en cosas que iban mucho en ellas. Y vino el paje Orteguilla y dijo que estaba muy alterado y triste Montezuma, y que aquella noche y parte del día habían estado con él muchos papas y capitanes muy principales, y secretamente hablaban de modo que no lo pudo entender. Y desde que Cortés lo oyó fue de presto al palacio donde estaba el Montezuma, y llevó consigo a Cristóbal de Olid, que era capitán de la guardia, y a otros cuatro capitanes, y a doña Marina, y a Jerónimo de Aguilar. Y después que se le hicieron mucho acato, dijo el Montezuma: «Oh señor Malinche y señores capitanes, cuánto me pesa de la respuesta y mando que nuestros teules han dado a nuestros papas y a mí y a todos mis capitanes, y es que os demos guerra y os matemos y os hagamos ir por la mar adelante. Lo que he colegido de ello, y me parece que antes que comiencen la guerra, que luego salgáis desta ciudad y no quede ninguno de vosotros aquí. Y esto, señor Malinche, os digo: que hagáis en todas maneras que os conviene, si no mataros han; y mirad que os va las vidas».

Y Cortés y nuestros capitanes sintieron pesar y aun se alteraron, y no era de maravillar cosa tan nueva y determinada, que era poner nuestras vidas en gran peligro sobre ello en aquel instante, pues tan determinadamente nos lo avisaban. Y Cortés le dijo que se lo tenía en mi vida el aviso, y que al presente de dos cosas le pesaba: no tener navíos en que irse — que mandó quebrar los que trujo —, y la otra que por fuerza había de ir el Montezuma con nosotros para que vea nuestro gran Emperador; y que le pide por mi vida que tenga por bien que, hasta que se hagan tres navíos en el arenal, detenga a los papas y capitanes, porque para ellos es el mejor partido si comienzan ellos la guerra, porque todos morirían si la quisiesen dar. Y más dijo que, porque vea Montezuma que quiere luego hacer lo que le dice, mande a sus carpinteros que vayan con dos de nuestros soldados, que son grandes maestros de hacer navíos, a cortar la madera cerca del arenal.

Y el Montezuma estuvo mucho más triste que de antes, como Cortés le dijo que había de ir con nosotros ante el Emperador; y dijo que daría los carpinteros, y que luego despachase y no hubiese más palabras sino obras, y que entre tanto él mandaría a los papas y a los capitanes que no curasen de alborotar la ciudad, y que a sus ídolos de Huichilobos mandaría aplacarlos con sacrificios, y que no sería con muerte de hombres. Y con esta tan alborotada plática se despidió Cortés y los capitanes del Montezuma, y estábamos todos con gran congoja, esperando cuándo habían de comenzar la guerra.

Luego Cortés mandó llamar a Martín López, carpintero de hacer navíos, y Andrés Núñez, y con los indios carpinteros que le dio el gran Montezuma, después de platicado el porte que se podrían labrar los tres navíos, le mandó que luego pusiese por la obra de los hacer y poner a punto — pues que en la Villa Rica había todo aparejo de hierro y herreros, y estopa, y cáñamo, y calafates y brea —, y así fueron y cortaron la madera en la costa de la Villa Rica; y con toda la cuenta y cuidado de ella y con buena prisa comenzó a labrar sus navíos. Lo que Cortés le dijo al Martín López sobre ello no lo sé, y esto digo porque dice el coronista Gómara en su historia que le mandó que hiciese muestra, como cosa de burla, que los labraba para que lo supiese el gran Montezuma; remito a lo que ellos dijeren, que gracias a Dios son vivos en este tiempo. Mas muy secretamente me dijo el Martín López que de hecho y apriesa los labraba, y así los dejó en astillero tres navíos.

Dejémosles labrando los navíos y digamos cuáles andábamos todos en aquella gran ciudad, tan pensativos, temiendo que de una hora a otra nos habían de dar guerra; y nuestras naborías de Tlascala, y doña Marina, así lo decían al capitán; y el Orteguilla, el paje de Montezuma, siempre estaba llorando; y todos nosotros muy a punto — y buenas guardas al Montezuma; digo de nosotros estar a punto. No había necesidad de decirlo tantas veces, porque de día ni de noche no se nos quitaban las armas, gorgueras y antipares, y con ello dormíamos. Y dirán ahora dónde dormíamos, de qué eran nuestras camas: sino un poco de paja y una estera, y el que tenía un toldillo ponérselo debajo; y calzados y armados, y todo género de armas muy a punto; y los caballos ensillados y enfrenados todo el día; y todos tan prestos que, en tocando al arma, como si estuviéramos puestos y aguardando para aquel punto — pues cada noche no quedaba soldado que no velase.

Y otra cosa digo, y no por me jactar de ello: que quedé yo tan acostumbrado a andar armado y dormir de la manera que he dicho, que después de conquistada la Nueva España tenía por costumbre de acostarme vestido y sin cama, y que dormía mejor que en colchones. Y ahora, cuando voy a los pueblos de mi encomienda, no llevo cama; y si alguna vez la llevo, no es por mi voluntad, sino por algunos caballeros que se hallan presentes, porque no vean que por falta de buena cama la dejo de llevar; mas en verdad que me echo vestido en ella. Y otra cosa digo: que no puedo dormir sino un rato de la noche, que me tengo de levantar a ver el cielo y estrellas, y me he de pasear un rato al sereno — y esto sin poner en la cabeza cosa ninguna de bonete ni paño —, y gracias a Dios no me hace mal por la costumbre que tenía. Y esto he dicho porque sepan de qué arte andábamos los verdaderos conquistadores, y cómo estábamos tan acostumbrados a las armas y a velar.

Y dejemos de hablar en ello, pues que salgo fuera de nuestra relación; y digamos cómo nuestro Señor Jesucristo siempre nos hace muchas mercedes — y es que en la isla de Cuba Diego Velázquez dio mucha prisa en su armada, como adelante diré —, y vino en aquel instante a la Nueva España un capitán que se decía Pánfilo de Narváez.</spanish>
<flags>voice: Bernal Díaz del Castillo — blunt, garrulous old soldier, plain and concrete, quietly aggrieved, proud eyewitness (held). ⚠ NO REFERENCE —. Closing asterisk line (“Bernal Díaz del Castillo.—46.”) omitted as edition apparatus, not narrative source.</flags>

=== CAPITULO CIX. Como diego velazquez, — Section 110 ===
<english>CHAPTER CIX. How Diego Velázquez, governor of Cuba, pressed very hard to send his fleet against us, with Pánfilo de Narváez as captain general in it, and how Licentiate Lucas Vázquez de Ayllón, oidor [royal judge] of the Royal Audiencia of Santo Domingo, came in his company, and what happened in the affair.

Let us now turn back a little in our account, so that what I am about to tell may be well understood, as I said in the chapter that speaks of it: how Diego Velázquez, governor of Cuba, learned that we had sent our procuradores to His Majesty with all the gold we had taken—the Sun and the Moon, and many sundry jewels, and gold in grains from the mines, and many other things of great value—and that we had sent him nothing; and likewise he learned how Don Juan Rodríguez de Fonseca, Bishop of Burgos—or Archbishop of Rosano, as he was also called—who at that time was president of the Indies and commanded everything very absolutely, because His Majesty was in Flanders, had treated our procuradores very badly; and they say that the same bishop sent from Castile at that time many favors to Diego Velázquez, with notice and orders to send and arrest us, and that he would give him from Castile every favor for it. With that great favor Diego Velázquez fitted out a fleet of nineteen ships with fourteen hundred soldiers, carrying about twenty guns and much powder and every sort of tackle for stones and balls, and two gun-founders—for the captain of the artillery was one Rodrigo Martín—and eighty horse and ninety crossbowmen and seventy harquebusiers; and Diego Velázquez himself in person—and although he was quite fat and heavy, he went through Cuba from town to town and village to village, provisioning the fleet and drawing the settlers who had Indians, and kin, and friends, to come with Pánfilo de Narváez so they might bring Cortés prisoner, and all of us his captains and soldiers—or at least that some of us might not be left alive—and he went so inflamed with anger, and so diligent, that he came as far as Guaniguanico, which is past Havana more than sixty leagues; and going in this fashion, before his fleet sailed, it seems they came to know of it—the Royal Audiencia of Santo Domingo and the Jeronymite friars who were governing—and Licenciado Zuazo sent them the notice and report of it from Cuba; he had come to that island to take up residence with the same Diego Velázquez. When the Royal Audiencia learned of it, and remembered our many good and loyal services to God and His Majesty, and that we had sent our procuradores with great presents to our king and lord, and that Diego Velázquez had neither reason nor justice to come with armed hand to take vengeance on us, but should demand it through justice, and that if he came with the fleet it would be a great hindrance to our conquest, they resolved to send a licentiate named Lucas Vázquez de Ayllón, who was oidor of that same Royal Audiencia, to obstruct Diego Velázquez's fleet and not let it pass, and to impose great penalties on him besides. The same oidor came to Cuba and carried out his diligences and protests as the Royal Audiencia commanded him, so that Velázquez might not carry out his intention; and for all the penalties and requerimientos he made and laid on him, nothing availed—because Diego Velázquez, so favored by the Bishop of Burgos and having spent all he had raising that war party against us, held all the requerimientos they made to him worth nothing, not a castanet's worth; rather he showed himself bolder. And when the oidor saw that, he came over with Narváez himself to make peace and arrange good terms between Cortés and Narváez. Other soldiers said he came with intent to help us, and if he could not do that, to take the land for himself by his authority as oidor; and in this manner he came as far as the port of San Juan de Ulúa, and there he remained—and what happened afterward shall be told.</english>
<spanish>CAPÍTULO CIX. Cómo Diego Velázquez, gobernador de Cuba, dio muy gran prisa en enviar su armada contra nosotros, y en ella por capitán general a Pánfilo de Narváez, y cómo vino en su compañía el licenciado Lucas Vázquez de Ayllón, oidor de la Real Audiencia de Santo Domingo, y lo que sobre ello se hizo.

Volvamos ahora a decir algo atrás de nuestra relación, para que bien se entienda lo que ahora diré ya, como dije en el capítulo que de ello habla: que como Diego Velázquez, gobernador de Cuba, supo que habíamos enviado nuestros procuradores a Su Majestad, con todo el oro que habíamos habido, y el Sol y la Luna, y muchas diversidades de joyas, y oro en granos sacado de las minas, y otras muchas cosas de gran valor, y que no le acudimos con cosa ninguna; y asimismo supo cómo don Juan Rodríguez de Fonseca, obispo de Burgos, o arzobispo de Rosano, que así se nombraba, y en aquella sazón era presidente de Indias, y lo mandaba todo muy absolutamente porque Su Majestad estaba en Flandes, y había tratado muy mal el obispo a nuestros procuradores; y dicen que le envió el mismo obispo desde Castilla en aquella sazón muchos favores a Diego Velázquez, y aviso y mandado para que nos enviase a prender, y que le daría desde Castilla todo favor para ello. El Diego Velázquez, con aquel gran favor, hizo una armada de diez y nueve navíos y con mil y cuatrocientos soldados, en que traían sobre veinte tiros y mucha pólvora y todo género de aparejos de piedras y pelotas, y dos astilleros—que el capitán de la artillería se decía Rodrigo Martín—y traía ochenta de a caballo y noventa ballesteros y setenta escopeteros; y el mismo Diego Velázquez, por su persona—y aunque era bien gordo y pesado, andaba en Cuba de villa en villa y pueblo en pueblo, proveyendo la armada y atrayendo los vecinos que tenían indios, y parientes, y amigos, que viniesen con Pánfilo de Narváez para que le llevasen preso a Cortés y a todos nosotros sus capitanes y soldados, o al de menos no quedásemos algunos con las vidas—y andaba tan encendido en enojo, y tan diligente, que vino hasta Guaniguanico, que es pasada la Habana más de sesenta leguas; y andando desta manera, antes que saliese su armada, pareció ser que alcanzaron a saberlo la Real Audiencia de Santo Domingo y los frailes jerónimos que estaban por gobernadores; el cual aviso y relación de ello les envió desde Cuba el licenciado Zuazo, que había venido a aquella isla a tomar residencia al mismo Diego Velázquez. Pues como lo supieron la Real Audiencia, y tenían memoria de nuestros muchos y buenos y leales servicios que hacíamos a Dios y a Su Majestad, y habíamos enviado nuestros procuradores con grandes presentes a nuestro rey y señor, y que el Diego Velázquez no tenía razón ni justicia para que con su mano armada venga a tomar venganza de nosotros, sino que por justicia lo demandase, y que si venía con la armada, que era gran estorbo para nuestra conquista, acordaron de enviar a un licenciado que se decía Lucas Vázquez de Ayllón, que era oidor de la misma Real Audiencia, para que estorbase la armada al Diego Velázquez y no la dejase pasar, y que sobre ello pusiese grandes penas; y vino a Cuba el mismo oidor, y hizo sus diligencias y protestaciones como le era mandado por la Real Audiencia, para que no saliese con su intención el Velázquez; y por más penas y requerimientos que le hizo y puso, no aprovechó cosa ninguna, porque, como el Diego Velázquez era tan favorecido del obispo de Burgos y había gastado cuanto tenía en hacer aquella gente de guerra contra nosotros, no tuvo todos los requerimientos que le hicieron en una castañeta, en nada; antes se mostró más bravoso. Y desde que aquello vio el oidor, vino con el mismo Narváez para poner paces y dar buenos conciertos entre Cortés y el Narváez. Otros soldados dijeron que venía con intención de ayudarnos, y si no lo pudiese hacer, tomar la tierra en sí por su magistratura como oidor; y desta manera vino hasta el puerto de San Juan de Ulúa, y quedarse aquí, y pasar adelante, lo que sobre ello se hizo.</spanish>
<flags>voice: Bernal Díaz del Castillo — blunt, garrulous old soldier, plain and concrete, quietly aggrieved, proud eyewitness (held). ⚠ NO REFERENCE —. Colonial spellings modernized throughout (bolvamos→volvamos, dezir→decir, nra→nuestra, rrelaçion→relación, avia→había, Enbiado→enviado, nros→nuestros, avido→habido, ansimismo→asimismo, nonbrava→nombrava, estava→estaba, Enbio→envió, fabores→favores, grā→gran, quatrozientos→cuatrocientos, polvora→pólvora, cavallo→caballo, gordo kept, vezinos→vecinos, q̄dasemos→quedásemos, Ençendido→encendido, paresçio→pareció, questavan→estaban, çuaço→Zuazo, rresidençia→residencia, serviçios→servicios, justiçia→justicia, estorvo→estorbo, nra→nuestra, diligençias→diligencias, protestaçiones→protestaciones, belazq̄z→Velázquez, faboresçido→favorecido, Castaneta→castañeta, paçes→paces, conçiertos→conciertos, dixeron→dijeron, maḡ→magistratura, vlua→Ulúa). Page numbers and edition apparatus omitted. "Astilleros" rendered as gun-founders given the artillery captain named immediately after; uncertain whether shipwrights was meant. "Rosano" kept as in manuscript (likely Rossano). Closing forward pointer ("what happened afterward shall be told") preserved as narrative hinge, not apparatus.</flags>

=== CAPITULO CIX [CX].¹ Como panfilo de — Section 111 ===
<english>CHAPTER CIX [CX]. How Pánfilo de Narváez reached the port of San Juan de Ulúa, called Veracruz, with his whole armada, and what befell him.

Pánfilo de Narváez was coming over the sea with his whole fleet, which was nineteen ships. It seems that near the sierras called San Martín he met a north wind; on that coast the seas run crosswise, and by night he lost a ship of little size that went across. A gentleman who called himself Cristóbal de Morante, a native of Medina del Campo, was captain in her, and certain men drowned; and with all the rest of the fleet he came to San Juan de Ulúa. And when word spread of that great armada — for an armada made against us on the island of Cuba may well be called great — the soldiers Cortés had sent out to look for the mines got news of it, and three of them came over to Narváez’s ships: the ones called Cervantes el Chocarrero, Escalona, and the other called Alonso Hernández Carretero. And when they found themselves aboard the ships with Narváez, they say they raised their hands to God, who had delivered them from Cortés’s power and from being in the great city of Mexico, where every day they looked for death staring them in the face. And as they ate with Narváez and drank wine — and drank far too much — they kept saying to one another, right before the general himself: “See whether it is not better to be here drinking good wine than captive in Cortés’s power, where he kept us so abased night and day that we dared not speak, waiting from one day to the next

for death before our eyes.” And Cervantes even said what a swindler he was under color of favors: “Narváez, Narváez, how fortunate you are, and what a time you have come — that traitor Cortés has gathered more than seven hundred thousand pesos of gold, and all the soldiers are very angry with him because he has taken a large part of what belonged to them from the gold shares, and they will not accept what he gives them.” And those soldiers who deserted to us — for they were wretched, base fellows — told Narváez far more than he wished to know; and they also gave him word that eight leagues from there a town was settled in a villa called the Rich Town of Veracruz, and Gonzalo de Sandoval was captain there with seventy soldiers, all old men and sick, and that if he sent fighting men against them they would surrender at once; and they told him many other things besides. Let us leave all this talk and say how the great Montezuma soon came to know it: as they lay at anchor in the port with the ships, many captains and soldiers, he sent his chief men secretly — Cortés did not know — and ordered that they be given food and gold and clothing, and that the nearest towns should supply them with provisions. And Narváez sent to tell Montezuma many harsh words and insults against Cortés and against all of us — that we were bad people, thieves, who had come fleeing Castile without license from our king and lord; and that once word reached our king and lord that we were in these lands, and of the harms and robberies we committed, we held Montezuma prisoner; and to stop so much damage he ordered Narváez to come at once with all those ships and soldiers and horses, so that they would release him from prison, and that he should seize or kill Cortés and all of us as evil men, and send us to Castile in those same ships; and that once we arrived there, he would order us killed. And he sent him many other wild things besides. And the interpreters who made all this understood to the Indians were the three soldiers who had deserted to us and already knew the language. And besides all this talk Narváez sent Montezuma certain things from Castile. And when Montezuma learned of it he was greatly pleased with those tidings, because, as they told him, Narváez had so many ships and horses and gunners and harquebusiers and crossbowmen, and there were one thousand three hundred soldiers; and from that he believed they would seize us. And besides this, when his chief men saw our three soldiers with Narváez and heard them speak so ill of Cortés, he took for certain everything Narváez had sent to say; and they carried the whole armada painted on cloths, true to life. Then Montezuma sent him much more gold and mantas, and ordered that all the towns of the district should bring him plenty of food. And Montezuma had already known it for three days, and Cortés knew nothing at all. And one day, as our captain went to visit him and keep him in court, after the courtesies they exchanged it seemed to Captain Cortés that Montezuma was very cheerful and of good countenance, and he said he felt so; and Montezuma answered that he was better still. And Montezuma, because he saw him come to visit twice in one day, feared that Cortés knew of the ships; and to get the jump on him and not be thought suspicious, he said: “Lord Malinche, at this very moment messengers have reached me telling how at the port where you landed have come eighteen and more ships, with many people and horses — and they bring it all painted on mantas. And because you visited me twice today, I believed you had come to give me news of them; so you will not need to build ships. And because you did not tell me on the side, I was angry with you for keeping it hidden; yet on the other hand I was glad, for your brothers are coming, so that you may all go to Castile and there be no more words.” And when Cortés heard of the ships and saw the painting on the cloth, he was greatly pleased and said: “Thanks be to God, who provides at the best time.” As for us soldiers, our joy was so great we could not keep still; the horsemen skirmished, and we fired shots. And Cortés was very thoughtful, for he well understood that that armada was the one Governor Diego Velázquez sent against him and against all of us; and as a man who shares openly what he feels, he laid before all of us, captains and soldiers, what he thought of it, and with great gifts of gold that he gave us and promises that he would make us rich he drew us all so that we should stand with him. And we did not yet know who came as captain. We were very glad at the news, and at the extra gold beyond what he had given us by way of rewards — as though he gave it from his own estate, and not from what belonged to us by share — and it was a great relief and help that our Lord Jesus Christ sent us. And here I leave off, and will tell what passed in Narváez’s camp.</english>
<spanish>CAPÍTULO CIX [CX]. Cómo Pánfilo de Narváez llegó al puerto de San Juan de Ulúa, que se dice la Veracruz, con toda su armada y lo que le sucedió.

Viniendo Pánfilo de Narváez con toda su flota, que eran diez y nueve navíos por la mar, parece ser que junto a las sierras de San Martín que así se llaman tuvo un viento norte, y en aquella costa es travesía, y de noche se le perdió un navío de poco porte que dio al través; venía en él por capitán un hidalgo que se decía Cristóbal de Morante, natural de Medina del Campo, y se ahogaron cierta gente; y con toda la más flota vino a San Juan de Ulúa. Y como se supo de aquella grande armada — que para haberse hecho en la isla de Cuba, grande se puede llamar — tuvieron noticia de ella los soldados que había enviado Cortés a buscar las minas, y viénense a los navíos del Narváez los tres de ellos que se decían Cervantes el Chocarrero, y Escalona, y el otro que se decía Alonso Hernández Carretero. Y cuando se vieron dentro en los navíos y con el Narváez, dicen que alzaban las manos a Dios, que les libró del poder de Cortés y de salir de la gran ciudad de México, donde cada día esperaban la muerte al ojo. Y como comían con el Narváez y bebían vino — y hartos de beber, demasiado — estaban diciéndose los unos a los otros delante del mismo general: «Mira si no es mejor estar aquí bebiendo buen vino que cautivo en poder de Cortés, que nos traía de noche y de día tan avasallados que no osábamos hablar, aguardando de un día a otro

la muerte al ojo.» Y aun decía el Cervantes qué truhán era bajo color de gracias: «Narváez, Narváez, qué bienaventurado que eres, y a qué tiempo has venido — que tiene ese traidor de Cortés allegados más de setecientos mil pesos de oro, y todos los soldados están muy mal con él porque les ha tomado mucha parte de lo que les cabía del oro de parte, y no lo quieren recibir lo que les da.» Y aquellos soldados que se nos huyeron — como eran ruin y soezes — decían al Narváez mucho más de lo que quería saber; y también le dieron por aviso que ocho leguas de allí estaba poblada en una villa que se dice la Villa Rica Veracruz, y estaba en ella por capitán un Gonzalo de Sandoval, con setenta soldados, todos viejos y dolientes, y que si enviase a ellos gente de guerra luego se le darían; y le dicen otras muchas cosas. Dejemos todas estas pláticas y digamos cómo luego lo alcanzó a saber el gran Montezuma: como estaban allí surtos en el puerto con los navíos, muchos capitanes y soldados, envió sus principales secretamente — no lo supo Cortés — y les mandó dar comida y oro y ropa, y que de los pueblos más cercanos les proveyesen de bastimento. Y el Narváez envió a decir al Montezuma muchas malas palabras y descomedimientos contra Cortés y de todos nosotros — que éramos unas gentes malas, ladrones, que venimos huyendo de Castilla sin licencia de nuestro rey y señor; y que como se tuvo noticia, el rey nuestro señor, que estábamos en estas tierras, y de los males y robos que hacíamos, teníamos preso al Montezuma; y para estorbar tantos daños le mandó al Narváez que luego viniese con todas aquellas naos y soldados y caballos, para que le suelten de las prisiones, y que a Cortés y a todos nosotros, como malos, nos prendiesen o matasen, y en las mismas naos nos enviase a Castilla; y que desde que allá llegásemos nos mandaría matar. Y le envió a decir otros muchos desatinos. Y eran los intérpretes para dárselo a entender a los indios los tres soldados que se nos fueron, que ya sabían la lengua. Y demás de estas pláticas le envió el Narváez ciertas cosas de Castilla. Y cuando Montezuma lo supo tuvo gran contento con aquellas nuevas, porque, como le decían que tenía tantos navíos y caballos y tiros y escopeteros y vallesteros, y eran mil trescientos soldados, de ahí arriba creyó que nos prendería. Y demás de esto, como sus principales vieron a nuestros tres soldados con el Narváez y veían que decían mucho mal de Cortés, tuvo por cierto todo lo que el Narváez le envió a decir; y toda la armada se la llevaron pintada en unos paños al natural. Entonces el Montezuma le envió mucho más oro y mantas, y mandó que todos los pueblos de la comarca le llevasen bien de comer. Y ya había tres días que lo sabía el Montezuma, y Cortés no sabía cosa ninguna. Y un día, yéndole a ver nuestro capitán y tenelle palacio, y después de las cortesías que entre ellos se tenían, pareció al capitán Cortés que el Montezuma estaba muy alegre y de buen semblante, y le dijo que tal se sentía; y el Montezuma respondió que mejor estaba. Y también, como Montezuma lo vio ir a le visitar en un día dos veces, temió que Cortés sabía de los navíos; y por ganar por la mano, y no le tuviese por sospechoso, le dijo: «Señor Malinche, ahora en este punto me han llegado mensajeros de cómo en el puerto a donde desembarcaste han venido diez y ocho y más navíos, y mucha gente y caballos — y todo nos lo traen pintado en unas mantas. Y como me visitaste hoy dos veces, creí que me venías a dar nuevas de ellos; así que no habrás menester hacer navíos. Y porque no me lo decías por una parte tenía enojo de vos, por tenérmelo en cubierto; y por otra me holgaba, porque vienen vuestros hermanos, para que todos os vayáis a Castilla y no haya más palabras.» Y cuando Cortés oyó lo de los navíos y vio la pintura del paño, se holgó en gran manera y dijo: «Gracias a Dios que al mejor tiempo provee.» Pues nosotros los soldados era tanto el gozo que no podíamos estar quedos; y de alegría escaramucearon los de a caballo, y tiramos tiros; y Cortés estuvo muy pensativo, porque bien entendió que aquella armada la enviaba el gobernador Diego Velázquez contra él y contra todos nosotros; y como sabio que era, comunicó lo que sentía de ella con todos nosotros, capitanes y soldados, y con grandes dádivas de oro que nos da y ofrecimientos que nos haría ricos nos atrajo a todos para que estuviésemos con él. Y no sabíamos quién venía por capitán. Y estábamos muy alegres con las nuevas, y con el más oro de lo que nos había dado por vía de mercedes — como que lo daba de su hacienda, y no de lo que nos cabía de parte — y fue gran socorro y ayuda que nuestro señor Jesucristo nos enviaba. Y aquí me quedo, y diré lo que pasó en el real de Narváez.</spanish>
<flags>voice: Bernal Díaz del Castillo — blunt, garrulous old soldier, plain and concrete, quietly aggrieved, proud eyewitness (held). ⚠ NO REFERENCE —. Colonial spellings modernized throughout (e.g. paresçe→parece, çierta→cierta, flota, notiçia→noticia, avia→había, Enbiado→enviado, dezir→decir, çibdad→ciudad, cō→con, mysmo→mismo, avasallados, rresçibir→recibir, rruynes→ruin, aviso, estava→estaba, Enbio→envió, prinçipales→principales, monteçuma/montezuma, monteżuma→Montezuma, yen→yendo, tenelle, senblante→semblante, rrespondio→respondió, desEnbarcartes→desembarcaste, an→han, abras→habrás, deziades→decías, vros→vuestros, aya→haya, oyo→oyó, holgo→se holgó, Escaramuçearon→escaramucearon, governador, ofresçimientos, estubiesemos→estuviésemos, avia→había, merçedes→mercedes, hazienda, Jesuxpō→Jesucristo, Enbiava→enviaba, rreal→real). Edition footnote on crossed-out chapter lines and page numbers omitted as apparatus. "Dizque" rendered as "they say" / "dicen que." "Truhan so color de gracias" kept as author’s scornful image. "La muerte al ojo" kept as "death staring them in the face" / "la muerte al ojo." Direct speech preserved in Bernal Díaz’s plain register. Closing forward pointer ("here I leave off… Narváez's camp") preserved as narrative hinge.</flags>

=== CAPITULO CXI. Como panfilo de narvaez — Section 112 ===
<english>CHAPTER CXI. How Pánfilo de Narváez sent five men from his fleet to summon Gonzalo de Sandoval, who was captain at the Villa Rica, to come at once with all the residents, and what happened thereon.

As for those three wicked men of our soldiers, as I have already told, who went over to Narváez and gave him notice of everything Cortés and all of us had done since we entered New Spain — they told him that Captain Gonzalo de Sandoval was about eight or nine leagues from there, in a settled town called the Villa Rica de la Veracruz, and that he had with him seventy residents, and that the oldest and sickest among them agreed to send to the town a cleric named Guevara, who had a gift for speech, and another man of much account named Amaya, a kinsman of Diego Velázquez of Cuba, and a scribe named Vergara, and three witnesses whose names I do not remember — men Narváez sent to notify Gonzalo de Sandoval to surrender to him at once. For that purpose they said they brought copies of the provisions.

They say Gonzalo de Sandoval already knew of the ships from Indian news and from the great number of people coming in them. As he was a very valiant man in his affairs, he and his soldiers were always armed and on their guard, suspecting that the fleet belonged to Diego Velázquez and that he would send men to that town to seize it. To be freer of the old and sick soldiers, he soon sent them to an Indian town called Papalote and remained with the healthy ones. Sandoval always kept good spies on the roads to Cempoala — the route by which they would have to come to the town — and he was summoning his soldiers and drawing them to his side, [saying] that if Diego Velázquez or anyone else came, the town must not be handed over. All the soldiers say they answered as he wished. He ordered a gallows built on a hill.

While his spies were on the roads, they came in haste and gave him notice that six Spaniards and Indians from Cuba were coming near the town where he was. Sandoval waited in his house and did not go out to receive them; he had already ordered that no soldier leave his house or speak to them. The cleric and the others who came with them could find no Spanish resident to speak with — only Indians working on the fortress, and they could not understand them. When they entered the town they went to the church to pray, then to Sandoval's house, which seemed to them the largest in the town. The cleric, after saying good evening — so they say he said — and Sandoval answered that he had come at a good hour, they say the cleric Guevara — that was his name — began a speech. He said that Señor Diego Velázquez, governor of Cuba, had spent much money on the fleet, and that Cortés and all the others he had brought in his company had been traitors to him, and that he had come to notify them to go at once and give obedience to Señor Pánfilo de Narváez, who came as captain general of Diego Velázquez.

When Sandoval heard those words and the insolence Father Guevara spoke, he was bursting with anger at what he heard and told him: “Señor Father, you speak very ill in calling us traitors. Here we are better servants of His Majesty than Diego Velázquez. And because you are a cleric, we punish you as your bad upbringing deserves. Go with God to Mexico, where Cortés is — he is captain general and chief justice of this New Spain — and he will answer you. Here you have nothing more to say.”

Then the cleric said very boldly to his scribe Vergara to take out at once the provisions he carried in his bosom and notify Sandoval and the residents with him. Sandoval told the scribe not to read any papers — he did not know whether they were provisions or other writings. In talk after talk the scribe began to take from his bosom the papers he carried, and Sandoval told him: “Look, Vergara — I have already told you not to read any papers here. Go to Mexico instead, and I promise you that if you do read them, I will have you given a hundred lashes. We do not even know whether you are the king's scribe — show your title for it, and if you bring it, read it. Nor do we know whether the provisions are originals, copies, or other papers.” The cleric, who was very arrogant, said: “What are you doing with these traitors? Take out those provisions and notify them.” He said this in great anger. When Sandoval heard that word he said he lied like a vile cleric, and at once ordered his soldiers to take them prisoner to Mexico. He had scarcely said it when many Indians who worked on the fortress snatched them up in net hammocks like sinful souls, carried them on their backs, and in four days brought them near Mexico City — marching day and night with relay Indians. They went terrified when they saw so many great cities and towns; they were given food — some taking them, others leaving them and going their way. They say they went along thinking whether it was enchantment or a dream. Sandoval sent an alguazil [constable] with them until they were brought to Mexico City, to Solís's forge — Ordóñez's ironworks, which they now call Solís, behind the gate.

As soon as he sent them off prisoner, he wrote posthaste to Cortés, who was captain of the fleet, telling him everything that had happened. When Cortés learned they were coming as prisoners and were nearing Mexico City, he sent mounts for the three most principal men and ordered them released from prison at once. He wrote them that he was grieved Gonzalo de Sandoval had shown such disrespect, and that he would have wished Sandoval had done them much honor. When they reached Mexico City he went out to receive them and lodged them in the city very honorably. When the cleric and his companions saw Mexico City — so vast a city — and the wealth of gold we had, and the many other cities on the waters of the lagoon, and all our captains and soldiers, and Cortés's great generosity, they stood astonished. After two days with us, Cortés spoke to them with promises and flattery, and even anointed their hands with gold tiles and jewels, and sent them back to their Narváez with supplies for the road. Where they had come as very bold lions, they returned very tame and offered themselves as his servants. When they reached Cempoala to report to their captain, they began to summon all Narváez's camp to pass over to us.

And I leave it here, and will tell how Cortés wrote to Narváez and what happened thereon.</english>
<spanish>CAPÍTULO CXI. Cómo Pánfilo de Narváez envió cinco personas de su armada a requerir a Gonzalo de Sandoval, que estaba por capitán en la Villa Rica, que se diese luego con todos los vecinos, y lo que sobrello pasó.

Como aquellos tres malos de nuestros soldados, por mí memorados, que se le pasaron a Narváez y le daban aviso de todas las cosas que Cortés y todos nosotros habíamos hecho desde que entramos en la Nueva España — le avisaron que el capitán Gonzalo de Sandoval estaba obra de ocho o nueve leguas de allí, en una villa que estaba poblada, que se decía la Villa Rica de la Veracruz, y que tenía consigo setenta vecinos, y todos los más viejos y dolientes acordaron de enviar a la villa a un clérigo que se decía Guevara, que tenía buena expresiva, y a otro hombre de mucha cuenta que se decía Amaya, pariente del Diego Velázquez de Cuba, y a un escribano que se decía Vergara, y tres testigos, los nombres de ellos no me acuerdo — a quienes envió para que notificasen a Gonzalo de Sandoval que luego se diese a Narváez. Para ello dijeron que traían unos traslados de las provisiones.

Dicen que ya Gonzalo de Sandoval sabía de los navíos por nuevas de indios y de la mucha gente que en ellos venía. Como era muy varón en sus cosas, siempre estaba muy apercibido, él y sus soldados armados, sospechando que aquella armada era de Diego Velázquez y que enviaría a aquella villa de sus gentes para apoderarse de ella. Y por estar más desembarazados de los soldados viejos y dolientes, los envió luego a un pueblo de indios que se dice Papalote, y quedó con los sanos. Sandoval siempre tenía buenas velas en los caminos de Cempoala — que es por donde habían de venir a la villa — y estaba convocando a Sandoval y atrayendo a sus soldados [a que], si viniese Diego Velázquez u otra persona, no se les diese la villa. Y todos los soldados dicen que le respondieron conforme a su voluntad. Y mandó hacer una horca en un cerro.

Estando sus espías en los caminos, vienen de presto y le dan noticia de que vienen cerca de la villa donde estaba seis españoles e indios de Cuba. Sandoval aguardó en su casa y no les salió a recibir; ya había mandado que ningún soldado saliese de su casa ni les hablase. Y como el clérigo y los demás que traían en su compañía no topaban a ningún vecino español con quien hablar, sino indios que hacían la obra de la fortaleza, y no les entendían, cuando entraron en la villa fueron a la iglesia a hacer oración, y luego se fueron a la casa de Sandoval, que les pareció que era la mayor de la villa. El clérigo, después de “en hora buena estéis” — que así dicen que dijo — y Sandoval le respondió que en tal buena hora viniese, dicen que el clérigo Guevara — que así se llamaba — comenzó un razonamiento, diciendo que el señor Diego Velázquez, gobernador de Cuba, había gastado muchos dineros en la armada, y que Cortés y todos los demás que había traído en su compañía le habían sido traidores, y que les venía a notificar que luego fuesen a dar la obediencia al señor Pánfilo de Narváez, que venía por capitán general del Diego Velázquez.

Y como Sandoval oyó aquellas palabras y descomedimiento que el padre Guevara dijo, estaba cargándose de pesar de lo que oía, y le dijo: “Señor padre, muy mal habláis en decir esas palabras de traidores. Aquí somos mejores servidores de su Majestad que no Diego Velázquez. Y porque sois clérigo, nos castigamos conforme a vuestra mala crianza: andad con Dios a México, que allá está Cortés, que es capitán general y justicia mayor de esta Nueva España, y os responderá. Aquí no tenéis más que hablar.”

Entonces el clérigo dijo muy bravoso a su escribano Vergara que luego sacase las provisiones que traía en el seno y las notificase a Sandoval y a los vecinos que con él estaban. Y dijo Sandoval al escribano que no leyese ningunos papeles — que no sabía si eran provisiones u otras escrituras. Y de plática en plática, ya el escribano comenzaba a sacar del seno las escrituras que traía, y Sandoval le dijo: “Mirad, Vergara: ya os he dicho que no leáis ningunos papeles aquí, sino id a México, y os prometo que si tal leyesedes, que yo os haga dar cien azotes, porque ni sabemos si sois escribano del rey o no — mostrad título dello; y si le traéis, leedlo. Ni tampoco sabemos si son originales las provisiones, o traslados, u otros papeles.” Y el clérigo, que era muy soberbio, dijo que hacíais con estos traidores — sacad esas provisiones y notificádselas — y esto dijo con mucho enojo. Y como Sandoval oyó aquella palabra, le dijo que mentía como ruin clérigo, y luego mandó a sus soldados que los llevasen presos a México. Y no lo hubo bien dicho, cuando en hamacas de redes, como ánimas pecadoras, los arrebataron muchos indios de los que trabajaban en la fortaleza, que los llevaron a cuestas; y en cuatro días dan con ellos cerca de México, que de noche y de día, con indios de remuda, caminaban. E iban espantados, desde que vieron tantas ciudades y pueblos grandes, y les traían de comer — unos los tomaban y otros los dejaban y andaban por su camino. Dicen que iban pensando si era encantamiento o sueño. Y Sandoval envió con ellos por alguacil, hasta que los llevase a México, a la puerta de Solís — el hierro que fue de Ordóñez, que ahora llaman Solís, tras de la puerta.

Y así como los envió presos, escribió muy de posta a Cortés, que era el capitán de la armada, todo lo acaecido. Y como Cortés lo supo — que venían presos y llegaban cerca de México — envióles cabalgaduras para los tres más principales y mandó que luego los soltasen de la prisión, y les escribió que le pesó de que Gonzalo de Sandoval tal desacato hubiese hecho, y que quisiera que les hiciera mucha honra. Y desde que llegaron a México les salió a recibir y los metió en la ciudad muy honradamente. Y desde que el clérigo y los demás sus compañeros vieron a México ser tan grandísima ciudad, y la riqueza de oro que teníamos, y otras muchas ciudades en el agua de la laguna, y todos nuestros capitanes y soldados, y la gran franqueza de Cortés, estaban admirados. Y a cabo de dos días que estuvieron con nosotros, Cortés les habló de tal manera, con prometimientos y halagos, y aun les untó las manos de tejuelos y joyas de oro, y los tornó a enviar a su Narváez con bastimento que les dio para el camino — que donde venían muy bravosos leones, volvieron muy mansos y se le ofrecieron por servidores. Y así como llegaron a Cempoala a dar relación a su capitán, comenzaron a convocar todo el real de Narváez a que se pasasen con nosotros.

Y déxalo aquí, y diré cómo Cortés escribió a Narváez y lo que sobrello pasó.</spanish>
<flags>voice: Bernal Díaz del Castillo — blunt, garrulous old soldier, plain and concrete, quietly aggrieved, proud eyewitness (held). ⚠ NO REFERENCE —. "buena espesiva" read as expresiva (gift for speech / buena expresiva) — uncertain colonial spelling; flagged. "nos castigo" (Sandoval to Guevara) rendered as "we punish you" — reflexive/courtesy sense of the original preserved. Colonial spellings modernized throughout (gonçalo→Gonzalo, narvaez→Narváez, yndios→indios, çibdad→ciudad, mxᶜᵒ/mx<sup>co</sup>→México, etc.). "Dizque/dicen que" rendered as "they say." Direct speech in Bernal Díaz's plain register. Closing forward pointer preserved as narrative hinge. Edition footnote and page apparatus omitted. Hamaca/net image and "ánimas pecadoras" doubling kept. Solís forge / Ordóñez ironworks glossed once in English.</flags>

=== CAPITULO CXII—Como Cortes despues — Section 113 ===
<english>CHAPTER CXII — How Cortés, after being well informed as to who the captain was, how many were coming in the fleet, the war supplies they brought, and about three false soldiers of ours who had gone over to Narváez, wrote to the captain and to other friends of his, especially Andrés de Duero, secretary to Diego Velázquez; and also learned how Moctezuma was sending gold and clothing to Narváez, and the words he sent to say to Moctezuma; and how Licenciado Lucas Vázquez de Ayllón, oidor of the Royal Audiencia of Santo Domingo, was coming in that fleet, and the instruction he carried.

As Cortés in all things took great care and watchfulness, and nothing slipped past him that he did not try to remedy, and as many times before and now he had such skilled and good captains and soldiers who, besides being very brave, gave him sound counsel, it was agreed by all that letters should go by express, carried by Indians, to Narváez before Father Guevara arrived — with many courtesies and offers that we all together made him: that we would do whatever his lordship commanded, and we begged him by his lordship not to stir up the country nor let the Indians see divisions among us. This offer was made because we of Cortés were few soldiers compared with those Narváez brought, so that he might bear us good will, and to see what would come of it, and so that we might offer ourselves as his servants; and also, beneath these fair words, we would not fail to seek friends among Narváez's captains, because Father Guevara and the scribe Vergara told Cortés that Narváez was not well liked by his captains, and that he should send them some gold ingots and chains — for gifts break rocks. And Cortés wrote to them that he and all of us his companions were greatly pleased at his arrival at that port, and since they are friends from times past he begs him by his lordship not to cause Moctezuma, who is a prisoner, to be released and the city to rise up, for it will mean the loss of his life and his men's and all of ours, because of the great power Moctezuma has; and he says this because Moctezuma is very agitated and the whole city in revolt, with the words they have sent from there for him to hear, and he believes and holds for certain that from so brave and wise a man as he is, things of such craft could not have come from his mouth, nor at such a time, but that Cervantes the buffoon and the soldiers he had with him would have said them; and besides the other words in the letter, he offered himself with his person and his estate, and that in everything he would do whatever was commanded. Cortés also wrote to Secretary Andrés de Duero and to Oidor Lucas Vázquez de Ayllón, and with the letters sent certain gold jewels for his friends; and after he had sent this letter, he secretly ordered chains and ingots given to the oidor, and begged our father of his lordship that right after the letters he should go to Narváez's camp; and he gave him other chains of gold and ingots and very valuable jewels to give there to his friends. And so, when the first letter we have said Cortés wrote arrived with the Indians before Father Guevara came — he was the one Narváez sent to us — Narváez was going about showing it to his captains, making mock of it and even of us; and a captain of those Narváez brought, who came as inspector and was called Salvatierra, they say was raging from the moment he heard it, and said to Narváez, reproaching him, why he should read the letter of a traitor like Cortés and those with him, and that he should go at once against us and leave not one alive; and he swore that of Cortés's ears he would roast and eat one of them, and said other wild things — so that Narváez would not answer the letter, nor did he hold us in any account. At that moment Father Guevara and his companions arrived, and they told Narváez that Cortés was a very good knight and a great servant of the king, and spoke to him of the great power of Mexico and the many cities they had seen on the way, and that they understood Cortés would serve him and do whatever he commanded, and that it would be well for there to be agreement between the one side and the other, in peace and without uproar; and that Lord Narváez should look to which part of all New Spain he wishes to go with the men he brings — let him go there and leave Cortés in the other provinces, since there are lands enough where they can settle. And when Narváez heard this, they say he grew so angry with Father Guevara and with Amaya that he did not want to see or hear them anymore; and when those in Narváez's camp saw Father Guevara and Scribe Vergara and the others go off so rich, and were told secretly by all of us so much good of Cortés and all of us, and that they had seen such a multitude of gold going about in the camp at play, many of Narváez's men already wished they were in our camp. At that moment our father of his lordship arrived, as I have said, at Narváez's camp with the ingots Cortés gave him and with secret letters, and went to kiss Narváez's hands and tell him that Cortés would do everything he commanded and that they should have peace and love; and Narváez, as he was hard-headed and came very proud, would not hear him — instead he said before the very friar that Cortés and all of us were traitors, and because the friar answered that rather we were very loyal servants of the king, he treated him harshly in words; and very secretly the friar distributed the ingots and gold chains to those Cortés had ordered him to, and summoned and drew to our side the principal men of Narváez's camp. And I leave it here, and will tell what befell Oidor Lucas Vázquez de Ayllón and Narváez, and what passed regarding it.</english>
<spanish>CAPÍTULO CXII — Cómo Cortés, después de estar bien informado de quién era el capitán, quién y cuántos venían en la armada, los pertrechos de guerra que traía y de los tres falsos soldados nuestros que a Narváez se pasaron, escribió al capitán y a otros sus amigos, especialmente a Andrés de Duero, secretario de Diego Velázquez; y supo también cómo Moctezuma enviaba oro y ropa a Narváez y las palabras que le envió a decir a Moctezuma; y de cómo venía en aquella armada el licenciado Lucas Vázquez de Ayllón, oidor de la Real Audiencia de Santo Domingo, y la instrucción que traía.

Como Cortés en todo tenía gran cuidado y advertencia, y cosa ninguna se le pasaba que no procuraba poner remedio, y como muchas veces y dicho antes de ahora tenía tan acertados y buenos capitanes y soldados que, demás de ser muy esforzados, le dábamos buenos consejos, acordóse por todos que se escribiese en posta con indios que llevasen las cartas a Narváez antes que llegase el clérigo Guevara, con muchas cortesías y ofrecimientos que todos a una le hiciésemos: que haríamos lo que su merced mandase y que le pedíamos por merced que no alborotase la tierra ni los indios viesen entre nosotros divisiones; y esto de este ofrecimiento fue por causa de que, como éramos los de Cortés pocos soldados en comparación de los que el Narváez traía, para que nos tuviese buena voluntad y a ver lo que sucedía y nos ofreciésemos por sus servidores; y también debajo de estas buenas palabras no dejásemos de buscar amigos entre los capitanes del Narváez, porque el padre Guevara y el escribano Vergara dijeron a Cortés que Narváez no venía bien quisto con sus capitanes, y que les enviase algunos tejuelos y cadenas de oro — porque dadivas quebrantan peñas. Y Cortés les escribió que se había holgado en gran manera él y todos nosotros sus compañeros con su llegada a aquel puerto, y pues son amigos de tiempos pasados, que le pide por merced que no dé causa a que Moctezuma, que está preso, se suelte y la ciudad se levante, porque será para perderse él y su gente y todos nosotros las vidas por los grandes poderes que tiene; y esto lo dice porque Moctezuma está muy alterado y toda la ciudad revuelta, con las palabras que de allá le han enviado a decir, y que cree y tiene por cierto que de un tan esforzado y sabio varón como él es no habían de salir de su boca cosas de tal arte dichas ni en tal tiempo, sino que el Cervantes el chocarrero y los soldados que llevaba consigo lo dirían; y demás de otras palabras que en la carta iban, se le ofreció con su persona y hacienda y que en todo se haría lo que mandare. Y también escribió Cortés al secretario Andrés de Duero y al oidor Lucas Vázquez de Ayllón, y con las cartas envió ciertas joyas de oro para sus amigos; y después que hubo enviado esta carta, secretamente mandó dar al oidor cadenas y tejuelos, y rogó al padre de la merced que luego tras las cartas fuese al real de Narváez; y le dio otras cadenas de oro y tejuelos y joyas muy estimadas que diese allá a sus amigos. Y así, como llegó la primera carta que dicho hemos que escribió Cortés con los indios antes que llegase el padre Guevara — que fue el que Narváez nos envió —, andaba mostrándola Narváez a sus capitanes, haciendo burla de ella y aun de nosotros; y un capitán de los que traía el Narváez, que venía por veedor y se decía Salvatierra, dicen que hacía bramuras desde que la oyó y decía al Narváez, reprendiéndole, que para qué leyese la carta de un traidor como Cortés y los que con él estaban, y que luego fuese contra nosotros y que no quedase ninguno con vida; y juró que de las orejas de Cortés que había de asar y comer la una de ellas, y decía otras liviandades — por manera que no quiso responder a la carta ni nos tenía en una castañeta. Y en este instante llegó el clérigo Guevara y sus compañeros, y hablaron al Narváez que Cortés era muy buen caballero y gran servidor del rey, y le dicen del gran poder de México y de las muchas ciudades que vieron por donde pasaron, y que entendieron que Cortés le será servidor y hará cuanto mandase, y que será bien que por paz y sin ruido haya entre los unos y los otros concierto, y que mire el señor Narváez a qué parte quiere ir de toda la Nueva España con la gente que trae, que allí vaya y deje a Cortés en otras provincias, pues hay tierras bastas donde se pueden albergar. Y como esto oyó el Narváez, dicen que se enojó de tal manera con el padre Guevara y con el Amaya, que no los quería después más ver ni escuchar; y desde que los del real de Narváez les vieron ir tan ricos al padre Guevara y al escribano Vergara y a los demás, y les decían secretamente a todos los de Narváez tanto bien de Cortés y de todos nosotros y que habían visto tanta multitud de oro que en el real andaba en el juego, muchos de los de Narváez deseaban estar ya en nuestro real. Y en este instante llegó nuestro padre de la merced, como dicho tengo, al real de Narváez con los tejuelos que Cortés le dio y con cartas secretas, y fue a besar las manos de Narváez y a decirle que Cortés hará todo lo que mandare y que tengan paz y amor; y el Narváez, como era cabezudo y venía muy pujante, no le quiso oír; antes dijo delante del mismo padre que Cortés y todos nosotros éramos unos traidores, y porque el fraile respondía que antes éramos muy leales servidores del rey, le trató mal de palabra; y muy secretamente repartió el fraile los tejuelos y cadenas de oro a quien Cortés le mandó, y convocaba y atraía a los más principales del real de Narváez. Y déjalo aquí, y diré lo que al oidor Lucas Vázquez de Ayllón y el Narváez les aconteció, y lo que sobre ello pasó.</spanish>
<flags>voice: Bernal Díaz del Castillo — blunt, garrulous old soldier, plain and concrete, quietly aggrieved, proud eyewitness (held). ⚠ NO REFERENCE —. Opening "OMO" read as "Como" (chapter-body incipit; likely scribal/typographic error). Colonial spellings modernized throughout (ynformado→informado, espeçialmente→especialmente, çibdad→ciudad, montezuma/montezuma→Moctezuma, narvaez/narbaez→Narváez, yndios→indios, conparaçion→comparación, petrechos→pertrechos, etc.). Edition page numbers, "Digitized by Google" boilerplate, and closing folio marker omitted. Proverb "dadivas quebrantan peñas" kept literally. "en una castañeta" kept as contempt/trivialization image. "concierto" rendered as agreement/good order. "liviandades" as wild/vulgar talk. Closing forward pointer preserved as narrative hinge.</flags>

=== CAPITULO CXIII. Como ovieron palabras — Section 114 ===
<english>CHAPTER CXIII. How Captain Pánfilo de Narváez and the royal judge Lucas Vázquez de Ayllón had words, and Narváez had him arrested and sent aboard a ship as a prisoner to Cuba or to Castile, and what came of it.

It seems that as the judge Lucas Vázquez de Ayllón came to favor the cause of Cortés and all of us — because the Royal Audiencia of Santo Domingo and the Jeronymite friars who were governing there had so ordered him, knowing the many good and loyal services we were rendering first to God and to our king and lord, and the great present we were sending to Castile with our procurators, and besides what the Royal Audiencia had commanded him — when the judge saw Cortés’s letters and with them gold ingots, if before he had said that the fleet Narváez was sending was unjust and against all justice, and that it was wrong to come against servants of the king as loyal as we were, from then on he said it much more plainly and openly, and he spoke so well of Cortés and of all who were with him that in Narváez’s camp nothing else was talked about. And besides this, as they saw and knew Narváez for the utter miser he was — and the gold and clothing that Moctezuma sent them he kept it all and gave none of it to any captain or soldier, but would say in a voice that rang very pompous, half in foolishness, to his steward, “See that not a single blanket is missing, for they are all entered in the account” — and as they knew this of him, and heard what I have said of Cortés and those of us with him speaking so freely, his whole camp was half in an uproar, and Narváez took it into his head that the judge was mixed up in it and sowing discord. And besides this, when Moctezuma sent them provisions, which Narváez’s quartermaster or steward distributed, he kept no account with the judge or with his servants, as was right, and on top of that there were certain frictions and noise in the camp; and also from the counsel given Narváez by Salvatierra, whom I have mentioned, who had come as inspector, and a Juan Bono de Cuexo, a Basque; and above all, the great favor Narváez enjoyed in Castile from Don Juan Rodríguez de Fonseca, bishop of Burgos and archbishop of Rosano — Narváez had such audacity that he arrested the king’s judge, and sent him prisoner, him and certain of his servants and his notary, and had them embarked on a ship and sent them to Castile, or to the island of Cuba, under a gentleman named Gonzalo de Olanca [possibly from Huelva], who was a man of letters, because he said that Cortés was a very loyal servant of the king, and that all of us who were with him were worthy of many favors from His Majesty, and that it seemed wrong to call us traitors, and that it was wrong to arrest a judge of His Majesty; and for saying this he ordered him thrown into prison. And as Gonzalo de Olanca was a very noble man, he died of rage within four days. He also ordered two other soldiers thrown into prison, whom he had on his ship, knowing they spoke well of Cortés; among them was a Sancho de Barahona, who had once been in Guatemala.

Let us return to the judge they were taking prisoner to Castile. With fair words and with the fears he put into the captain and the pilot and master who had charge of carrying him on the ship — that once they reached Castile, His Majesty, instead of paying them for what they did, would order them hanged — when they heard those words, they told him to pay them for their trouble and they would take him to Santo Domingo; and so they changed the course Narváez had ordered them, and when they reached the island of Santo Domingo and disembarked, as soon as the Royal Audiencia residing there and the Jeronymite friars who were governing heard the licentiate Lucas Vázquez de Ayllón, and saw such great disrespect and audacity, they felt it deeply, and with such anger that they at once wrote to Castile, to the Royal Council of His Majesty. And as the bishop of Burgos was president and commanded everything, and His Majesty had not yet returned from Flanders, there was no room to do anything just in our favor; rather Don Juan Rodríguez de Fonseca, they say, was very pleased, believing that Narváez had already undone us. And when His Majesty, who was in Flanders, heard our procurators, and what Diego Velázquez and Narváez had done in sending the fleet without his royal license, and in having arrested his judge, it did them great harm in the lawsuits and claims they afterward brought against Cortés, and against all of us, as I shall tell further on — though they said they had license from the bishop of Burgos, who was president, to send the fleet against us. Then, as certain soldiers who were kin and friends of the judge Lucas Vázquez de Ayllón saw the great disrespect and madness Narváez had committed against the king’s judge, whom he had taken prisoner, and feared Narváez, who already had them in his sights and was ill-disposed toward them, they agreed to flee from the sandy camp to the town where Captain Sandoval was, and he paid them great honor, and learned from them everything I have here said, and that Narváez wanted to send soldiers to that town to arrest him. And what else happened I shall tell further on.</english>
<spanish>CAPÍTULO CXIII. Cómo tuvieron palabras el capitán Pánfilo de Narváez y el oidor Lucas Vázquez de Ayllón, y Narváez le mandó prender y le envió en un navío preso a Cuba o a Castilla, y lo que sobre ello aconteció.

Parece ser que, como el oidor Lucas Vázquez de Ayllón venía a favorecer las cosas de Cortés y de todos nosotros — porque así se lo habían mandado la Real Audiencia de Santo Domingo y los frailes jerónimos que estaban por gobernadores, como sabían los muchos y buenos y leales servicios que hacíamos a Dios primeramente y a nuestro rey y señor, y del gran presente que enviábamos a Castilla con nuestros procuradores, y demás de lo que la Audiencia Real le mandó —, como el oidor vio las cartas de Cortés y con ellas tejuelos de oro, si de antes decía que aquella armada que enviaba era injusta y contra toda justicia, que a tan buenos servidores del rey como éramos era mal hecho venir, de allí adelante lo decía mucho más claro y abiertamente, y decía tanto bien de Cortés y de todos los que con él estábamos, que ya en el real de Narváez no se hablaba de otra cosa. Y demás de esto, como veían y conocían en Narváez la pura miseria, y el oro y ropa que Moctezuma les enviaba todo se lo guardaba y no daba cosa de ello a ningún capitán ni soldado, antes decía con voz que hablaba muy entonada, medio de bobedad, a su mayordomo: «Mira que no falte ninguna manta, porque todas están puestas por memoria» — y como aquello conocían de él, y oían lo que dicho tengo de Cortés y los que con él estábamos de muy francos, todo su real estaba medio alborotado, y tuvo pensamiento Narváez de que el oidor entendía en ello y de poner cizaña. Y demás de esto, cuando Moctezuma les enviaba bastimento, que repartía el despensero o mayordomo de Narváez, no tenía cuenta con el oidor ni con sus criados, como era razón, y sobre ello hubo ciertas cosquillas y ruido en el real; y también por consejo que daban a Narváez Salvatierra, que dicho tengo, que venía por veedor, y un Juan Bono de Cuexo, vizcaíno; y sobre todo, los grandes favores que tenía Narváez de Castilla de don Juan Rodríguez de Fonseca, obispo de Burgos y arzobispo de Rosano — tuvo tal atrevimiento Narváez que prendió al oidor del rey, y le envió preso a él y a ciertos sus criados y a su escribano, y los hizo embarcar en un navío y los envió a Castilla, o a la isla de Cuba, y a un hidalgo que se decía hulano de Olanca, y era letrado, porque decía que Cortés era muy servidor del rey, y todos nosotros los que estábamos con él éramos dignos de muchas mercedes, y que parecía mal llamarnos traidores, y que era mal hecho prender a un oidor de su Majestad; y por esto que le dijo le mandó echar preso. Y como Gonzalo de Olanca era muy noble, del enojo murió dentro de cuatro días. También mandó echar presos a otros dos soldados que traía en su navío, que sabía que hablaban bien de Cortés; entre ellos fue un Sancho de Barahona, vez que fue de Guatemala.

Tornemos a decir del oidor que llevaban preso a Castilla, que con palabras buenas y con temores que puso al capitán y al piloto y maestre que le llevaban a cargo en el navío — que llegados a Castilla, su Majestad en lugar de paga de lo que hacen, les mandaría ahorcar —, y desde que aquellas palabras oyeron, le dijeron que les pagase su trabajo y les llevarían a Santo Domingo; y así mudaron la derrota que les había mandado Narváez. Y llegados a la isla de Santo Domingo y desembarcado, desde que la Audiencia Real que allí residía y los frailes jerónimos que estaban por gobernadores oyeron al licenciado Lucas Vázquez de Ayllón, y vieron tan gran desacato y atrevimiento, lo sintieron mucho, y con tanto enojo que luego lo escribieron a Castilla, al Real Consejo de su Majestad. Y como el obispo de Burgos era presidente y lo mandaba todo, y su Majestad no había venido de Flandes, no hubo lugar de hacer cosa ninguna justa en nuestro favor; antes don Juan Rodríguez de Fonseca, dicen que se holgó mucho, creyendo que Narváez nos había ya desbaratado. Y cuando su Majestad, que estaba en Flandes, oyó a nuestros procuradores, y lo que Diego Velázquez y Narváez habían hecho en enviar la armada sin su real licencia, y en haber prendido a su oidor, les hizo harto daño en los pleitos y demandas que después, cuando acusaron a Cortés, les pusieron, y a todos nosotros, como adelante diré — por más que decían que tenían licencia del obispo de Burgos, que era presidente, para hacer la armada que contra nosotros enviaron. Pues, como ciertos soldados deudos y amigos del oidor Lucas Vázquez de Ayllón vieron que Narváez había hecho aquel gran desacato y desatino contra el oidor de su Majestad, que había llevado preso, y temerosos de Narváez, que les traía ya sobre los ojos y estaba mal con ellos, acordaron de huirse de los arenales a la villa, donde estaba el capitán Sandoval, y les hizo mucha honra, y supo de ellos todo lo aquí por mí dicho, y que quería enviar Narváez a aquella villa soldados a prenderle. Y lo que más pasó diré adelante.</spanish>
<flags>voice: Bernal Díaz del Castillo — blunt, garrulous old soldier, plain and concrete, quietly aggrieved, proud eyewitness (held). ⚠ NO REFERENCE —. Colonial spellings modernized throughout (pareçe→parece, oydor→oidor, faboresçer→favorecer, ynjusta→injusta, conoçian→conocían, montezuma→Moctezuma, çizaña→cizaña, fabores→favores, arçobispo→arzobispo, paresçia→parecía, mds→mercedes, dizque→dicen que, devdos→deudos, honrra→honra, etc.). Edition page numbers, "Digitized by Google" boilerplate, and folio markers omitted. "hulano de oblanca" normalized to Gonzalo de Olanca per same-sentence identification; "hulano" [possibly from Huelva] glossed once in English — term uncertain, may be regional/nickname. "pura miseria" kept as stinginess image. "con voz... medio de bobedad" kept as author's contemptuous portrait. "puestas por memoria" as accounted on the books. Closing forward pointer preserved.</flags>

=== CAPITULO CXIIII como narvaez despues — Section 115 ===
<english>CHAPTER CXIV — How, after Narváez had imprisoned the oidor Lucas Vázquez de Ayllón and his scribe, he went with the whole fleet to a town called Cempoal, which at that season was a large place; and what was agreed upon, and what our Cortés and all of us did while we were in Mexico, and how we resolved to march against Narváez.

After Narváez had already sent the oidor of the Royal Audiencia of Santo Domingo to prison, he immediately set about leaving with all his baggage, munitions, and war supplies to establish a royal camp in a town that at that season was very populous, called Cempoal. The first thing he did was forcibly take from the Fat Cacique — that is what he is called — all the mantas, clothing, and gold that Cortés had given him to guard before we set out for Tlaxcala. He also took from him the Indian women whom the caciques of that town had given — women we had left in their fathers’ houses because they were daughters of lords and, for going to war, very delicately kept. When this was done, the Fat Cacique told Narváez many times not to take anything at all of what Cortés had left in his keeping, for if he knew they were taking it from him he would die for it; and he even complained to Narváez himself of the many harms and robberies his men were doing in that town. They told him that when Malinche [Cortés] — for that is what they called Cortés — and his men were there, they took nothing from them, and that he was very good and upright, he and all the captains he brought with him, and that he should give back his Indian women and gold and mantas at once; otherwise he would send to complain to Malinche. And when they heard that, they mocked what he said — and Salvatierra the drunkard, whom I have named before, who was the one who talked the most bravado, said to some of his friends and to Narváez himself: “Do you not hear what fear all these caciques have of this nobody Cortés?” I say: look how much it is worth not to speak ill of what is good — I speak the truth — for when we fell upon Narváez, one of the greatest cowards was Salvatierra, as I shall tell farther on; and not because he lacked a stout body and strength, but because he was foul-tempered and not bold of tongue. They said he was a native of a town beyond Burgos. Let us leave off speaking of him, and tell how Narváez sent to summon our captain and all of us with certain provisions that they said were copies of the originals he carried, by which he was to be captain under Governor Diego Velázquez; which he sent so that they might be read out to us by a scribe named Gonzalo de Mata, who afterward became a crossbowman and, as time went on, a resident of Puebla. He sent with him four soldiers, men of very good standing, to serve as witnesses. And leaving Narváez and the scribe he sent there as I have said, we return to Cortés — who, as each day he had letters and reports both from those in Narváez’s camp and from Captain Gonzalo de Sandoval, who remained in the town of Veracruz, and who let him know that he had with him there five soldiers, men of the highest rank, kinsmen and friends of Licenciado Lucas Vázquez de Ayllón, whom Narváez had sent to prison, who had come over to him from Narváez’s camp; and the reason they gave for coming was that, since Narváez had shown no respect to a royal oidor, he would show even less to them, who were his kinsmen — of which soldiers Sandoval learned at great length everything that was passing in Narváez’s camp, who said he was to march in search of us at Mexico to take us prisoner. Let us go forward and say that Cortés immediately took counsel with our captains, and with all of us who he knew would be very loyal servants to him — for he was accustomed to call council in cases of such weight as these — and by all it was agreed that we should march against Narváez at once, without waiting for more letters or other reasons; and that Pedro de Alvarado should remain in Mexico to guard Moctezuma, with all the soldiers who were not fit to make that march; also so that the persons we suspected of being friends of Diego Velázquez might remain there.
