=== CAPITULO CXXIII. como despues de des- — Section 124 ===
<english>CHAPTER CXXIII. How, after Narváez had been routed a second time, the Indians of Chinanta whom Cortés had sent for came—and other things that happened

As I already said in the chapter that speaks of it, Cortés sent word to the peoples of Chinanta—where they had brought the lances and pikes—that two thousand of their Indians should come with their lances, which are much longer than ours, to help us. They came that same day, somewhat late, after Narváez had been taken prisoner. The caciques of those same towns came as captains, and with them one of our soldiers named Barrientos, who had remained in Chinanta for that purpose.

They entered Cempoal in great order, two by two. They carried very large lances of good thickness, with a full arm-span of flint blade set in them—blades that cut as well as knives, as I have already said on other occasions. Each Indian carried a buckler like a small shield, with banners spread, many plumes, drums and little trumpets; and between each lancer and lancer went an archer. Shouting and whistling, they cried, “Long live the king! Long live our lord the king and Hernando Cortés in his royal name!” They entered very boldly—a thing worth noting—and though they were about fifteen hundred, from the manner and good order in which they came it looked as though they were three thousand. When Narváez’s men saw them, they stood astonished, and he says they said to one another that if that people had taken them in the middle or had entered with us, what would have become of them.

Cortés spoke to the Indian captains very warmly, thanking them for their coming, and gave them coins of Castile; and he ordered them to return at once to their towns, and that along the road they should do no harm to other towns, and he sent back with them the same Barrientos. And here I stay, to tell what else Cortés did.</english>
<spanish>CAPÍTULO CXXIII. Cómo, después de desbaratado Narváez por segunda vez, vinieron los indios de Chinanta a quienes Cortés había mandado llamar, y de otras cosas que pasaron

Ya dije en el capítulo que dello habla que Cortés envió a decir a los pueblos de Chinanta, donde trajeron las lanzas y picas, que viniesen dos mil indios de ellos con sus lanzas, que son mucho más largas que las nuestras, para ayudarnos; y vinieron aquel mismo día, ya algo tarde, después de preso Narváez. Venían por capitanes los caciques de los mismos pueblos, y uno de nuestros soldados, que se decía Barrientos, que había quedado en Chinanta para aquel efecto; y entraron en Cempoal con gran ordenanza, de dos en dos. Y como traían las lanzas muy grandes, de buen grosor, y tienen en ellas una braza de cuchilla de pedernales, que cortan tanto como navajas—según ya otras veces dije—, y traía cada indio una rodela como pavesina, y con sus banderas tendidas, y con muchos plumajes y atambores y trompetillas, y entre cada lancero y lancero un flechero; y dando gritos y silbos decían: «¡Viva el rey, viva el rey nuestro señor y Hernando Cortés en su real nombre!» Y entraron muy bravosos, que era cosa de notar, y serían mil quinientos, que parecía, de la manera y concierto que venían, que eran tres mil; y cuando los de Narváez los vieron, se admiraron, y dice que dijeron unos a otros que si aquella gente les tomara en medio o entraran con nosotros, qué tal les parara.

Cortés habló a los indios capitanes muy amorosamente, agradeciéndoles su venida, y les dio cuentas DE Castilla, y les mandó que luego se volviesen a sus pueblos, y que por el camino no hiciesen daño a otros pueblos; y tornó a enviar con ellos el mismo Barrientos. Y quedaré aquí, y diré lo que más Cortés hizo.</spanish>
<flags>voice: Bernal Díaz del Castillo — blunt, garrulous old soldier, plain and concrete, quietly aggrieved, proud eyewitness (held). ⚠ NO REFERENCE —. Colonial spellings modernized throughout (çenpoal→Cempoal, lanças→lanzas, conçierto→concierto, narbaez/narvaez→Narváez, truxeron→trajeron, paresçio→parecía, dezian→decían, agradesçiendoles→agradeciéndoles, bolviesen→volviesen, hiziesen→hiciesen, dezia→decía, avia→había, atanbores→atambores, tronpetillas→trompetillas, vanderas→banderas, yndios→indios, etc.). Edition page numbers, folio markers, “Digitized by Google” boilerplate omitted. “quedarse A aqui e dire” read as quedaré aquí, y diré (abbreviated first person). Author’s emphatic capitals preserved in Spanish (DE Castilla). “real nombre” rendered as royal name in English; kept as real nombre in Spanish. Closing forward pointer preserved. “braza de cuchilla de pedernales” kept as concrete image (arm-span flint blade). Bernal’s “diz que dixeron” reporting formula preserved.</flags>

=== CAPITULO CXXIIII. Como Cortes Enbio — Section 125 ===
<english>CHAPTER CXXIV. How Cortés sent to the port Captain Francisco de Lugo, and with him in his company two soldiers who had been ship masters, so that they would at once bring there to Cempoal all the masters and pilots of Narváez's ships and fleet, and strip from them the sails, tillers, and compasses, so they would not go to give word to the island of Cuba to Diego Velázquez of what had happened; and how he made an admiral of the sea, and other things that took place.

Once Narváez had been thoroughly broken and he and his captains taken prisoner, and the arms of all the rest seized,

Cortés ordered Captain Francisco de Lugo to go to the port where Narváez's fleet lay — eighteen ships — and to have all the pilots and ship masters come there to Cempoal, and to strip from them sails, tillers, and compasses, so they would not go to give word to Cuba to Diego Velázquez; and that if they would not obey him, he was to take them prisoner. Francisco de Lugo took with him two of our soldiers who had been men of the sea, to help him. Cortés also ordered that they send at once to him a Sancho de Barahona, whom Narváez had held prisoner, with two other soldiers. This Barahona was a rich man from Guatemala; I remember that when he came before Cortés he was very sick and thin, and Cortés ordered that honor be done him.

Let us return to the ship masters and pilots, who soon came to kiss Captain Cortés's hands; he took oath from them that they would not leave his command and that they would obey him in everything he ordered them. He then made admiral and captain of the sea a Pedro Cavallero, who had been master of one of Narváez's ships, a man in whom our Cortés placed great trust — they say he first gave him good gold tiles — and ordered him not to let any ship leave that port for any destination. He commanded all the other masters and pilots and mariners to obey him, and that if Diego Velázquez sent more ships from Cuba — for he had word that two ships were coming — he was to find a way and keep watch, and that to whatever captain came in them he was to take prisoner and strip tiller, sails, and compasses, until Cortés ordered otherwise; which Pedro Cavallero did, as I shall tell farther on.

Let us leave the ships and the port secure, and say what was agreed in our royal camp and with Narváez's men: order was given at once that Juan Velázquez de León should go to conquer and settle Panuco; for this Cortés assigned him one hundred and twenty soldiers — one hundred were to be from Narváez's men and twenty from ours — mixed in, because they had more experience in war. He was also to take two ships, so that from the river of Panuco they might go to discover the coast ahead. Cortés likewise gave Diego de Ordás another captaincy of another one hundred and twenty soldiers, to go settle at Guazacualco — one hundred from Narváez's men and twenty from ours, in the same way and manner as Juan Velázquez de León — and he was to take two other ships, so that from the river of Guazacualco they might send to the island of Jamaica for herds of mares and steers, and pigs, and sheep and Castilian fowl, and goats, to multiply the land, because the province of Guazacualco was good for it.

So that those captains might go with their soldiers and carry all their arms, Cortés ordered that all the prisoners be given and released — the captains of Narváez, except Narváez himself and Salvatierra, who said he was ill in the stomach. But to give them all their arms — some of our soldiers had already taken horses and swords and other things from them — Cortés ordered that we return them at once; and over not giving them back there were certain angry talks, and we soldiers who had them said very plainly that we did not want to give them back, since in Narváez's royal camp they had proclaimed war against us, and free booty, and with that intention they had come to seize us and take what we had; and that we, who were such great servants of His Majesty, they called traitors; and that we would not give the arms back. Cortés still insisted that we give them; and as he was captain general, it had to be done as he ordered. I gave back one horse I had already hidden, saddled and bridled, and two swords and three daggers and one dagger-knife; and many of our other soldiers likewise gave back other horses and arms.

Since Alonso de Ávila was a captain and a man who dared tell Cortés things that were fitting, he and the father of the Madonna spoke apart to Cortés and told him that it seemed he wanted to remedy Alexander of Macedon — who, after he and his soldiers had done some great deed, strove more to honor and do more for those he conquered than for his own captains and soldiers who were the ones who conquered; and they said this because of what they saw in those days while we were there, after Narváez was prisoner: that all the gold jewels the Indians presented to Cortés, and provisions, he gave to Narváez's captains; and that as if he did not know us, so he forgot us; and that it was not well done but very great ingratitude, having put him in the state he was in.

To this Cortés answered that everything he had, persons as well as goods, was for us; and that at present he could do no more than give Narváez's men gifts, and words, and offers, because as they are many and we few, they might rise against him and against us and kill him. To this Alonso de Ávila answered and spoke certain words somewhat haughty, in such a way that Cortés told him that whoever would not follow him, women in Castile had borne or would bear soldiers; and Alonso de Ávila said, with words very haughty and without respect, that it was indeed true — soldiers, and captains, and governors — and that we deserved what he said. And as at that season things stood so that Cortés could do nothing else, he held his peace; and with gifts and offers he drew him to himself. And knowing how very bold he was, Cortés always feared that perhaps one day or another he might do something to his harm; he dissembled, and from then on always sent him on matters of importance, as to the island of Santo Domingo, and afterward to Spain when we sent the chamber and treasure of the great Moctezuma — which Joan Florin, a great French corsair, robbed, as I shall tell in its time and place.

Let us return now to Narváez and to a black man who brought smallpox — black enough for New Spain, for he was the cause that the whole land caught and swelled with it, of which there was great mortality; for the Indians, they said, had never had such a sickness, and because they did not know it they bathed themselves many times, and for this cause a great number of them died — so black was Narváez's fortune, and blacker still the death of so many people who were not Christians.

Let us leave all this now, and say how the settlers of the rich town who had remained settled there — who had not gone to Mexico — demanded from Cortés the shares of gold that fell to them, and told Cortés that although he had ordered them to remain in that port and town, they would serve God and the king there as well as we who went to Mexico, since they understood guarding the land and building the fortress; and some of them had been in the affair at Almería, who still did not have their wounds healed; and that most of them had been in Narváez's imprisonment; and that they should be given their shares. And Cortés, seeing that what they said was very just, said that two principal men, settlers of that town, with power from all, should go, and that he had it set apart and would give it to them; and it seems to me he told them it was kept in Tlaxcala — this I do not remember well — and so at once two settlers from that town were dispatched for the gold and shares; the principal was called Juan de Alcantara, the elder. Let us stop talking of it; afterward we shall tell what befell Alcantara and the gold.

And let us say how adverse fortune turns her wheel quickly: to great fair winds and pleasures she gives sorrow — and it is that at this instant news come that Mexico is risen, and that Pedro de Alvarado is besieged in his fortress and lodging, and that they were setting fire to the same fortress in two places, and that they had killed seven soldiers, and that many others were wounded; and he was sending to ask for relief with great urgency and haste. This news two Tlaxcalans brought, without any letter; and then a letter came with other Tlaxcalans that Pedro de Alvarado sent, in which he said the same. And after we heard that very bad news, God knows how it grieved us; and at great marches we began to march for Mexico. Narváez and Salvatierra remained prisoner in the rich town; and as lieutenant and captain, it seems to me, Rodrigo Rangel remained, to have charge of guarding Narváez and of gathering many of Narváez's men who were sick.

And at this same instant, just when we wanted to depart, four great lords whom the great Moctezuma sent came before Cortés to complain of Pedro de Alvarado; and what they said, weeping many tears from their eyes, was that Pedro de Alvarado came out of his lodging with all the soldiers Cortés had left him, and without any cause fell upon their lords and chiefs who were dancing and holding festival to their idols Huitzilopochtli and Tezcatlipoca, with license that Pedro de Alvarado had given them for it; and that he killed and wounded many of them; and that in defending themselves they killed six of his soldiers — so that they gave many complaints of Pedro de Alvarado. Cortés answered the messengers somewhat roughly, and that he would go to Mexico and put remedy in everything; and so they went with that answer to their great Moctezuma. They say he took it very ill, and was angered by it. Likewise Cortés at once dispatched letters to Pedro de Alvarado, in which he sent to tell him to see that Moctezuma did not get loose, and that we were coming at great marches, and let him know of the victory we had won against Narváez — which the great Moctezuma already knew.

I shall remain here, and tell what passed farther on.</english>
<spanish>CAPÍTULO CXXIV. Cómo Cortés envió al puerto al capitán Francisco de Lugo y en su compañía dos soldados que habían sido maestros de navíos, para que luego trajesen allí a Cempoal a todos los maestres y pilotos de los navíos y flota de Narváez, y que les sacasen las velas y timones y agujas, porque no fuesen a dar mandado a la isla de Cuba a Diego Velázquez de lo acaecido; y cómo puso almirante de la mar, y otras cosas que pasaron.

Pues acabado de desbaratar a Pánfilo de Narváez, y preso él y sus capitanes, y a todos los demás tomadas las armas,

mandó Cortés al capitán Francisco de Lugo que fuese al puerto adonde estaba la flota de Narváez, que eran diez y ocho navíos, y que mandase venir allí a Cempoal a todos los pilotos y maestres de los navíos, y que les sacasen velas y timones y agujas, porque no fuesen a dar mandado a Cuba a Diego Velázquez; y que si no le quisiesen obedecer, que les echase presos. Llevó consigo Francisco de Lugo dos de nuestros soldados, que habían sido hombres de la mar, para que le ayudasen. También mandó Cortés que luego le enviasen a un Sancho de Barahona, que le tenía preso Narváez, con otros dos soldados. Este Barahona fue vecino de Guatemala, hombre rico; y acuérdome que cuando llegó ante Cortés venía muy doliente y flaco, y le mandó hacer honra.

Volvamos a los maestres y pilotos, que luego vinieron a besar las manos al capitán Cortés; a los cuales tomó juramento de que no saldrían de su mandado y que le obedecerían en todo lo que les mandase. Luego les puso por almirante y capitán de la mar a un Pedro Cavallero, que había sido maestro de un navío de los de Narváez, persona de quien nuestro Cortés se fió mucho — al cual dicen que le dio primero buenos tejuelos de oro —, y mandó que no dejase ir de aquel puerto ningún navío a parte ninguna. Mandó a todos los demás maestres y pilotos y marineros que todos le obedeciesen; y que si de Cuba enviase Diego Velázquez más navíos — porque tuvo aviso de que estaban dos navíos para venir —, que tuviese manera y aviso, y que al capitán que en ellos viniese le echase preso y le sacase el timón y velas y agujas, hasta que otra cosa en ello Cortés mandase; lo cual así hizo Pedro Cavallero, como adelante diré.

Y dejemos ya los navíos y el puerto seguro, y digamos lo que se conciertó en nuestro real y los de Narváez: luego se dio orden de que fuese a conquistar y poblar Juan Velázquez de León a lo de Pánuco; y para ello Cortés le señaló ciento y veinte soldados — los ciento habían de ser de los de Narváez y los veinte de los nuestros —, entremetidos, porque tenían más experiencia en la guerra. También había de llevar dos navíos, para que desde el río de Pánuco fuesen a descubrir la costa adelante. También a Diego de Ordás dio otra capitanía de otros ciento y veinte soldados, para ir a poblar a lo de Guazacualco; y los ciento habían de ser de los de Narváez y los veinte de los nuestros, según y de la manera que a Juan Velázquez de León; y había de llevar otros dos navíos, para que desde el río de Guazacualco enviaran a la isla de Jamaica por ganados de yeguas y becerros, y puercos, y ovejas y gallinas de Castilla, y cabras, para multiplicar la tierra, porque la provincia de Guazacualco era buena para ello.

Pues para ir aquellos capitanes con sus soldados y llevar todas sus armas, Cortés mandó dar y soltar a todos los prisioneros, capitanes de Narváez, excepto a Narváez y a Salvatierra, que decía que estaba malo del estómago. Pero para darles todas las armas — algunos de nuestros soldados les teníamos ya tomado caballos y espadas y otras cosas —, manda Cortés que luego se las volviésemos; y sobre no dárselas hubo ciertas pláticas enojosas, y fueron que dijimos los soldados que las teníamos muy claramente que no se las queríamos dar, pues que en el real de Narváez pregonaron guerra contra nosotros, y a ropa franca, y con aquella intención nos venían a prender y tomar lo que teníamos; y que, siendo nosotros tan grandes servidores de su Majestad, nos llamaban traidores; y que no se las queríamos dar. Y Cortés todavía porfiaba a que se las diésemos; y como era capitán general, hubo de hacerse lo que mandó: que yo les di un caballo que tenía ya escondido, ensillado y enfrenado, y dos espadas y tres puñales y una daga; y otros muchos de nuestros soldados dieron también otros caballos y armas.

Y como Alonso de Ávila era capitán y persona que osaba decir a Cortés cosas que convenían, juntamente con él el padre de la Madre hablaron aparte a Cortés y le dijeron que parecía que quería remediar a Alejandro Macedonio, que después de que con sus soldados había hecho alguna gran hazaña, más procuraba de honrar y hacer más a los que vencía que no a sus capitanes y soldados que eran los que lo vencían; y esto que lo decían porque lo que veían en aquellos días, que allí estábamos, después de preso Narváez: que todas las joyas de oro que le presentaban los indios a Cortés, y bastimentos, daba a los capitanes de Narváez; y que, como si no nos conociera, así nos olvidaba; y que no era bien hecho, sino muy gran ingratitud, habiéndole puesto en el estado en que estaba.

A esto respondió Cortés que todo cuanto tenía, así persona como bienes, era para nosotros; y que al presente no podía más, sino con dádivas y palabras y ofrecimiento dar a los de Narváez, porque como son muchos y nosotros pocos, no se levanten contra él y contra nosotros y le maten. A esto respondió Alonso de Ávila y le dijo ciertas palabras algo soberbias, de tal manera que Cortés le dijo que quien no le quisiese seguir, que las mujeres han parido o paren en Castilla soldados; y Alonso de Ávila dijo, con palabras muy soberbias y sin acato, que así era verdad — soldados, y capitanes, y gobernadores —, y que aquello merecíamos que dijese. Y como en aquella sazón estaba la cosa de arte que Cortés no podía hacer otra cosa sino callar, y con dádivas y ofertas le atrajo a sí; y como conoció del ser muy atrevido, y tuvo siempre Cortés temor de que por ventura un día u otro no hiciese alguna cosa en su daño, disimuló; y desde allí adelante siempre le enviaba a negocios de importancia, como fue a la isla de Santo Domingo, y después a España, cuando enviamos la recámara y tesoro del gran Moctezuma — que robó Joan Florín, gran corsario francés, lo cual diré en su tiempo y lugar.

Y volvamos ahora a Narváez y a un negro que traía lleno de viruela — harto negro fue para la Nueva España, que fue causa de que se pegase y hinchiese toda la tierra de ellas —, de lo cual hubo gran mortandad; que, según decían los indios, jamás tal enfermedad tuvieron, y como no lo conocían, se lavaban muchas veces, y a esta causa se murieron gran cantidad de ellos; por manera que negra la ventura del Narváez, y más prieta la muerte de tanta gente sin ser cristianos.

Dejemos ahora todo esto, y digamos cómo los vecinos de la villa rica que habían quedado poblados — que no fueron a México — demandaron a Cortés las partes del oro que les cabía, y dijeron a Cortés que, puesto que allí les mandó quedar en aquel puerto y villa, que también servirían allí a Dios y al rey como los que fuimos a México, pues entendían en guardar la tierra y hacer la fortaleza; y algunos de ellos se hallaron en la de Almería, que aún no tenían sanas las heridas; y que todos los más se hallaron en la prisión de Narváez; y que les diesen sus partes. Y viendo Cortés que era muy justo lo que decían, dijo que fuesen dos hombres principales, vecinos de aquella villa, con poder de todos, y que lo tenía apartado y se lo darían; y me parece que les dijo que en Tlaxcala estaba guardado — esto no me acuerdo bien —; y así luego despacharon de aquella villa dos vecinos por el oro y partes; el principal se decía Juan de Alcantara, el viejo. Y dejemos de platicar en ello; y después diremos lo que sucedió al Alcantara y al oro.

Y digamos cómo la adversa fortuna vuelve de presto su rueda: que a grandes bonanzas y placeres da tristeza; y es que en este instante vienen nuevas de que México está alzado, y que Pedro de Alvarado está cercado en su fortaleza y aposento, y que le ponían fuego por dos partes en la misma fortaleza, y que le han muerto siete soldados, y que estaban otros muchos heridos; y enviaba a demandar socorro con mucha instancia y prisa. Y esta nueva trajeron dos tascaltecas sin carta ninguna; y luego vino una carta con otros tascaltecas que envió Pedro de Alvarado, en que decía lo mismo. Y desde que aquella tan mala nueva oímos, sabe Dios cuánto nos pesó; y a grandes jornadas comenzamos a marchar para México. Y quedó preso en la villa rica Narváez y Salvatierra; y por teniente y capitán, me parece, quedó Rodrigo Rangel, que tuviese cargo de guardar a Narváez y de recoger muchos de los de Narváez que estaban dolientes.

Y también en este instante, ya que queríamos partir, vinieron cuatro grandes principales que envió el gran Moctezuma ante Cortés, a quejarse del Pedro de Alvarado; y lo que dijeron, llorando muchas lágrimas de sus ojos, fue que Pedro de Alvarado salió de su aposento con todos los soldados que le dejó Cortés, y sin causa ninguna dio en sus principales y caciques que estaban bailando y haciendo fiesta a sus ídolos Huitzilopochtli y Tezcatlipoca, con licencia que para ello les dio Pedro de Alvarado; y que mató e hirió muchos de ellos; y que por defenderse le mataron seis de sus soldados — por manera que daban muchas quejas del Pedro de Alvarado. Y Cortés les respondió a los mensajeros algo desabrido, y que él iría a México y pondría remedio en todo; y así fueron con aquella respuesta a su gran Moctezuma. Y dicen que lo sintió por muy mala, y hubo enojo de ella. Y así mismo luego despachó Cortés cartas para Pedro de Alvarado, en que le envió a decir que mirase que Moctezuma no se soltase, y que íbamos a grandes jornadas, y le hizo saber de la victoria que habíamos habido contra Narváez — lo cual ya sabía el gran Moctezuma.

Y me quedo aquí, y diré lo que más adelante pasó.</spanish>
<flags>voice: Bernal Díaz del Castillo — blunt, garrulous old soldier, plain and concrete, quietly aggrieved, proud eyewitness (held). ⚠ NO REFERENCE —. Colonial spellings modernized throughout (çenpoal→Cempoal, françisco→Francisco, obedesçer→obedecer, ynportançia→importancia, biruela→viruela, guaçaqualco→Guazacualco, xamayca→Jamaica, proui*→provincia [truncation in edition], etc.). Edition page numbers, folio markers, “Digitized by Google” boilerplate, and closing attribution (“Bernal Díaz del Castillo.—53.”) omitted. “y dexallo E Aqui, y dire” read as me quedo aquí, y diré (abbreviated first person). “real” in “nro rreal” rendered as royal camp (English) / real (Spanish). “diz que dixeron” / “dicen que” reporting formulas preserved. “braza de cuchilla de pedernales” not in this section. === CAPITULO CXXV. Como fuymos A gran- — Section 126 ===
<english>Chapter CXXV. How we went on great marches — Cortés, all his captains, and all those of Narváez, except Pánfilo de Narváez and El Salvatierra, who remained as prisoners.

When the news I have already told of arrived — how Pedro de Alvarado was besieged and Mexico in revolt — the captaincies that were to go settle Panuco and Guazacualco, which had been given to Juan Velázquez de León and Diego de Ordás, ceased. None of them went; all came with us. Cortés spoke to Narváez’s men, feeling they would not willingly go with us on that relief march, and begged them to set aside past enmities on Narváez’s account, offering to make them rich and give them offices; and since they had come seeking their living and were in lands where they could serve God and His Majesty and grow wealthy — and now, he said, fortune was turning — with so many words he spoke to them that all together offered to go with us. And if they had known Mexico’s strength, surely not one would have.

We marched at once on very great journeys until we reached Tlaxcala, where we learned that until Montezuma and his captains knew we had routed Narváez, they did not cease making war on him; they had already killed seven soldiers and burned the quarters. Once they learned of our victory they ceased the war, but said they were very worn down for lack of water and supplies — supplies Montezuma had never ordered given them. Tlaxcala Indians brought this news in that same hour we arrived.

Cortés then ordered a review of the men he was leading and found over thirteen hundred soldiers, both ours and Narváez’s, and over ninety-six horses and eighty crossbowmen and as many harquebusiers. With that force Cortés thought he had men enough to enter Mexico quite safely. Moreover, in Tlaxcala the chiefs gave us two thousand war Indians, and we went on great marches to Texcoco, which is a large city. No honor was done us there, nor did any lord appear — everything very remote and ill-disposed. We reached Mexico on the feast of Saint John, June 1520, and no chiefs or captains or known Indians appeared in the streets; all the houses were deserted. When we reached the quarters where we used to lodge, the great Montezuma came out to the courtyard to speak to Cortés, embrace him, and welcome him and [congratulate him on] the victory over Narváez. Cortés, coming victorious, would not hear him, and Montezuma withdrew to his quarters very sad and thoughtful.

Once each of us was lodged where we had stayed before leaving Mexico for Narváez’s country, and Narváez’s men in other quarters, we had already seen and spoken with Pedro de Alvarado and the soldiers who had remained with him. They gave us account of the war the Mexicans were making on them and the hardship they had them in, and we gave them relation of the victory against Narváez. I leave this here and will tell how Cortés sought to learn what had caused Mexico to rise; for we well understood that Montezuma regretted it, and that if it had been his wish or by his counsel — many soldiers who had remained with Pedro de Alvarado in those straits said that if Montezuma had been behind it, they would all have been killed, and that Montezuma was appeasing them to cease the war. What Pedro de Alvarado told Cortés about the case was that it was to free the Mexicans and Montezuma, and because Huichilobos had commanded it — because we had placed in his house the image of Our Lady the Virgin Saint Mary and the cross. Further he said that many Indians had come to take the holy image from the altar where we had placed it and could not; the Indians took it as a great miracle and told Montezuma, who ordered them to leave it in the same place and on the same altar and not bother doing anything else, and so they left it. Further Pedro de Alvarado said that because Narváez had sent word to Montezuma that he was coming to release him from prison and seize us — which did not prove true — and because Cortés had told Montezuma that once we had ships we would embark and leave the whole land, and we did not go, and it was all words; and now he had seen many more teules [Spaniards] arrive before all of Narváez’s men and ours re-entered Mexico — it would be well to kill Pedro de Alvarado and his soldiers and release the great Montezuma, and afterward leave none of ours or Narváez’s men alive; especially as they were certain Narváez and his soldiers would defeat us.

These accounts and explanations Pedro de Alvarado gave Cortés. Cortés asked him again what cause he had had for making war on them while they were dancing and holding their festivals, and he answered that he knew very certainly that when the festivals, dances, and sacrifices they made to their Huichilobos and Tezcatlipoca were finished, they would at once come make war on him according to the agreement they had made among themselves; and all the rest he learned from a papá [priest] and two principals and other Mexicans. Cortés told him they had said they asked permission to hold the areito [ritual dance] and dances; he said that was true, and that it was to catch them off guard, and so they would fear and not come make war on him — and for that reason he struck first. When Cortés heard that, he said, very angry, that it was very badly done and a great folly [marginal note in the original: and little truth], and that God grant Montezuma had been released and that he had never heard such a thing done to his idols. And he left off speaking to him of it.

[Later interpolation: testado in the original] I want to say what Pedro de Alvarado was saying — that when the Mexican Indians were fighting with him, many of them said that a great Teclecíguata, who is a great lady, another like the one who was in her great cue [temple], was throwing earth in their eyes and blinding them, and that a teule on a white horse was doing them much harm; and that if not for them they would have killed them all; and they say this was told to the great Montezuma by his principals; and if that was so, they are very great miracles, and we must always give thanks to God and the Virgin Saint Mary our Lady, his blessed mother, who aids us in everything, and to the blessed Lord Saint James.

Also the same Pedro de Alvarado said that when he was with them in that war he ordered a loaded cannon set — it had a ball and many shot — and as many squadrons of Indians came to burn the quarters, he went out to fight them and ordered fire put to the cannon, and it did not go off. After he made one charge against the squadrons making war on him and many Indians pressed him, as he was retreating to the stronghold and quarters, then — without firing the cannon — the ball and shot came out and killed many Indians; and if that had not happened the enemies would have killed them all, as on that occasion they carried off two of his soldiers alive.

Another thing Pedro de Alvarado said — and this alone other soldiers said; the rest of the accounts only Pedro de Alvarado related — was that they had no water to drink; they dug in the courtyard and made a well and drew fresh water, though everything was salty. All of this too was great blessings God our Lord did for us. And on this matter of water I say that in Mexico there was a fountain that many times, and most often, flowed with water. [Words missing: the manuscript has a crossed-out passage here; see edition note.]

These things, and others, are to be said — I hear them from persons of credit and to be believed, who were with Pedro de Alvarado when that happened. I leave this here and will tell the great war they then gave us, and it is in this manner.</english>
<spanish>Capítulo CXXV. Cómo fuimos a grandes jornadas, así Cortés como todos sus capitanes y todos los de Narváez, excepto Pánfilo de Narváez y El Salvatierra, que quedaban presos.

Cuando llegó la nueva que ya he contado — cómo Pedro de Alvarado estaba cercado y México rebelado — cesaron las capitanías que habían de ir a poblar Panuco y Guazacualco, que habían dado a Juan Velázquez de León y a Diego de Ordás; no fue ninguno de ellos, que todos fueron con nosotros. Y Cortés habló a los de Narváez, que sintió que no irían con nosotros de buena voluntad a hacer aquel socorro, y les rogó que dejasen atrás enemistades pasadas por lo de Narváez, ofreciéndoseles de hacerlos ricos y darles cargos; y pues venían a buscar la vida y estaban en tierras donde podrían hacer servicio a Dios y a su Majestad y enriquecer, y pues que ahora venía la lanza y tantas palabras les dijo, que todos a una se le ofrecieron que irían con nosotros; y si supieran las fuerzas de México, cierto está que no fuera ninguno. Y luego caminamos a muy grandes jornadas hasta llegar a Tlaxcala, donde supimos que hasta que Montezuma y sus capitanes habían sabido cómo habíamos desbaratado a Narváez, no dejaron de darle guerra y le habían ya muerto siete soldados y le quemaron los aposentos; y que desde que supieron nuestra victoria cesaron de darle guerra, mas dijeron que estaban muy fatigados por falta de agua y bastimento — el cual bastimento nunca se lo había mandado dar el Montezuma. Esta nueva trajeron indios de Tlaxcala en aquella misma hora que hubimos llegado.

Y luego Cortés mandó hacer alarde de la gente que llevaba y halló sobre mil y trescientos soldados, así de los nuestros como de los de Narváez, y sobre noventa y seis caballos y ochenta ballesteros y otros tantos escopeteros, con los cuales le pareció a Cortés que llevaba gente para poder entrar muy a nuestro salvo en México. Y demás de esto, en Tlaxcala nos dieron los caciques dos mil indios de guerra, y luego fuimos a grandes jornadas hasta Texcoco, que es una gran ciudad; y no se nos hizo honra ninguna en ella ni pareció ningún señor, sino todo muy remoto y de mal arte. Y llegamos a México día de señor San Juan de junio de mil y quinientos y veinte años, y no parecían por las calles caciques ni capitanes ni indios conocidos, sino todas las casas despobladas. Y como llegamos a los aposentos en que solíamos posar, el gran Montezuma salió al patio para hablar y abrazar a Cortés y darle el bienvenido y [darle la bienvenida por] la victoria con Narváez; y Cortés, como venía victorioso, no le quiso oír, y el Montezuma se entró en su aposento muy triste y pensativo.

Pues ya aposentados cada uno de nosotros donde solíamos estar antes de que saliésemos de México para ir a lo de Narváez, y los de Narváez en otros aposentos, ya habíamos visto y hablado con el Pedro de Alvarado y los soldados que con él quedaron; y ellos nos daban cuenta de las guerras que los mexicanos les daban y del trabajo en que les tenían puesto, y nosotros les dábamos relación de la victoria contra Narváez. Y me quedo aquí, y diré cómo Cortés procuró saber qué fue la causa de levantarse México; porque bien entendido teníamos que al Montezuma le pesó dello, y que si le pluguiera o fuera por su consejo — dijeron muchos soldados de los que se quedaron con Pedro de Alvarado en aquellos trances que si el Montezuma fuera en ello, que a todos les mataran, y que el Montezuma los aplacaba que cesasen la guerra. Y lo que contaba el Pedro de Alvarado a Cortés sobre el caso era que por libertar a los mexicanos y al Montezuma, y porque su Huichilobos se lo mandó — porque pusimos en su casa la imagen de Nuestra Señora la Virgen Santa María y la cruz. Y más dijo que habían llegado muchos indios a quitar la santa imagen del altar donde la pusimos, y que no pudieron; y que los indios lo tuvieron a gran milagro y que se lo dijeron al Montezuma, y que les mandó que la dejasen en el mismo lugar y altar y que no curasen de hacer otra cosa, y así la dejaron. Y más dijo el Pº de Alvarado que por lo que el Narváez les había enviado a decir al Montezuma — que le venía a soltar de las prisiones y a prendernos, y no salió verdad — y como Cortés había dicho al Montezuma que en teniendo navíos nos habíamos de ir a embarcar y salir de toda la tierra, y que no nos íbamos, y que todo eran palabras; y que ahora había visto venir muchos más teules antes de que todos los de Narváez y los nuestros tornásemos a entrar en México — que sería bien matar al Pedro de Alvarado y a sus soldados y soltar al gran Montezuma, y después no quedar a vida a ninguno de los nuestros ni de los de Narváez; cuanto más que tuvieron por cierto que nos vencería el Narváez y sus soldados.

Estas pláticas y descargo dio Pedro de Alvarado a Cortés. Y le tornó a decir Cortés a qué causa les fue a dar guerra estando bailando y haciendo sus fiestas; y respondió que sabía muy ciertamente que en acabando las fiestas y bailes y sacrificios que hacían a su Huichilobos y a Tezcatlipoca, que luego le habían de venir a dar guerra según el concierto tenían entre ellos hecho; y todo lo demás que lo supo de un papá y de dos principales y de otros mexicanos. Y Cortés le dijo: pues me han dicho que le demandaron licencia para hacer el areito y bailes. Dijo que así era verdad, y que fue por tomarlos descuidados, y que porque temiesen y no viniesen a darle guerra; que por esto se adelantó a dar en ellos. Y desde que aquello Cortés le oyó, le dijo, muy enojado, que era muy mal hecho y gran desatino [nota marginal en el original: y poca verdad], y que pluguiera a Dios que el Montezuma se hubiera soltado y que tal cosa no la oyera a sus ídolos. Y así le dejó que no le habló más en ello.

[Interpolación posterior: testado en el original] Yo quiero decir que decía el Pedro de Alvarado, que cuando peleaban los indios mexicanos con él, que dijeron muchos de ellos que una gran Teclecíguata, que es gran señora, otra como la que estaba en su gran cue, les echaba tierra en los ojos y les cegaba, y que un güey teule que andaba en un caballo blanco les hacían mucho mal; y que si por ellos no fueran, que les mataran a todos; y que aquello dicen que se lo dijeron al gran Montezuma sus principales; y si aquello fue así, grandísimos milagros son, y de contino hemos de dar gracias a Dios y a la Virgen Santa María Nuestra Señora, su bendita madre, que en todo nos socorre, y al bienaventurado señor Santiago.

También dijo el mismo Pº de Alvarado que cuando andaba con ellos en aquella guerra mandó poner a un tiro que estaba cebado fuego, el cual tenía una pelota y muchos perdigones; y que como venían muchos escuadrones de indios a quemar los aposentos, que salió a pelear con ellos y mandó poner fuego al tiro, y que no salió; y desque hizo una arremetida contra los escuadrones que le daban guerra y cargaban muchos indios sobre él, y que venía retrayéndose a la fuerza y aposento, que entonces, sin poner fuego al tiro, salió la pelota y los perdigones y mató muchos indios; y que si aquello no acaeciera, que los enemigos les mataran a todos, como en aquella vez les llevaron dos de sus soldados vivos.

Otra cosa dijo el Pº de Alvarado — y esta sola cosa la dijeron otros soldados; que las demás pláticas solo el Pedro de Alvarado lo contaba — y es que no tenían agua para beber, y cavaron en el patio y hicieron un pozo y sacaron agua dulce, siendo todo salado; también todo fue muchos bienes que Nuestro Señor Dios nos hacía. Y a esto del agua digo yo que en México estaba una fuente que muchas veces y todas las más manaba agua. [palabras faltantes: el manuscrito tiene aquí un pasaje tachado; véase nota de edición.]

Estas cosas, y otras, se ha de decir que lo oí a personas de fe y creer, que se hallaron con el Pº de Alvarado cuando aquello pasó. Y me quedo aquí, y diré la gran guerra que luego nos dieron, y es desta manera.</spanish>
<flags>voice: Bernal Díaz del Castillo — blunt, garrulous old soldier, plain and concrete, quietly aggrieved, proud eyewitness (held). ⚠ NO REFERENCE —. === CAPITULO CXXVI. Como nos dieron gue- — Section 127 ===
<english>CHAPTER CXXVI. How they made war on us in Mexico, the battles they gave us, and other things we went through.

When Cortés saw that in Texcoco they had not received us at all, nor even given us food except badly and grudgingly, and that we found no principals with whom to speak, and he saw everything remote and in bad shape—and coming to Mexico, the same—and saw they were not holding markets but everything was shut down, and heard from Pedro de Alvarado about the manner and disorder with which he went to make war on them—it seems Cortés had said on the road to Narváez’s captains, boasting of himself, what great respect and command he had, and that along the roads they would come out to receive him and hold festivals and give him gold, and that in Mexico he commanded as absolutely over the great Montezuma as over all his captains, and that they would give him presents of gold as they used to—and seeing that everything was quite the opposite of his expectations, that they would not even give us food, he was very angry and overbearing with the great number of Spaniards he had with him, and very sad and sullen. And at that moment the great Montezuma sent two of his principals to beg our Cortés to come see him, that he wished to speak with him. The answer he gave them was: go tell that dog that he does not even want to hold markets, nor order food given us—

[Marginal note in the manuscript: concerning the Indians with whom they were dancing, that he went to make war on them—I do not believe it, nor have I ever heard such a thing, nor is it credible that he would have done it, though Bishop Fray Bartolomé de las Casas says so; that and other things that never happened, but that God truly struck them to put fear into them, and that with those harms he did them they would have enough to tend to and weep over among themselves, so they would not come to make war on him; and as they say, he who attacks conquers—and it turned out much worse, as it seemed; and we also learned on very good authority that Montezuma never ordered such a war to be made, and that when they were fighting Pedro de Alvarado, Montezuma was ordering his people not to do it, and his people answered that they could no longer endure keeping him prisoner, and that while they were dancing he meant to kill them as they were, and that they were going to take him out of there and kill all the teules [gods, the Spaniards] who defended him.]

—and then, when our captains heard that from Cortés—Juan Velázquez de León and Cristóbal de Olid and Alonso de Ávila and Francisco de Lugo—they said: “Sir, restrain your anger, and consider how much good and honor this king of these lands has done us, who is so good that if not for him we would already be dead, and they would have eaten us; and consider that he has even given us his daughters.” When Cortés heard this he grew angrier still at the words they spoke to him, as they seemed a reproach, and said that he and I had fulfilled our duty in dealing with a dog who was secretly making common cause with Narváez—and now you see they do not even give us food. Our captains said: “This seems to us what ought to be done, and it is good counsel.” And since Cortés had there in Mexico so many Spaniards, both ours and Narváez’s men, he cared for nothing at all, and spoke so angrily and discourteously that he spoke again to the principals, that they tell their lord Montezuma to order markets held at once, or else he would do—and see—what would happen. The principals understood well the insulting words Cortés spoke of their lord, and also the reproach our captains gave Cortés over it, for they knew well they had been the ones who used to keep their lord under guard, and knew they were great servants of their Montezuma; and as they understood it, they told Montezuma. And whether from anger, or because it was already agreed they would make war on us, within a quarter of an hour a soldier came at great speed, very badly wounded, from a town next to Mexico called Tacuba. He was bringing some Indian women who belonged to Cortés, and one was Montezuma’s daughter—it seems he had left them there in the care of the lord of Tacuba, who were kinsmen of the same lord, when we went to Narváez’s country. That soldier said the whole city and the road by which he came were full of warriors with every kind of weapon; that they took from him the Indian women he was bringing and gave him two wounds, and that if he had not broken free they already had hold of him to put him in a canoe and carry him off to sacrifice; and that they had destroyed a bridge. When Cortés and some of us heard that, it truly grieved us greatly, for we who were used to fighting Indians understood well the great multitude of them that usually gather, and that however well we fought and however many more soldiers we brought now, we were going to run great risk to our lives, limbs, and hardships—especially being in so strong a city. Let us go on and say that Cortés at once ordered a captain named Diego de Ordaz to go with four hundred soldiers, among them the most crossbowmen and harquebusiers and some horsemen, and see whether what the wounded soldier who had brought the news said was true; and if he saw it could be pacified without war and uproar, to pacify it. And as Diego de Ordaz went as he was ordered with his four hundred soldiers, he had not even well reached mid-street on his way when so many Mexican war squadrons came out against him, and many more who were on the rooftops, and gave him such great battles that at the first charges they killed eight soldiers, and wounded nearly all the rest, and Diego de Ordaz himself took three wounds, so that he could not advance a single step but had to turn back little by little toward the quarters; and as he withdrew they killed another good soldier named Liscano, who with a montante [two-handed sword] had done deeds of a very brave man. And at that moment, if many squadrons came out against Diego de Ordaz, many more came to our quarters, and they threw so many darts and stones with slings and arrows that they wounded more than forty-six of ours that time and twelve died of their wounds; and there were so many warriors upon us that Diego de Ordaz, as he was withdrawing, could not reach the quarters for the great war they gave him—some from behind, others from in front, others from the rooftops. Then perhaps our shots, neither muskets nor crossbows nor lances nor thrusts we gave them, nor our good fighting, availed much—for though we killed and wounded many of them, they closed their squadrons with the points of swords and lances and pressed in on us with all that; they did not lose ground in their good fighting, nor could we drive them from us. And in the end, with the shots and muskets and crossbows and the harm we did them with thrusts, Ordaz had time to enter the quarters, which until then, though he wished, he could not pass through—with his soldiers well wounded and fourteen fewer. And still many squadrons did not cease making war on us, calling us women and naming us cowards and other insults. And all the harm they had done us until then had been nothing compared to what they did afterward—for they had such boldness that some giving us war on one side and others on another, they came to set fire to our quarters, and we could not defend ourselves against the smoke and fire until remedy was applied by throwing much earth on top and blocking other halls through which the fire came; and truly there inside they believed they would burn us alive. These battles lasted all day and even into the night; so many of their squadrons were upon us, throwing darts and stones and arrows in volleys and at random, that from the day’s fighting and that of the night all those patios and floors were heaped with them. As for us, that night was spent caring for the wounded and remedying the breaches they had made and preparing for another day. With that the night passed. And when day broke, our Captain agreed that with all ours and Narváez’s men we should go out to fight them, carrying muskets and crossbows, and try to defeat them so that they would feel our strength and effort more than the day before. And I say we had made that plan because the Mexicans had agreed on the same; and we fought very well, but they were so strong and had so many squadrons relieving one another from moment to moment that even if ten thousand Hectors of Troy and as many Rolands had been there, they could not have broken them—for I know it now. Here I mean to tell how it went and what stubbornness we saw in the fighting; I say I do not know how to write it, for neither shots nor muskets nor crossbows availed, nor closing with them, nor killing thirty or forty of them each time we charged—for they fought as solidly and with more vigor than at the beginning. And if sometimes we gained a little ground or part of a street, they made as if to retreat so we would follow and draw away from our strength and quarters, to strike us more safely, believing we would not return alive to the quarters—for when we withdrew they did us great harm. And to pass and burn their houses—I have already said in the chapter that speaks of it that from house to house they had a wooden drawbridge; they raised it, and we could not pass except through very deep water; and from the rooftops the stones and rocks we could not endure—so they abused and wounded many of ours. And I do not know why I write it so faintly, for three or four soldiers who had been in Italy and were there with us swore many times to God they had never seen wars so fierce—not in some they had been in between Christians and against the artillery of the King of France, nor of the Great Turk, nor people like those Indians, with such spirit to close squadrons as they saw; and for other reasons they gave for it, as you will see later. Let me stay here, and I will tell how with great labor we withdrew to our quarters, and still many squadrons of warriors upon us with great shouts and whistles and little trumpets and drums, calling us cowards and good-for-nothings—we dared not stand to them in battle all day but kept turning back. That day they killed another ten or twelve soldiers, and we all returned well wounded. And what passed that night was agreeing that in two days all the soldiers as many as were healthy in the whole camp should go out with four engines like towers, made of stout wood, under each of which twenty-five men could go; they had loopholes and holes for shots; harquebusiers and crossbowmen went in them too, and alongside them other harquebusiers and crossbowmen, and the guns and all the rest and the horsemen were to make some charges. With this plan made, since that day we spent on the work and strengthening many breaches they had made for us, we did not go out to fight—I do not know how to say it—and the great squadrons of warriors who came to our quarters to make war on us came not only from ten or twelve directions but from more than twenty, for we were spread through all of them and in many other places besides; and meanwhile, as we repaired and strengthened them as I have said, many other squadrons tried to enter our quarters in plain sight, and neither shots nor crossbows nor muskets nor many charges and thrusts could drive them back. And what they said was that that day not one of us was to remain, and that they were to sacrifice our hearts and blood to their gods, and with the legs and arms they would have plenty to make pageants [festive displays] and festivals, and they would throw the bodies to the tigers and lions and vipers and snakes they kept penned, to gorge on them; and that that effect, two days after they ordered that food not be given them, and the gold we had—we opened it with little joy—and all the mantles, and to the men of Tlaxcala who were with us they said they would put them in cages to fatten and little by little make their sacrifices with their bodies; and very earnestly they said we should give them their great lord Montezuma. And they said other things; and at night likewise always many whistles and voices and showers of darts and stones and arrows. And when day broke, after commending ourselves to God, we came out of our quarters with our towers—which it seems to me in other places where I have been in wars, when such things were needed, they call mantlets and hurdles—with the guns and muskets and crossbows in front and the horsemen making some charges. And as I have said, though we killed many of them, nothing availed to make them turn their backs; rather, if they had fought very bravely the two past days, this day they were much stronger with greater forces and squadrons. And still we resolved that even at the cost of all our lives we would go with our towers and engines as far as the great cue [temple] of Huichilobos—I do not tell at length the great battles they gave us in one strong house, nor will I tell how they wounded the horses nor how we availed ourselves of them, for though the horses charged the squadrons to break them, they threw so many arrows and darts and stones at them that they could not defend themselves however well armored they were; and if they were overtaking them, the Mexicans at once let themselves fall safely into the canals and lagoons, where they had made other defenses against the horsemen, and many other Indians with very long lances to finish killing them—so nothing availed. And to separate ourselves to burn or destroy any house was useless, for as I have said, they all stand in water, and from house to house a drawbridge; to cross by swimming was very dangerous, for from the rooftops they had so many stones and rocks and defenses that it was a lost cause to put ourselves to it. And besides, in some houses where we set fire, one house took a whole day to burn and fire could not spread from one house to another—one thing being that they stood apart, water between, and another that they were of rooftop construction—so our labors were useless in risking our persons in that. In such fashion we went as far as the great cue of their idols, and suddenly more than four thousand Mexicans climbed up on it, besides other captaincies that were in them, with great lances and stones and darts, and took up the defense and resisted our ascent a good while; the towers and the shots and crossbows and muskets and horsemen did not suffice, for though the horses wished to charge there were great slabs, the whole patio paved, and the horses went on hands and knees, and they were so smooth they fell; and as from the steps of the high cue they defended the pass, and on one side and another we had so many enemies—and though our shots carried off ten or fifteen of them, and at thrusts and charges we killed many more—so many people pressed that we could not climb to the high cue. And with great order we turned to press on again, without carrying the towers because they were already broken up, and we climbed up to them. Here Cortés showed himself a very brave man, as he always was; the fight and hard battle we had here was something to note—seeing us all running blood and full of wounds and others dead. And our Lord willed that we reached where we used to keep the image of Our Lady, and we did not find it—it seemed, as we learned, the great Montezuma had devotion to it and ordered it kept. We set fire to their idols, and a good piece of the hall burned with the idols Huichilobos and Tezcatepuca [Tezcatlipoca]. Then the Tlaxcalans helped us very well. And with that done, as some of us were fighting and others setting the fire, as I have said, we saw the priests who were in this great cue, and above three or four thousand Indians, all principals—as we were coming down, some made us come rolling down six steps and even ten—and there is so much to tell of other squadrons who were on the parapets and inner chambers of the great cue, throwing so many darts and arrows at us, that whether squadrons or others we could not face them. We agreed, with great labor and risk to our persons, to return to our quarters—the towers destroyed, all wounded, and sixteen dead—and the Indians always pressing us, and other squadrons at our backs—he who did not see us, though I speak more clearly here, I cannot make it understood; for I have not yet told what the Mexican squadrons did who were making war in the quarters while we were outside, and the great stubbornness and persistence with which they tried to enter them. In this battle we took two principal priests, whom Cortés ordered carried off under good guard. Many times I have seen this battle and our ascent of this great cue painted among the Mexicans and Tlaxcalans; they hold it a very heroic thing—though they paint us all very wounded, running blood, and many dead in portraits they have made of it. They think much of our setting fire to the cue and so many warriors guarding it on the parapets and inner chambers and many other Indians below on the ground and patios full, and on the sides, and many others, and our towers destroyed—as it was possible to climb it, let us leave off speaking of it. And let us say how with great labor we returned to the quarters; and if many people followed us giving war, many others were in the quarters who had already torn down some walls to enter them—and with our arrival they ceased, but not so that all the rest of the day they stopped throwing darts and stones and arrows; and at night, shouts and stones and darts—let us leave off their great stubbornness and persistence, which they always kept continuously upon our quarters, as I have said. And let us say that that night was spent caring for the wounded and burying the dead and preparing to go out another day to fight and putting strength and defenses to the walls they had torn down and to other breaches they had made, and taking counsel how and in what manner we could fight without receiving so many hurts and deaths—and in all we discussed we found no remedy. I also want to tell the curses Narváez’s men heaped on Cortés, and the words they said—that they renounced him and the land and even Diego Velázquez who sent them here; that they had been quite peaceful in their houses on the island of Cuba and were enchanted and out of their senses. Let us return to our discussion, which was agreed: to ask them for peace, to get out of Mexico. And when day broke, many more squadrons of warriors came, and in earnest they besieged our quarters on all sides; and if before they threw many stones and arrows, many thicker came this day with greater outcries and whistles; and other squadrons on other sides tried to enter us—neither shots nor muskets availed, though they did them much harm. And seeing all this, Cortés agreed that the great Montezuma should speak to them from a rooftop and tell them to cease the wars and that we wished to leave their city. And when they went to tell the great Montezuma on Cortés’s behalf, they say he said with great grief: “He already wants my ruin, Malinche—I do not wish to live nor hear him, since in such a state my fortune has brought me for his sake”; and he would not come. And they also say he said he no longer wished to see or hear him or his false words and promises and lies. And the father of the mass and Cristóbal de Olid went, and spoke to him with great respect and very loving words. Montezuma said: “I have believed that nothing will avail to make the war cease, for they have already raised up another lord and have resolved not to let you leave here alive; and so I believe you must all die.” And let us return to the great battles they gave us. Montezuma placed himself on a parapet of a rooftop, with many of our soldiers who guarded him, and began to speak to them with very loving words that they should leave off the war and that we would leave Mexico; and many Mexican principals and captains knew him well and at once ordered their people to be silent and not throw darts nor stones nor arrows. And four of them drew near where Montezuma could speak to them and they to him, and weeping they said: “Lord, our grave lord, how it grieves us for all your harm and loss and for your sons and kinsmen—we make known to you that we have already raised up a kinsman of yours as lord,” and there they named him, who was called Coadlabaca, lord of Iztapalapa—not Guatemuz, who later became lord. And they said further that the war they were to finish, and that they had promised their idols not to leave it until we had all died; and that they begged Huichilobos and Tezcatepuca each day to keep him free and safe from our power, and that when he came out as they wished they would not fail to hold him in much greater honor than before as lord, and that they begged his pardon. And they had scarcely finished their speech when at that moment they threw so many stones and darts that our men who were shielding him, when they saw that while he spoke with them they were not making war, grew careless for a moment in shielding him quickly—and they hit him with three stones, one on the head and another on an arm and another on a leg. And though they begged him to be cured and eat, and spoke good words to him about it, he would not; rather, when we were not looking, they came to say he was dead. Cortés wept for him, and all our captains and soldiers; and there was not a man among us who knew him and dealt with him who was not mourned as if he had been our father—and we need not wonder at it, seeing how good he was. They said he had reigned seventeen years, and that he was the best king there had been in Mexico, and that in person he had won three challenges he had over the lands he subjugated. And let us go on.</english>
<spanish>CAPÍTULO CXXVI. Cómo nos dieron guerra en México y los combates que nos daban y otras cosas que pasamos.

Como Cortés vio que en Texcoco no nos habían hecho ningún recibimiento ni aún dado de comer, sino mal y por mal cabo, y que no hallamos principales con quien hablar, y lo vio todo remoto y de mal arte, y venido a México lo mismo, y vio que no hacían tianguis, sino todo levantado, y oyó a Pedro de Alvarado de la manera y desconcierto con que les fue a dar guerra, y parece ser había dicho Cortés en el camino a los capitanes de Narváez, alabándose de sí mismo el gran acato y mando que tenía, y que por los caminos le saldrían a recibir y hacer fiestas y le darían oro, y que en México mandaba tan absolutamente así al gran Montezuma como a todos sus capitanes, y que le darían presentes de oro como solían; y viendo que todo estaba muy al contrario de sus pensamientos, que aún de comer no nos daban, estaba muy airado y soberbio con la mucha gente de españoles que traía, y muy triste y mohino. Y en este instante envió el gran Montezuma dos de sus principales a rogar a nuestro Cortés que le fuese a ver, que le quería hablar; y la respuesta que les dio dijo: vaya para perro, que aún tianguis no quiere hacer, ni de comer no nos—

[Nota marginal en el manuscrito: con que bailaban los indios, les fue a dar guerra; yo no lo creo, ni nunca tal oí, ni es de creer que tal hiciese, puesto que lo dice el obispo fray Bartolomé de las Casas; aquello y otras cosas que nunca pasaron, sino que verdaderamente Dios entró en ellos por meterles temor, y que con aquellos males que les hizo tuviesen harto qué curar y llorar en ellos, porque no le viniesen a dar guerra; y como dicen que quien acomete vence, y fue muy peor según pareció; y también supimos de mucha verdad que tal guerra nunca el Montezuma mandó dar, y que cuando combatían a Pedro de Alvarado, el Montezuma les mandaba a los suyos que no lo hiciesen, y que le respondían los suyos que ya no era de sufrir tenerle preso, y estando bailando él les a matar como fueron, y que le habían de sacar de allí y matar a todos los teules que le defendían.]

—manda dar; y entonces, como aquello le oyeron a Cortés nuestros capitanes, que fue Juan Velázquez de León y Cristóbal de Olid y Alonso de Ávila y Francisco de Lugo, dijeron: «Señor, tenga su ira y mire cuánto bien y honra nos ha hecho este rey de estas tierras, que es tan bueno que si por él no fuese ya fuéramos muertos, y nos habrían comido; y mire que hasta las hijas nos ha dado». Y como esto oyó Cortés se indignó más de las palabras que le dijeron, como parecían de reprensión, y dijo que cumplimos él y yo de tener con un perro que se hacía con Narváez secretamente; y ahora veis que aún de comer no nos dan. Y dijeron nuestros capitanes: «Esto nos parece que debe hacer, y es buen consejo». Y como Cortés tenía allí en México tantos españoles, así de los nuestros como de los de Narváez, no se daba nada por cosa ninguna, y hablaba tan airado y descomedido que tornó a hablar a los principales, que dijesen a su señor Montezuma que luego mande hacer tianguis y mercados, si no que hará y verá lo que acontecerá. Y los principales bien entendieron las palabras injuriosas que Cortés dijo de su señor, y aún también la reprensión que nuestros capitanes dieron a Cortés sobre ello, porque bien los conocían que habían sido los que solían tener en guarda a su señor, y sabían que eran grandes servidores de su Montezuma; y según y de la manera que lo entendieron, se lo dijeron al Montezuma. Y de enojo, o porque ya estaba concertado que nos diesen guerra, no tardó un cuarto de hora que vino un soldado a gran prisa muy mal herido, que venía de un pueblo que está junto a México que se dice Tacuba, y traía unas indias que eran de Cortés, y la una hija del Montezuma; que parece ser las dejó a guardar allí al señor de Tacuba, que eran sus parientes del mismo señor, cuando fuimos a lo de Narváez. Y dijo aquel soldado que estaba toda la ciudad y camino por donde venía lleno de gente de guerra, con todo género de armas, y que le quitaron las indias que traía y le dieron dos heridas, y que si no se las soltara, que le tenían ya asido para meterle en una canoa y llevárselo a sacrificar; y habían deshecho una puente. Y desde que aquello oyó Cortés y algunos de nosotros, ciertamente nos pesó mucho, porque bien entendido teníamos, los que solíamos batallar con indios, la mucha multitud que de ellos se suelen juntar, y que por bien que peleásemos y aunque más soldados trujésemos ahora, que habíamos de pasar gran riesgo de nuestras vidas y miembros y trabajos, especialmente estando en tan fuerte ciudad. Pasemos adelante y digamos que luego Cortés mandó a un capitán que se decía Diego de Ordaz que fuese con cuatrocientos soldados, y entre ellos los más ballesteros y escopeteros y algunos de caballo, y que mirase qué era aquello que decía el soldado que había venido herido y traído las nuevas, y que si viese que sin guerra y ruido se pudiese apaciguar, lo pacificase. Y como fue el Diego de Ordaz de la manera que le fue mandado con sus cuatrocientos soldados, aún no hubo bien llegado a media calle por donde iba, cuando le salen tantos escuadrones mexicanos de guerra, y otros muchos que estaban en las azoteas, y le dieron tan grandes combates que le mataron a las primeras arremetidas ocho soldados, y a todos los más hirieron, y al mismo Diego de Ordaz le dieron tres heridas, por manera que no pudo pasar un paso adelante, sino volverse poco a poco al aposento; y al retráerle mataron a otro buen soldado que se decía Liscano, que con un montante había hecho cosas de muy esforzado varón. Y en aquel instante, si muchos escuadrones salieron al Diego de Ordaz, muchos más vinieron a nuestros aposentos, y tiran tanta vara y piedras con honda y flecha, que nos hirieron de aquella vez sobre cuarenta y seis de los nuestros y doce murieron de las heridas; y estaban tantos guerreros sobre nosotros que el Diego de Ordaz, que se venía retrayendo, no podía llegar a los aposentos por la mucha guerra que le daban, unos por detrás y otros por delante y otros desde las azoteas. Pues quizá aprovechaban mucho nuestros tiros, ni escopetas, ni ballestas, ni lanzas ni estocadas que les dábamos, ni nuestro buen pelear, que aunque les matábamos y heríamos muchos de ellos, por las puntas de las espadas y lanzas se nos metían con todo esto; cerraban sus escuadrones y no perdían punto de su buen pelear, ni les podíamos apartar de nosotros. Y en fin, con los tiros y escopetas y ballestas y el mal que les hacíamos de estocadas, tuvo tiempo de entrar el Ordaz en el aposento, que hasta entonces, y aunque quisiera, no podía pasar, y con sus soldados bien heridos y catorce menos. Y todavía no cesaban muchos escuadrones de darnos guerra, y decirnos que éramos como mujeres, y nos llamaban de cobardes, y otros vituperios. Y aún no ha sido nada todo el daño que nos han hecho hasta ahora, a lo que después hicieron; y es que tuvieron tanto atrevimiento que unos dándonos guerra por unas partes y otros por otra, entraron a ponernos fuego en nuestros aposentos, que no nos podíamos valer con el humo y fuego hasta que se puso remedio con derrocar sobre ello mucha tierra y atajar otras salas por donde venía el fuego; que verdaderamente allí dentro creyeron de quemarnos vivos. Y duraron estos combates todo el día, y aún la noche; estaban sobre nosotros tantos escuadrones de ellos, y tiraban varas y piedras y flechas a bulto y piedra perdida, que de lo del día y lo de entonces estaban todos aquellos patios y suelos hechos parvas de ellos. Pues nosotros, aquella noche en curar heridos y en poner remedio en los portillos que habían hecho, y en apercibirnos para otro día, en esto se pasó. Pues desde que amaneció, acordó nuestro capitán que con todos los nuestros y los de Narváez saliésemos a pelear con ellos, y que llevásemos tiros y escopetas y ballestas, y procurásemos de vencerlos al menos que sintiesen más nuestras fuerzas y esfuerzo mejor que el día pasado. Y digo que teníamos hecho aquel concierto, que los mexicanos tenían concertado lo mismo; y peleábamos muy bien, mas ellos estaban tan fuertes y tenían tantos escuadrones que se remudaban de rato en rato, que aunque hubieran allí diez mil Héctores troyanos y otros tantos Rolands, no les pudieran entrar, porque lo sé ahora. Yo aquí de decir cómo pasó, y vimos el tesón en el pelear; digo que no lo sé escribir, porque ni aprovechaban tiros ni escopetas ni ballestas, ni apechugar con ellos, ni matarles treinta ni cuarenta de cada vez que arremetíamos, que tan enteros y con más vigor peleaban que al principio. Y si algunas veces les íbamos ganando alguna poca de tierra o parte de calle, hacían que se retrayasen era para que les siguiésemos por apartarnos de nuestra fuerza y aposento, para dar más a su salvo en nosotros, creyendo que no volveríamos con las vidas a los aposentos; porque al retrayernos hacían mucho mal. Pues para pasar a quemarles las casas, ya he dicho en el capítulo que dello habla, que de casa a casa tenían una puente de madera levadiza; alzábanla, y no podíamos pasar sino por agua muy hondo; pues desde las azoteas los cantos y piedras no lo podíamos sufrir, por manera que nos maltrataban y herían muchos de los nuestros. Y no sé yo para qué lo escribo así tan tímidamente, porque unos tres o cuatro soldados que se habían hallado en Italia, que allí estaban con nosotros, juraron muchas veces a Dios que guerras tan bravas jamás habían visto, en algunas que se habían hallado entre cristianos y contra la artillería del rey de Francia, ni del gran turco, ni gente como aquellos indios, con tanto ánimo cerrar los escuadrones vieron; y porque decían otras muchas cosas y causas que daban a ello, como adelante verán. Y quédese aquí, y diré cómo con harto trabajo nos retrajimos a nuestros aposentos, y todavía muchos escuadrones de guerreros sobre nosotros, con grandes gritos y silbidos y trompetillas y atambores, llamándonos de cobardes y para poco, que no osábamos atenderles todo el día en batalla, sino volvernos retrayendo. Aquel día mataron otros diez o doce soldados, y todos volvimos bien heridos. Y lo que pasó de la noche fue en concertar para de ahí a dos días saliésemos todos los soldados cuantos sanos había en todo el real, y con cuatro ingenios a manera de torres que se hicieron de madera bien recios, en que pudiesen ir debajo de cualquiera de ellos veinticinco hombres, y llevaban sus ventanillas y agujeros en ellos para ir los tiros; y también iban escopeteros y ballesteros, y junto con ellos habíamos de ir otros soldados escopeteros y ballesteros, y los tiros y todos los demás y los de a caballo hacer algunas arremetidas. Y hecho este concierto, como estuvimos aquel día que entendíamos en la obra y en fortalecer muchos portillos que nos tenían hechos, no salimos a pelear aquel día—no sé cómo lo diga—y los grandes escuadrones de guerreros que nos vinieron a los aposentos a dar guerra no solamente por diez o doce partes, sino por más de veinte, porque en todos estábamos repartidos y en otras muchas partes; y entre tanto que los adobamos y fortalecíamos como dicho tengo, otros muchos escuadrones procuraban entrarnos en los aposentos a escala vista, que ni por tiros ni ballestas ni escopetas ni por muchas arremetidas y estocadas les podían retratar. Pues lo que decían era que en aquel día no habían de quedar ninguno de nosotros, y que habían de sacrificar a sus dioses nuestros corazones y sangre, y con las piernas y brazos que bien tendrían para hacer artificios y fiestas, y que los cuerpos echarían a los tigres y leones y víboras y culebras que tienen encerrados, que se harten de ellos; y que aquel efecto, a dos días que mandaron que no les diesen de comer, y el oro que teníamos—que abríamos mal gozo del—y de todas las mantas, y a los de Tlaxcala que con nosotros estaban les decían que los meterían en jaulas a engordar, y que poco a poco harían sus sacrificios con sus cuerpos; y muy afectuosamente decían que les diésemos su gran señor Montezuma. Y decían otras cosas; y de noche asimismo siempre muchos silbidos y voces, y rociadas de vara y piedra y flecha. Y desde que amaneció, después de encomendarnos a Dios, salimos de nuestros aposentos con nuestras torres—which me parece a mí que en otras partes donde me he hallado en guerras, en cosas que han sido menester, las llaman burros y mantas—y con los tiros y escopetas y ballestas delante y los de caballo haciendo algunas arremetidas. Y como es dicho, aunque les matábamos muchos de ellos, no aprovechaba cosa para hacerles volver las espaldas; sino que si muy bravamente habían peleado los dos días pasados, muy más fuertes y con mayores fuerzas y escuadrones estaban este día. Y todavía determinamos que, aunque a todos costase la vida, de ir con nuestras torres e ingenios hasta el gran cue de Huichilobos; no digo por extenso los grandes combates que en una casa fuerte nos dieron, ni diré cómo los caballos los herían ni nos aprovechábamos de ellos, porque aunque arremetiesen a los escuadrones para ronperlos, tirábanles tanta flecha y vara y piedra que no se podían valer por bien armados que estaban; y si los iban alcanzando, luego se dejaban caer los mexicanos a su salvo en las acequias y lagunas, donde tenían hechos otros amparos para los de caballo, y estaban otros muchos indios con lanzas muy largas para acabar de matarlos; así que no aprovechaba cosa ninguna. Pues apartarnos a quemar ni deshacer ninguna casa era por demás, porque, como es dicho, están todas en el agua, y de casa a casa una puente levadiza; pasarla a nado era cosa muy peligrosa, porque desde las azoteas tenían tanta piedra y cantos y amparos, que era cosa perdida ponernos en ello. Y demás de esto, en algunas casas que les poníamos fuego, tardaba una casa en quemarse un día entero y no se podía pegar fuego de una casa a otra—lo uno estar apartadas una de otra y el agua en medio, y lo otro ser de azoteas—así que eran por demás nuestros trabajos en aventurar nuestras personas en aquello. Por manera que fuimos hasta el gran cue de sus ídolos, y luego de repente suben en él más de cuatro mil mexicanos, sin otras capitanías que en ellos estaban, con grandes lanzas y piedra y vara, y se ponen en defensa y nos resistieron la subida un buen rato, que no bastaban las torres ni los tiros ni ballestas ni escopetas ni los de caballo, porque aunque quisieran arremeter los caballos, había unas losas muy grandes, empedrado todo el patio, que se iban a los caballos pies y manos, y eran tan lisas que caían; y como desde las gradas del alto cue nos defendían el paso, y a un lado y a otro teníamos tantos contrarios, y aunque nuestros tiros llevaban diez o quince de ellos, y a estocadas y arremetidas matábamos otros muchos, cargaba tanta gente que no les podíamos subir al alto cue. Y con gran concierto tornamos a porfiar, sin llevar las torres porque ya estaban desbaratadas, y les subimos arriba. Aquí se mostró Cortés muy varón, como siempre lo fue; o que pelear y fuerte batalla que aquí tuvimos era cosa de notar, vernos a todos corriendo sangre y llenos de heridas y otros muertos. Y quiso nuestro Señor que llegásemos a donde solíamos tener la imagen de nuestra Señora, y no la hallamos; que pareció, según supimos, que el gran Montezuma tenía devoción en ella y le mandó guardar. Y pusimos fuego a sus ídolos, y se quemó un buen pedazo de la sala con los ídolos Huichilobos y Tezcatepuca. Entonces nos ayudaron muy bien los tascaltecas. Pues ya hecho esto, estando que estábamos unos peleando y otros poniendo el fuego, como dicho tengo, vimos los papas que estaban en este gran cue, y sobre tres o cuatro mil indios, todos principales; ya que nos bajábamos, cual nos hacían venir rodando seis gradas y aún diez abajo. Y hay tanto que decir de otros escuadrones que estaban en los petriles y concavidades del gran cue, tirándonos tanta vara y flecha, que así a unos escuadrones como a los otros no podíamos hacer cara. Acordamos, con mucho trabajo y riesgo de nuestras personas, de volvernos a nuestros aposentos—los castillos deshechos y todos heridos y dieciséis muertos—y los indios siempre apretándonos, y otros escuadrones por las espaldas; quien no nos vio, aunque aquí más claro lo diga, yo no lo sé significar. Pues aún no digo lo que hicieron los escuadrones mexicanos que estaban dando guerra en los aposentos, en tanto que andábamos fuera, y la gran porfía y tesón que ponían de entrarles. En esta batalla prendimos dos papas principales, que Cortés nos mandó que los llevásemos a buen recaudo.
