=== CAPITULO CXXXVI. Como demandaron — Section 137 ===
<english>[CHAPTER CXXXVI.] How the captains and most principal persons whom Narváez had brought in his company asked Cortés for license to return to the island of Cuba, and Cortés gave it to them and they left; and how Cortés dispatched ambassadors to Castile and to Santo Domingo and Jamaica, and what happened in each matter.

When the captains of Narváez saw that we already had reinforcements — both from those who had come from Cuba and from Jamaica, whom Francisco de Garay had sent for his fleet, as I have declared in the chapter that speaks of it — and they saw that the towns of the province of Tepeaca were pacified, after many words they spoke to Cortés, with great offers and pleas they begged him to give them license to return to the island of Cuba, since he had promised it to them; and Cortés gave it to them at once, and even promised that if he won back New Spain and the city of Mexico, he would give Andrés de Duero, his companion, much more gold than he had given him before; and likewise he made offers to the other captains, especially Agustín Bermúdez. He ordered them given ship's provisions — at that season there was maize and salted puppies and few chickens — and one of the best ships. Cortés also wrote to his wife, who was called doña Catalina Juárez, La Marcaida, and to Juan Suárez, his brother-in-law, who at that season lived on the island of Cuba; he sent them certain bars and jewels of gold and let them know all the outrages and hardships that had befallen us, and how we were driven out of Mexico. Let us leave this and tell who the persons were who asked for license to return to Cuba, who were still rich: Andrés de Duero; and Agustín Bermúdez; Juan Bono de Queso; and Bernardino de Quesada; and Francisco Velázquez El Corcobado, kinsman of Diego Velázquez, governor of Cuba; and Gonzalo Carrasco, who lives in Puebla and afterward returned to this New Spain; and a Melchor de Velasco who was alcalde of Guatemala; and a Ximénez who lives in Oaxaca, who went for his sons; and the comendador Leonel de Cervantes, who went for his daughters — whom, after Mexico was won, he married off very honorably; and one went who was called Maldonado, native of Medellín, who was ill — I do not mean Maldonado who was husband of doña María del Rincón, nor Maldónado El Ancho, nor another Maldonado called Álvaro Maldonado El Fiero, who was married to a lady called María Rias; and also went a Vargas, resident of Trinidad, whom they called in Cuba Vargas el Galán — I do not mean Vargas who was father-in-law of Cristóbal Lobo, vecino who was of Guatemala; and went a soldier of Cortés's who was called Cárdenas the pilot — that Cárdenas was the one who said to a companion of his, how could we soldiers rest, having two kings in this New Spain. This was the one to whom Cortés gave three hundred pesos so that he would go to his wife and children; and, to avoid the prolixity of setting them all down by memory, many others left whose names I do not well remember. And when Cortés gave them the license, we said that he gave it because we who remained were few; and he answered that it was to avoid scandals and importunities, and that we already saw that for war some of those who were returning were not fit, and that it was better to be alone than badly accompanied. And to dispatch them from the port Cortés sent Pedro de Alvarado, and once he had them embarked, ordered him to return at once to the town.

And let us say now that he also sent to Castile Diego de Ordás and Alonso de Mendoza, native of Medellín de Cáceres, with certain commissions from Cortés — I do not know what others he carried for us, nor did he tell us anything of the business he was sending to treat with his majesty, nor what happened in Castile did I manage to learn, save that the bishop of Burgos said openly, in front of Diego de Ordás, that both Cortés and all the soldiers who had passed with him were wicked and traitors — though Ordáz answered very well for all of us; and then they gave Ordás an encomienda of Señor Santiago, and for arms the volcano that stood between Huaxocingo and near Cholula; and what business went forward I will tell as we learned by letter. Let us set this aside and tell how Cortés sent Alonso de Ávila, who was captain and contador of this New Spain, and together with him sent another hidalgo called Francisco Álvarez Chico, who was a man who understood business; and he ordered that they go with another ship to the island of Santo Domingo to give relation of all that had happened to the Royal Audiencia that resided there and to the Jeronymite friars who stood as governors of all the islands — that they approve what we had done in the conquests and the rout of Narváez, and how he had made slaves in the towns that had killed Spaniards and withdrawn from the obedience they had given to our king and lord, and that thus he intended to do in all the more towns that were of the league and name of Mexicans; and that he begged them to give relation of it in Castile to our great Emperor, and keep in memory the great services we always did him; and that through their intercession and that of the Royal Audiencia and Jeronymite friars we might be favored with justice against the ill will and deeds that the bishop of Burgos and archbishop of Rossano were working against us. He also sent another ship to the island of Jamaica for horses and mares; and the captain who went in it was called Solano de Solís — whom, after Mexico was won, we called Solís El de la Huerta; error of one called the licenciado Ortega.

Well I know that some curious readers will say that without money — how was he sending Diego de Ordás on business to Castile, since it is clear that for Castile and for other parts money is needed; and that likewise he was sending Alonso de Ávila and Francisco Álvarez Chico to Santo Domingo on business, and to the island of Jamaica for horses and mares. To this I say that, upon leaving Mexico — as we left fleeing that night of mine so many times recalled — much gold in bars remained lost in a heap in the hall that all the more soldiers were grabbing from, especially the horsemen and those of Narváez much better; and the officials of his majesty who had it in their power and charge carried off made bundles; and besides this, when they loaded themselves with gold, more than eighty Tlaxcalan Indians by Cortés's order were the first who went out on the bridges — a sight it was that they would save many loads of it, that not all would be lost on the causeway. And as we poor soldiers, who had no command but to be commanded, at that season strove to save our lives and, after tending our wounds, did not look at the gold — whether many loads of it went out on the bridges or not, nor did we care much for it — Cortés with some of our captains strove to have it from the Tlaxcalans who brought it out; and we had suspicion that those who stayed with ten thousand pesos from the parts of those of the Rich Town also had it, and spread fame that they had robbed it. And with this he sent to Castile his personal business and to buy horses, and to the island of Santo Domingo to the Royal Audiencia — because at that time everyone kept quiet with the gold bars they had, though more proclamations had been issued.

Let us leave this and tell how, when all the neighboring towns of Tepeaca were already at peace, Cortés agreed that Francisco de Orozco should remain in the town of Segura de la Frontera as captain, with about twenty soldiers who were wounded and sick; and with all the rest of our army we went to Tlaxcala. Order was given that wood be cut to make thirteen brigantines to go again to Mexico, because we found it most certain that without brigantines we could not master the lake, nor could we make war nor enter again by the causeways into that great city, except with great risk to our lives. — And he who was master of cutting the wood, and of giving the keel and the account and reckoning of how they were to be sailers and light for that purpose, and made them, was a Martín López who certainly, besides being a good soldier in all the wars, served his majesty very well in this matter of the brigantines and worked on them like a strong man. And it seems to me that if by misfortune he had not come in our company from the first, as he came, they would have had to send for another master to Castile; much time would have passed, or none would have come — according to the great obstruction the bishop of Burgos put on everything.

I return to our matter and let us say now that when we arrived at Tlaxcala, our great friend and very loyal vassal of his majesty Mase Escaci had already died of the pox; at whose death we all grieved, and Cortés felt it as much, as he said, as if he had been his father, and he put on mourning of black blankets; and likewise many of our captains and soldiers. And to the sons and kinsmen of Mase Escaci, Cortés and all of us showed them much honor; and because in Tlaxcala there were disputes over command and chieftainship, he appointed and ordered that a legitimate son of the same Mase Escaci should hold it, because thus his father had ordered before he died; and he even said to his sons and kinsmen that they should see not to leave the command of Malinche [Cortés] and his brothers, because certainly we were those who were to lord these lands; and he said to them many other good counsels. Let us stop recounting Mase Escaci, since he is already dead, and let us tell of Xicotencatl the elder and of Chichimecatecle and of all the other chiefs of Tlaxcala who offered to serve Cortés — both in cutting wood for the brigantines and for all else they might be commanded in the war against the Mexicans. Cortés embraced them with much love and gave them thanks for it, especially Xicotencatl the elder and Chichimecatecle; and then strove that he should become Christian, and the good old Xicotencatl of good will said he wished to be; and with the greatest feast that at that season could be made in Tlaxcala, the father of the Merced fathers baptized him and gave him the name don Lorenzo de Vargas.

Let us return to speak of our brigantines: Martín López gave himself such haste in cutting the wood, with the great help of Indians who helped him, that in few days he already had it all cut and marked his reckoning on each timber for what part and place it was to be — as river officials, masters, and carpenters have their marks. Andrés Núñez, another good soldier, also helped him, and an old carpenter who was lame from a wound, called Ramírez the elder. Then Cortés dispatched to the Rich Town for much iron and nails and caulking of the ships we had wrecked, and for anchors and sails and yards and cables and oakum, and for all gear for making ships; and he ordered all the smiths there to come, and a Hernando de Aguilar who was half smith and helped to forge. And because at that time in our camp there were three men called Aguilar, we called this Hernando de Aguilar Maxa Yerro; and he sent as captain to the Rich Town for the gear that the said [commission] commanded brought a regidor of Santa Cruz de Burgos who afterward was of Mexico — a very good soldier and diligent — and even the cauldrons for making pitch, and everything they had before taken from the ships he brought, with more than a thousand Indians whom all the towns of those provinces, enemies of the Mexicans, then gave to carry the loads. Since we had no pitch for caulking, nor even did the Indians know how to make it, Cortés ordered four men of the sea who knew that trade that in some pine groves near Huaxocingo, where there are good ones, they should go make pitch.

Let us pass forward — and although it does not go much to the purpose of the matter I was speaking of, as certain curious gentlemen who knew Alonso de Ávila well have asked me: how, being captain and very stout, and contador of New Spain, and being warlike and his inclination more given to wars than to going to solicit business with the Jeronymite friars who stood as governors of all the islands, for what reason Cortés sent him, having other men who would have been more accustomed to business, such as an Alonso de Grado or a Juan de Cáceres El Rico, and others they named to me — to this I say that Cortés sent Alonso de Ávila because he felt him to be a very manly man, and because he would dare answer for us according to justice; and also he sent him because, as Alonso de Ávila had had differences with other captains and had great boldness to say to Cortés whatever he saw fitting to tell him, and to avoid quarrels, and to give the captaincy he held to Andrés de Tapia and the contaduría to Alonso de Grado — as he soon gave it — for these reasons he sent him.

Let us return to our relation: seeing Cortés that the wood for the brigantines was already cut, and the persons I named had gone to Cuba — who were of those of Narváez whom we held as dead weight, especially always putting fears that we would not be sufficient to resist the great power of the Mexicans when they heard us say we were going to lay siege to Mexico — free of those foolish fears, Cortés agreed we should go with all our soldiers to the city of Texcoco; and over this there were great and many counsels, because some soldiers said it was a better site, with canals and ditches, for making the brigantines in Ayotzingo next to Chalco than in the ditch and estuary; and others of us insisted it would be better in Texcoco, for being in a part and site near many towns, and having that city for ourselves — from there we would make entries into the neighboring lands of Mexico, and posted in that city we would take the better judgment as things succeeded. Since it was already agreed as I said, news comes and letters that soldiers brought: how a ship from Castile and the Canary Islands had come to the Rich Town, of good bearing, loaded with much merchandise, harquebuses, powder and crossbows and crossbow string, and three horses and other arms; and as lord of the merchandise and ship came a Juan de Burgos, and as master a Francisco de Amedel; and thirteen soldiers came. With that news we rejoiced greatly; and if before we knew of the ship we hurried our departure for Texcoco, much more then we hurried — because Cortés immediately sent to buy all the arms and powder and all else it brought, and even Juan de Burgos himself and Amedel and all the passengers it brought came at once by where we were; with whom we received them gladly, seeing such good relief at such a time.

I remember that then came a Juan del Espinar, resident who was of Guatemala, a person who was very rich; and also came a Basque called Monjaraz, uncle they said of Andrés de Monjaraz; also came a Sagredo, uncle of a woman called La Sagreda who was in Cuba — natives of the town of Medellín; and Gregorio de Monjaraz, soldiers who were with us, and father of a woman who afterward came to Mexico who was called La Monjaraça, a very beautiful woman. I bring this here to memory for what I will tell ahead; and it is that Monjaraz never went to any war nor entrada with us, because he went about sick at that time; and now that he was very well and healthy and presumed himself valiant, when we had laid siege to Mexico, Monjaraz said he wanted to go see how we battled with the Mexicans, because he did not hold the Mexicans for valiant; and he went and climbed up on a high cu [platform] like a little tower, and we never knew how or in what manner Indians killed him that same day. And many persons said they had known him on the island of Santo Domingo — that it was divine permission that he die that death, because he had killed his wife, a very honorable and good person, without any fault, and procured false witnesses who swore he practiced witchcraft.

I want now to leave off telling past things, and let us say how we went to the city of Texcoco and what else happened.</english>
<spanish>[CAPÍTULO CXXXVI.] Cómo demandaron licencia a Cortés los capitanes y personas más principales de los que Narváez había traído en su compañía para volverse a la isla de Cuba, y Cortés se la dio y se fueron; y de cómo despachó Cortés embajadores para Castilla y para Santo Domingo y Jamaica, y lo que sobre cada cosa acaeció.

Cuando vieron los capitanes de Narváez que ya teníamos socorros — así de los que vinieron de Cuba como los de Jamaica que había enviado Francisco de Garay para su armada, según lo tengo declarado en el capítulo que dello habla — y vieron que los pueblos de la provincia de Tepeaca estaban pacíficos, después de muchas palabras que a Cortés dijeron, con grandes ofertas y ruegos le suplicaron que les diese licencia para volverse a la isla de Cuba, pues se lo había prometido; y luego Cortés se la dio, y aun les prometió que si volvía a ganar la Nueva España y ciudad de México, a Andrés de Duero, su compañero, le daría mucho más oro del que le había dado antes; y así hizo ofertas a los demás capitanes, especialmente a Agustín Bermúdez. Les mandó dar matalotaje — que en aquella sazón había, que era maíz y perrillos salados y pocas gallinas — y un navío de los mejores. Escribió Cortés a su mujer, que se decía doña Catalina Juárez, la Marcaida, y a Juan Suárez, su cuñado, que en aquella sazón vivía en la isla de Cuba; les envió ciertas barras y joyas de oro y les hizo saber todos los desmanes y trabajos que nos habían acontecido y cómo nos echaron de México. Dejemos esto y digamos las personas que demandaron licencia para volver a Cuba, que todavía iban ricos: Andrés de Duero; y Agustín Bermúdez; Juan Bono de Queso; y Bernardino de Quesada; y Francisco Velázquez El Corcobado, pariente de Diego Velázquez, gobernador de Cuba; y Gonzalo Carrasco, el que vive en la Puebla, que después se volvió a esta Nueva España; y un Melchor de Velasco que fue alcalde de Guatemala; y un Ximénez que vive en Oaxaca, que fue por sus hijos; y el comendador Leonel de Cervantes, que fue por sus hijas — que, después de ganado México, las casó muy honradamente; y se fue uno que se decía Maldonado, natural de Medellín, que estaba doliente — no digo Maldonado el que fue marido de doña María del Rincón, ni Maldónado El Ancho, ni otro Maldonado que se decía Álvaro Maldonado El Fiero, que fue casado con una señora que se decía María Rias; y también se fue un Vargas, vecino de la Trinidad, que le llamaban en Cuba Vargas el Galán — no digo Vargas el que fue suegro de Cristóbal Lobo, vecino que fue de Guatemala; y se fue un soldado de los de Cortés que se decía Cárdenas, piloto — aquel Cárdenas fue el que dijo a un compañero suyo, cómo podíamos reposar los soldados, teniendo dos reyes en esta Nueva España. Este fue a quien Cortés dio trescientos pesos para que se fuese a su mujer e hijos; y, por escusar prolixidad de ponerles todos por memoria, se fueron otros muchos que no me acuerdo bien sus nombres. Y cuando Cortés les dio la licencia, dijimos que para qué se la daba, pues éramos pocos los que quedábamos; y respondió que por escusar escándalos e importunaciones, y que ya veíamos que para la guerra algunos de los que se volvían no lo eran, y que valía más estar solo que mal acompañado. Y para despacharlos del puerto envió Cortés a Pedro de Alvarado, y habiéndolos embarcado, que se volviese luego a la villa.

Y digamos ahora que también envió a Castilla a Diego de Ordás y a Alonso de Mendoza, natural de Medellín de Cáceres, con ciertos recados de Cortés — yo no sé otros que llevase por nosotros, ni nos dio parte de cosa de los negocios que enviaba a tratar con su majestad, ni lo que pasó en Castilla yo no lo alcanzé a saber, salvo que a boca llena decía el obispo de Burgos, delante de Diego de Ordás, que así Cortés como todos los soldados que pasamos con él éramos malos y traidores — puesto que Ordáz respondía muy bien por todos nosotros; y entonces le dieron a Ordás una encomienda de Señor Santiago, y por armas el volcán que estaba entre Huaxocingo y cerca de Cholula; y lo que negoció adelante lo diré según lo supimos por carta. Dejemos esto aparte y diremos cómo Cortés envió a Alonso de Ávila, que era capitán y contador de esta Nueva España, y juntamente con él envió a otro hidalgo que se decía Francisco Álvarez Chico, que era hombre que entendió de negocios; y mandó que fuesen con otro navío para la isla de Santo Domingo a hacer relación de todo lo acaecido a la Real Audiencia que en ella residía y a los frailes gerónimos que estaban por gobernadores de todas las islas — que tuviesen por bueno lo que habíamos hecho en las conquistas y el desbarate de Narváez, y cómo había hecho esclavos en los pueblos que habían muerto españoles y se habían quitado de la obediencia que habían dado a nuestro rey y señor, y que así entendía hacer en todos los más pueblos que fueron de la liga y nombre de mexicanos; y que les suplicaba que hiciesen relación dello en Castilla a nuestro gran Emperador, y tuviesen en la memoria los grandes servicios que siempre le hacíamos; y que por su intercesión y de la Real Audiencia y frailes gerónimos fuesemos favorecidos con justicia contra la mala voluntad y obras que contra nosotros trataba el obispo de Burgos y arzobispo de Rossano. También envió otro navío a la isla de Jamaica por caballos y yeguas; y el capitán que en él fue se decía Solano de Solís — que, después de ganado México, le llamamos Solís El de la Huerta; yerro de uno que se decía el bachiller Ortega.

Bien sé que dirán algunos curiosos lectores que, sin dineros — cómo enviaba a Diego de Ordás a negocios a Castilla, pues está claro que para Castilla y para otras partes son menester dineros; y que así mismo enviaba a Alonso de Ávila y a Francisco Álvarez Chico a Santo Domingo a negocios, y a la isla de Jamaica por caballos y yeguas. A esto digo que, al salir de México — como salimos huyendo la noche por mí muchas veces memorada — quedaba en la sala muchas barras de oro perdido, en un montón que todos los más soldados apañaban dello, especialmente los de a caballo y los de Narváez mucho mejor; y los oficiales de su majestad que la tenían en poder y cargo llevaron los fardos hechos; y demás de esto, cuando se cargaron de oro, más de ochenta indios tascaltecas, por mandado de Cortés, fueron los primeros que salieron en las pontes — vista cosa era que salvarían muchas cargas dello, que no se perdería todo en la calzada. Y como nosotros los pobres soldados que no teníamos mando, sino ser mandados, en aquella sazón procurábamos de salvar nuestras vidas y, después de curar nuestras heridas, no mirábamos en el oro si salieron muchas cargas dello en las pontes o no, ni se nos daba mucho por ello; Cortés, con algunos de nuestros capitanes, lo procuraron de haber de los tascaltecas que lo sacaron; y tuvimos sospecha que los que quedaban con diez mil pesos de las partes de los de la Villa Rica también lo tuvo, y echaron fama que lo habían robado. Y con ello envió a Castilla a los negocios de su persona y a comprar caballos, y a la isla de Santo Domingo a la Audiencia Real — porque en aquel tiempo todos se callaban con las barras de oro que tenían, aunque más pregones habían dado.

Dejemos esto y digamos cómo, ya estando de paz todos los pueblos comarcanos de Tepeaca, acordó Cortés que quedase en la villa de Segura de la Frontera por capitán un Francisco de Orozco, con obra de veinte soldados que estaban heridos y dolientes; y con todos los más de nuestro ejército fuimos a Tlaxcala. Se dio orden que se cortase madera para hacer trece bergantines para ir otra vez a México, porque hallábamos por muy cierto que para la laguna, sin bergantines, no la podíamos señorear, ni podíamos dar guerra ni entrar otra vez por las calzadas en aquella gran ciudad, sino con gran riesgo de nuestras vidas. — Y el que fue maestro de cortar la madera, y de dar el calado y cuenta y razón de cómo habían de ser veleros y ligeros para aquel efecto, y los hizo, fue un Martín López que ciertamente, demás de ser un buen soldado en todas las guerras, sirvió muy bien a su majestad en esto de los bergantines y trabajó en ellos como fuerte varón. Y me parece que si por desdicha no viniera en nuestra compañía de los primeros, como vino, hasta enviar por otro maestro a Castilla se pasara mucho tiempo, o no viniera ninguno — según el gran estorbo que en todo nos ponía el obispo de Burgos.

Volveré a nuestra materia y digamos ahora que, cuando llegamos a Tlaxcala, ya era fallecido de viruelas nuestro gran amigo y muy leal vasallo de su majestad Mase Escaci; de cuya muerte nos pesó a todos, y Cortés lo sintió tanto, como él decía, como si fuera su padre, y se puso luto de mantas negras; y así mismo muchos de nuestros capitanes y soldados. Y a sus hijos y parientes del Mase Escaci, Cortés y todos nosotros les hacíamos mucha honra; y porque en Tlaxcala había diferencias sobre el mando y cacicazgo, señaló y mandó que lo fuese un su hijo legítimo del mismo Mase Escaci, porque así lo había mandado su padre antes que muriese; y aun dijo a sus hijos y parientes que mirasen que no saliesen del mando de Malinche y de sus hermanos, porque ciertamente éramos los que habíamos de señorear estas tierras; y les dijo otros muchos buenos consejos. Dejemos ya de contar del Mase Escaci, pues es ya el muerto, y digamos del Xicotenga el viejo y de Chichimecatecle y de todos los más caciques de Tlaxcala que se ofrecieron de servir a Cortés — así en cortar la madera para los bergantines como para todo lo demás que les quisiesen mandar en la guerra contra mexicanos. Cortés les abrazó con mucho amor y les dio gracias por ello, especialmente al Xicotenga el viejo y a Chichimecatecle; y luego procuró que se volviese cristiano, y el buen viejo Xicotenga de buena voluntad dijo que lo quería ser; y con la mayor fiesta que en aquella sazón se pudo hacer en Tlaxcala, le bautizó el padre de la Merced y le puso nombre don Lorenzo de Vargas.

Volvamos a decir de nuestros bergantines: que el Martín López se dio tanta prisa en cortar la madera, con la gran ayuda de indios que le ayudaban, que en pocos días la tenía ya toda cortada y señalada su cuenta en cada madero, para qué parte y lugar había de ser — según tienen sus señales los oficiales, maestros y carpinteros de ribera. También le ayudaba otro buen soldado que se decía Andrés Núñez, y un viejo carpintero que estaba cojo de una herida que se decía Ramírez el viejo. Luego despachó Cortés a la Villa Rica por mucho hierro y clavazón de los navíos que dimos al través, y por anclas y velas y jarcias y cables y estopa, y por todo aparejo de hacer navíos; y mandó venir todos los herreros que había, y a un Hernando de Aguilar que era medio herrero que ayudaba a machar. Y porque en aquel tiempo había en nuestro real tres hombres que se decían Aguilar, llamamos a este Hernando de Aguilar Maxa Yerro; y envió por capitán a la Villa Rica por los aparejos que el dicho [mandado] mandó traer a un regidor de Santa Cruz de Burgos que después fue de México — persona muy buen soldado y diligente —; y hasta las calderas para hacer brea, y todo cuanto de antes habían sacado de los navíos, trajo con más de mil indios que todos los pueblos de aquellas provincias, enemigos de mexicanos, luego se los daban para traer las cargas. Puesto que no teníamos pez para brear, ni aun los indios lo sabían hacer, mandó Cortés a cuatro hombres de la mar que sabían de aquel oficio que en unos pinares cerca de Huaxocingo, que los hay buenos, fuesen a hacer la pez.

Pasemos adelante — y puesto que no va muy a propósito de la materia en que estaba hablando, pues me han preguntado ciertos caballeros curiosos que conocían muy bien a Alonso de Ávila: cómo, siendo capitán y muy esforzado, y era contador de la Nueva España, y siendo belicoso y su inclinación dado más para guerras que no para ir a solicitar negocios con los frailes gerónimos que estaban por gobernadores de todas las islas, por qué causa le envió Cortés, teniendo otros hombres que fueran más acostumbrados a negocios, como era un Alonso de Grado o un Juan de Cáceres El Rico, y otros que me nombraron — a esto digo que Cortés le envió a Alonso de Ávila porque sintió del ser muy varón, y porque osaría responder por nosotros conforme a justicia; y también le envió porque, como Alonso de Ávila había tenido diferencias con otros capitanes y tenía gran atrevimiento de decir a Cortés cualquiera cosa que veía que convenía decirle, y por escusar ruidos, y por dar la capitanía que tenía a Andrés de Tapia y la contaduría a Alonso de Grado — como luego se la dio —, por estas razones le envió.

Volvamos a nuestra relación: pues viendo Cortés que ya era cortada la madera para los bergantines y se habían ido a Cuba las personas por mí nombradas — que eran de los de Narváez que los teníamos por sobrehuesos, especialmente poniendo temores que siempre nos ponían de que no seríamos bastantes para resistir el gran poder de mexicanos cuando oían que decíamos que habíamos de ir a poner cerco sobre México —, libre de aquellas sofobras, acordó Cortés que fuesemos con todos nuestros soldados para la ciudad de Tezcuco; y sobre ello hubo grandes y muchos acuerdos, porque unos soldados decían que era mejor sitio y acequias y zanjas para hacer los bergantines en Ayotzingo, junto a Chalco, que no en la zanja y estero; y otros porfiábamos que mejor sería en Tezcuco, por estar en parte y sitio cerca de muchos pueblos, y que teniendo aquella ciudad por nosotros, desde allí haríamos entradas en las tierras comarcanas de México, y puestos en aquella ciudad tomaríamos el mejor parecer conforme sucediesen las cosas. Ya que estaba acordado lo por mí dicho, viene nueva y cartas que trajeron soldados: cómo había venido a la Villa Rica un navío de Castilla y de las islas de Canaria, de buen porte, cargado de muchas mercaderías, escopetas, pólvora y ballestas e hilo de ballestas, y tres caballos y otras armas; y venía por señor de la mercadería y navío un Juan de Burgos, y por maestre un Francisco de Amedel; y venían trece soldados. Con aquella nueva nos alegramos en gran manera; y si de antes que supiésemos del navío nos dábamos prisa en la partida para Tezcuco, mucho más nos dimos entonces — porque luego envió Cortés a comprar todas las armas y pólvora y todo lo más que traía; y aun el mismo Juan de Burgos y el Medel y todos los pasajeros que traía se vinieron luego por donde estábamos; con los cuales recibimos contento, viendo tan buen socorro en tal tiempo.

Acuérdome que entonces vino un Juan del Espinar, vecino que fue de Guatemala, persona que fue muy rico; y también vino un vizcaíno que se decía Monjaraz, tío que se decía ser de Andrés de Monjaraz; también vino un Sagredo, tío de una mujer que se decía la Sagreda, que estaba en Cuba — naturales de la villa de Medellín; y Gregorio de Monjaraz, soldados que estaban con nosotros, y padre de una mujer que después vino a México que se decía la Monjaraça, muy hermosa mujer. Traigo esto aquí a la memoria por lo que adelante diré; y es que jamás fue el Monjaraz a guerra ninguna ni entrada con nosotros, porque andaba doliente en aquel tiempo; y ya que estaba muy bueno y sano y presumía de valiente, cuando teníamos puesto cerco a México, dijo el Monjaraz que quería ir a ver cómo batallábamos con los mexicanos, porque no tenía a los mexicanos por valientes; y fue y se subió en un alto cu, como torrecilla, y nunca supimos cómo ni de qué manera le mataron indios en aquel mismo día. Y muchas personas dijeron que le habían conocido en la isla de Santo Domingo — que fue permisión divina que muriese aquella muerte, porque había muerto a su mujer, muy honrada y buena persona, sin culpa ninguna, y buscó testigos falsos que juraron que le hacía maleficio.

Quiero ya dejar de contar cosas pasadas, y digamos cómo fuimos a la ciudad de Tezcuco y lo que más pasó.</spanish>

=== CAPITULO CXXXVIII—Como fuymos A — Section 139 ===
<english>CHAPTER CXXXVIII — How we went to Iztapalapa with Cortés, and he took Cristóbal de Olid and Pedro de Alvarado in his company, and left Gonzalo de Sandoval as guard at Texcoco, and what befell us in the taking of that town and other things that were done there.

Well, as we had already been twelve days at Texcoco, and the Tlaxcalans — whom I have mentioned before on other occasions — had nothing with which to sustain themselves, and so that there might be enough for as many as they were, because the people of Texcoco could not supply them adequately, and so that they would not become a burden to them, and also because the Tlaxcalans were eager to make war on the Mexicans and take revenge for the many Tlaxcalans whom, in the past defeats I have already described, the Mexicans had killed and sacrificed — Cortés agreed that he, as captain general, with Andrés de Tapia and Cristóbal de Olid, and thirteen horsemen and twenty crossbowmen and six arquebusiers and two hundred and twenty soldiers, and with our friends the Tlaxcalans, and with another twenty principals of Texcoco whom Don Hernando gave us — and we knew they were cousins and kinsmen of the same cacique and enemies of Guatemuz, whom they had already raised as king in Mexico — we set out on the road to Iztapalapa, which lies from Texcoco about four leagues. I have already said on other occasions, in the chapter [ ] that speaks of it, that more than half the houses were built on the water and the other half on dry land.

And as we went our way with good order, as was our custom, and as the Mexicans always kept sentries and garrisons and warriors ready against us when they knew we were going to make war on some of their towns, so as to come to their relief at once — they did the same here. They let the people of Iztapalapa know so that they might prepare themselves, and they sent them about eight thousand Mexicans as reinforcements. So on dry land they waited like good warriors, both the Mexicans who came to their aid and the people of the town of Iztapalapa, and they fought us a good while, very boldly. But the horsemen broke through them, and with the crossbows and arquebuses, and all our friends the Tlaxcalans, who threw themselves at them like rabid dogs, they quickly left the field and withdrew into their town.

And this was something planned beforehand, and a stratagem they had agreed among themselves, which would have done us great harm if we had not quickly got out of that town where they had gone in. And it happened in this way: they made as if they had fled and got into canoes on the water and into the houses they had on the lagoon, and others into reed beds. And as it was already dark night, they let us lodge on dry land without noise or show of war. With the booty we had taken we were content — and all the more with the victory. And being posted in that manner, with sentries and spies and patrols, and even runners in the field, when we were not on our guard so much water came through the whole town that, if the principals we had brought from Texcoco had not shouted and warned us to leave the houses quickly for dry land, we would all have drowned. For they had opened the canals of fresh and salt water and broken a causeway, so that everything was quickly flooded.

And our friends the Tlaxcalans, as they were not used to the water and do not know how to swim, two of them died. We ourselves, with great risk to our persons, all well soaked and our gunpowder ruined, got out without our baggage. And as we were in that state, and with great cold, and even without supper, we passed a bad night. And the worst of all was the mockery and the shouting and the whistles they gave us — those of Iztapalapa and the Mexicans from their houses and canoes.

Then another worse thing befell us. For in Mexico they knew the plan they had made to drown us by breaking the causeway and the canals, and many battalions of warriors were waiting on land and in the lagoon. And at daybreak they gave us so much war that we had our hands full keeping them from routing us. They killed two soldiers and a horse, and wounded many others, both among our soldiers and the Tlaxcalans. Little by little they slackened in the fighting, and we returned to Texcoco somewhat shamed by the mockery and the stratagem of flooding us — and also because we did not gain much reputation in the last battle they gave us, for there was no gunpowder. But still they remained fearful, and they had their hands full burying or burning their dead, and tending their wounded, and repairing their houses. There I will leave them, as other towns came in peace to Texcoco, and what else was done.</english>
<spanish>CAPÍTULO CXXXVIII — Cómo fuimos a Iztapalapa con Cortés, y llevó en su compañía a Cristóbal de Olid y a Pedro de Alvarado, y quedó Gonzalo de Sandoval por guarda de Texcoco, y lo que nos aconteció en la toma de aquel pueblo y otras cosas que allí se hicieron.

Pues, como había doce días que estábamos en Texcoco, y no tenían los tlaxcaltecas, por mí ya otras veces memorados, con qué sustentarse, y para que lo tuviesen tantos como eran, porque no se lo podían dar abundantemente los de Texcoco, y para que no recibiesen pesadumbre de ellos, y también porque estaban deseosos los tlaxcaltecas de guerrear con mexicanos y vengarse por los muchos tlaxcaltecas que, en las derrotas pasadas por mí memoradas, les habían muerto y sacrificado, acordó Cortés que él, por capitán general, con Andrés de Tapia y Cristóbal de Olid, y trece de a caballo y veinte ballesteros y seis escopeteros y doscientos y veinte soldados, y con nuestros amigos los de Tlaxcala, y con otros veinte principales de Texcoco que nos dio don Hernando — y estos sabíamos que eran sus primos y parientes del mismo cacique y enemigos de Guatemuz, que ya le habían alzado por rey en México —, fuimos camino de Iztapalapa, que está de Texcoco obra de cuatro leguas. Ya he dicho otras veces, en el capítulo [ ] que sobrello habla, que estaban más de la mitad de las casas edificadas en el agua y la otra mitad en tierra firme.

Y yendo nuestro camino con mucho concierto, como lo teníamos de costumbre, y como los mexicanos siempre tenían velas y guarniciones y guerreros contra nosotros cuando sabían que íbamos a dar guerra a algunos de sus pueblos para luego socorrerles, así lo hicieron saber a los de Iztapalapa para que se apercibiesen, y les enviaron sobre ocho mil mexicanos de socorro. Por manera que en tierra firme aguardaron, como buenos guerreros, así los mexicanos que fueron en su ayuda como los del pueblo de Iztapalapa, y pelearon un buen rato, muy valerosamente, con nosotros. Mas los de a caballo rompieron por ellos, y con las ballestas y escopetas, y todos nuestros amigos los tlaxcaltecas, que se metían en ellos como perros rabiosos, de presto dejaron el campo y se metieron en su pueblo.

Y esto fue cosa pensada de antemano, y con un ardid que entre ellos tenían acordado, que fuera harto daño para nosotros si de presto no saliéramos de aquel pueblo donde entraban. Y fue desta manera: hicieron que huyeron y se metieron en canoas, en el agua y en las casas que tenían en la laguna, y otros de ellos en unos carrizales. Y como ya era noche oscura, nos dejaron aposentar en tierra firme sin hacer ruido ni muestras de guerra. Y con el despojo que habíamos habido estábamos contentos, y más con la victoria. Y estando de aquella manera puestos, que teníamos velas y espías y rondas, y aun corredores del campo, cuando no nos cuidamos, vino tanta agua por todo el pueblo que, si los principales que llevábamos de Texcoco no dieran voces y nos avisaran que saliésemos presto de las casas a tierra firme, todos quedáramos ahogados. Porque soltaron las acequias de agua dulce y salada y abrieron una calzada con que de presto se hinchió todo de agua.

Y los tlaxcaltecas, nuestros amigos, como no eran acostumbrados al agua y no saben nadar, quedaron muertos dos de ellos. Y nosotros, con gran riesgo de nuestras personas, todos bien mojados y la pólvora perdida, salimos sin hato. Y como estábamos de aquella manera, y con mucho frío, y aun sin cenar, pasamos mala noche. Y lo peor de todo era la burla y la grita y silvos que nos daban los de Iztapalapa y los mexicanos desde sus casas y canoas.

Pues otra cosa peor nos vino. Que, como en México sabían el concierto que tenían hecho de anegarnos con haber roto la calzada y acequias, estaban esperando en tierra y en la laguna muchos batallones de guerreros. Y desde que amaneció nos dieron tanta guerra que harto teníamos de sostenernos contra ellos, que no nos desbaratasen. Y mataron dos soldados y un caballo, e hirieron otros muchos, así de nuestros soldados como tlaxcaltecas. Y poco a poco aflojaron en la guerra, y nos volvimos a Texcoco medio afrentados de la burla y ardid de echarnos el agua, y también como no ganamos mucha reputación en la batalla postrera que nos dieron, porque no había pólvora. Mas todavía quedaron temerosos, y tuvieron bien en qué entender en enterrar o quemar muertos, y curar heridos, y reparar sus casas. Donde los dejaré, como vinieron de paz a Texcoco otros pueblos, y lo que más se hizo.</spanish>
<flags>voice: Bernal Díaz del Castillo — blunt, garrulous old soldier, plain and concrete, quietly aggrieved, proud eyewitness (held). ⚠ NO REFERENCE —. catamos→cuidamos (OCR/context). === CAPITULO CXXXI. como vino vn navio de — Section 132 ===
<english>CHAPTER CXXXI. How a ship came from Cuba that Diego Velázquez sent, with Pedro Barba as captain aboard it, and the way the admiral our Cortés had appointed as guardian of the sea had of taking them — and it was in this manner.

Since we were in that province of Tepeaca, punishing those who had had a hand in the death of our companions — the ones who killed them in those towns — and drawing them to peace, and all were giving obedience to His Majesty, letters came from Villa Rica saying a ship had arrived at the port. Aboard as captain came a gentleman called Pedro Barba, a great friend of Cortés. This Pedro Barba had served as lieutenant to Diego Velázquez in Havana, and brought thirteen soldiers, a horse, and a mare, for the ship he came in was very small. He carried letters for Pánfilo de Narváez, the captain Diego Velázquez had sent against us, believing New Spain was his — in which Velázquez wrote to tell him that if he had not killed Cortés, Cortés was to be sent at once to Cuba as a prisoner, to be shipped to Castile, as Don Juan Rodríguez de Fonseca, bishop of Burgos and archbishop of Rosano, president of the Indies, had ordered: that he and our other captains were to be seized at once, for Diego Velázquez was certain we were destroyed, or at the least that Narváez ruled New Spain.

When Pedro Barba reached the port with his ship and dropped anchor, the admiral went at once to visit him and welcome him. This was the admiral of the sea whom Cortés had appointed — called, as I recall, Pedro Caballero or Juan Caballero — who stood with Cortés, in a launch well manned with sailors and hidden arms, and he went to Pedro Barba’s ship. After words of courteous greeting, as one does when such a visitor arrives, they doff their caps and embrace one another. They ask the page about Señor Diego Velázquez, governor of Cuba, how he fares, and Pedro Barba answers that he is well. Pedro Barba and those with him ask after Señor Captain Pánfilo de Narváez and how things stand with Cortés, and they answer that very well — that Cortés is on the run, risen in rebellion with twenty of his followers, that Narváez is prospering and rich, and that the country is excellent. In talk after talk they tell Pedro Barba there is a town close by, that he should disembark and go sleep there and stay in it, and that they will bring food and whatever he needs — for that town alone is set apart for just that purpose. They say so much to him that, in the launch and in the others, men from the other anchored ships who were coming up right then took them ashore; and once they were off the ship, there was already a band of sailors together with Admiral Pedro Caballero. They said to Pedro Barba: “You are a prisoner by order of my lord Señor Captain Hernando Cortés” — and so they seized them. They stood astonished. At once they took from the ship its sails, rudder, and compasses and sent them to where we were with Cortés in Tepeaca. We were greatly pleased by the relief that arrived at the very best time it could have come, for in those expeditions I speak of we were not so safe that many of our soldiers went unhurt, and others wore themselves sick with the labor — from blood and dust caked in our bellies we could bring up nothing else through our mouths, since we always carried our arms on our backs and never rested night or day. Five of our soldiers had already died of pleurisy in about fifteen days.

I must also say that with this Pedro Barba came a Francisco López, vecino and former regidor of Guatemala. Cortés paid Pedro Barba great honor and made him captain of the crossbowmen. He brought word that another small ship in Cuba Diego Velázquez meant to send him, with cassava and provisions. It came eight days later, with a gentleman from Medina del Campo as captain, called Rodrigo Morejón de Lobera, bringing eight soldiers, six crossbowmen, much thread for cordage, and a mare. No differently than they had taken Pedro Barba, they did the same to this Rodrigo Morejón. Then they went to a safe place on the frontier, and we all rejoiced with them. Cortés honored them greatly and gave them commands, and thanks to God we were already growing stronger — with soldiers, crossbowmen, and two or three horses more. Leaving this here, I will return to tell what the Mexican armies on the frontier were doing at Guacachula, and how the caciques of that town came secretly to ask Cortés for favor to drive them out from there [ ]</english>
<spanish>CAPÍTULO CXXXI. Cómo vino un navío de Cuba que enviaba Diego Velázquez, y venía en él por capitán Pedro Barba, y la manera que el almirante que puso nuestro Cortés por guarda de la mar tenía para prenderlos, y es desta manera.

Pues como andábamos en aquella provincia de Tepeaca castigando a los que fueron en la muerte de nuestros compañeros que fueron los que mataron en aquellos pueblos e atrayéndolos de paz, y todos daban la obediencia a su Magestad, vinieron cartas de la Villa Rica, como había venido un navío al puerto, e vino en él por capitán un hidalgo que se decía Pedro Barba, muy amigo de Cortés, y este Pedro Barba había estado por teniente del Diego Velázquez en la Habana y traía trece soldados y un caballo y una yegua, porque el navío que traía era muy chico, y traía cartas para Pánfilo de Narváez, el capitán que Diego Velázquez había enviado contra nosotros, creyendo que estaba por él la Nueva España, en que le enviaba a decir el Velázquez que si no había muerto a Cortés que luego se le enviase a Cuba preso para enviarle a Castilla, que así lo mandaba don Juan Rodríguez de Fonseca, obispo de Burgos e arzobispo de Rosano, presidente de Indias, que luego fuese preso con otros nuestros capitanes, porque el Diego Velázquez tenía por cierto que éramos desbaratados o al de menos que Narváez señoreaba la Nueva España.

Pues como el Pedro Barba llegó al puerto con su navío y echó anclas, luego le fue a visitar y dar el bienvenido. El almirante de la mar que puso Cortés, el cual se decía Pedro Caballero o Juan Caballero, por mi memorado, que estaba por Cortés, con un batel bien escalfado de marineros y armas encubiertas, e fue al navío del Pedro Barba; y después de hablar palabras de buen comedimiento, que tal viene, quitan las gorras y abrazarse unos a otros como se suele hacer, preguntan al page escudero por el señor Diego Velázquez, gobernador de Cuba, qué tal quedaba, y responden el Pedro Barba que bueno; y el Pedro Barba y los demás que consigo traían preguntan por el señor capitán Pánfilo de Narváez y cómo le va con Cortés, y responden que muy bien, y que Cortés anda huyendo e alzado con veinte de sus compañeros, y que Narváez está muy próspero e rico, y que la tierra es muy buena; y de plática en plática les dicen al Pedro Barba que allí junto está un pueblo, que desembarque y que se vayan a dormir y estar en él, y que les traerán comida, y lo que hubiere menester, que para solo aquel efecto está señalado aquel pueblo; y tantas palabras les dicen que, en el batel y en otros que luego allí venían de los otros navíos que estaban surtos, les sacaron en tierra; y desde que los vieron fuera del navío, ya tenía copia de marineros juntos con el almirante Pedro Caballero. Dijeron al Pedro Barba: “Sed preso por el señor capitán Hernando Cortés, mi señor” — y así los prendían, y quedaban espantados; y luego les sacaban del navío las velas y timón y agujas, y las enviaban adonde estábamos con Cortés en Tepeaca, con los cuales habíamos gran placer con el socorro que venía en el mejor tiempo que podía ser, porque en aquellas entradas que digo que hacíamos no éramos tan en salvo que a muchos de nuestros soldados no quedábamos heridos, y otros adolecían del trabajo, porque de sangre y polvo que estaba cuajado en las entrañas no echábamos otra cosa del cuerpo por la boca, como traíamos siempre las armas a cuestas y no parar noches ni días, por manera que ya se habían muerto cinco de nuestros soldados de dolor de costado en obra de quince días.

También quiero decir que con este Pedro Barba vino un Francisco López, vecino y regidor que fue de Guatemala, y Cortés hacía mucha honra al Pedro Barba y le hizo capitán de ballesteros, el cual dio nuevas de que estaba otro navío chico en Cuba que le quería enviar el Diego Velázquez con caçabi y bastimentos, el cual vino de allí a ocho días, y venía en él por capitán un hidalgo natural de Medina del Campo que se decía Rodrigo Morejón de Lobera, y traía consigo ocho soldados y seis ballesteros y mucho hilo para cuerdas y una yegua; e ni más ni menos que habían prendido al Pedro Barba, así hicieron a este Rodrigo Morejón, y luego fueron a segura de la frontera, y con todos ellos nos alegramos, y Cortés les hacía mucha honra y les daba cargos; y gracias a Dios ya nos íbamos fortaleciendo con soldados y ballesteros y dos o tres caballos más. Y dejando esto aquí, volveré a decir lo que en Guacachula hacían los ejércitos mexicanos que estaban en frontera, y cómo los caciques de aquel pueblo vinieron secretamente a demandar favor a Cortés para echarlos de allí [ ]</spanish>
<flags>voice: Bernal Díaz del Castillo — blunt, garrulous old soldier, plain and concrete, quietly aggrieved, proud eyewitness (held). ⚠ NO REFERENCE —. === CAPITULO CXXXVII [CXXXIX] como — Section 140 ===
<english>CHAPTER CXXXVII [CXXXIX]: how three neighboring towns of Texcoco came to ask for peace and pardon for the past wars and deaths of Spaniards, and the excuses they gave about it; and how Gonzalo de Sandoval went to Chalco and Tamanalco in their aid against the Mexicans, and what else happened.

Having been two days in Texcoco on our return from the entry at Iztapalapa, three towns at peace came to Cortés to ask pardon for the past wars and for the deaths of Spaniards they had killed, and for the excuses they gave. They said it was the lord of Mexico who had been raised up after the death of the great Montezuma, who was called Cuitláhuac, that by his command they had gone out to make war with the rest of his vassals, and that if some Teules [gods] were killed and captured and robbed, that same lord had commanded them to take them to Mexico, and that thus they did it — and the Teules they took to Mexico to sacrifice — and they also took the gold and horses and clothing; and that now they asked pardon for it, and that for this reason they had no blame at all, for they had been commanded and forced to do it.

The towns I say that came at that season were called Tepezcuico and Otumba; I do not remember the name of the other town; but it is said that at this one, Otumba, was the famous battle they gave us when we fled from Mexico, where the greatest squadrons of warriors that had ever been gathered in all New Spain stood together against us, where they believed we would not escape with our lives — as I have written at greater length in the past chapters that speak of it. And as those towns found themselves guilty and had seen that we had gone to Iztapalapa and our going had not gone very well for them, and although they had wanted to drown us with water and had awaited two pitched battles with many Mexican squadrons — in the end, so as not to find themselves in another like the past ones, they came to ask for peace before we went to their towns to punish them. And Cortés, seeing that it was not a time to do anything else, pardoned them, although he gave them great reprimands about it; and they bound themselves with words of many offers always to be against the Mexicans and to be vassals of His Majesty and to serve us, and thus they did.

Let us leave off speaking of these towns, and let us say how there then came to ask for peace and our friendship the people of a town that lies in the lagoon called Mezquique, which by another name we called Benaventura. And these, as it seemed, had never been on good terms with the Mexicans and hated them wholeheartedly; and Cortés and all of us held the coming of this town in high regard, for being inside the water, to hold them as friends; and with them we believed their neighbors who were also settled in the water would be summoned. And Cortés thanked them greatly and, with offers and soft words, dismissed them.

Then, while we were in this state, they came to tell Cortés that great squadrons of Mexicans were coming against the four towns that had first come to our friendship, which were called Guatinchan or Guaxultlan — I do not remember the names of the other two towns — and they told Cortés they would not dare wait in their houses and that they wanted to go to the mountains or come to Texcoco, where we were; and they told Cortés so many things for him to go aid them that he immediately prepared twenty horsemen and two hundred soldiers, and thirteen crossbowmen, and ten arquebusiers, and took in his company Pedro de Alvarado and Cristóbal de Olid, who was more of the field; and we went to the towns that had come to Cortés making so many complaints — it would be about two leagues from Texcoco — and, as it seemed, it was true that the Mexicans were sending them warnings with threats that they would destroy them and make war on them for having taken our friendship. But what they threatened them most about and had disputes over were some great maize-field labors that were already ready to harvest near the lagoon where those of Texcoco and those towns supplied our camp; and the Mexicans wanted to take the maize from them because they said it was theirs, and that valley of maize fields those four towns were accustomed to sow and work for the priests of the Mexican idols; and over these maize fields many Indians had killed one another.

And after Cortés understood this, after telling them not to fear and to stay in their houses, he ordered that when they had to go gather the maize, both for their sustenance and to supply our camp, he would send for that purpose a captain with many horsemen and soldiers to guard those who went to bring the maize; and with what Cortés told them they were very content, and we returned to Texcoco. And from then on, whenever there was need in our camp for maize, we prepared the tamemes [porters] from all those towns, and with our friends the Tlaxcalans and with ten horsemen and a hundred soldiers, with some crossbowmen and arquebusiers, we went for the maize; and I say this because I went twice for it, and once we had a good skirmish with great squadrons of Mexicans who had come in more than a thousand canoes waiting for us in the maize fields. And although we had friends, the Mexicans fought very much like men; we made them embark in their canoes, and there one of our soldiers was killed and twelve wounded, and likewise certain Tlaxcalans were wounded; and they did not go off praising themselves much, for there fifteen or twenty lay stretched out, and another five we carried away prisoners.

Let us leave off speaking of this and say how another day we had news that those of Chalco and Tamanalco and their subjects wanted to come in peace, but because of the Mexican garrisons that were in their towns they were not allowed, and they did much harm in their land and took their women — moreover, if they were beautiful, in front of their fathers or mothers or husbands they had access to them. And likewise, as the wood for making the brigantines was cut in Tlaxcala and set ready, and time passed without bringing it to Texcoco, we all felt great grief about it, the greater part of the soldiers. And besides this, from the town of Benaventura that was called Mezquique and from other towns our friends came to tell Cortés that the Mexicans were going to make war on them for having taken our friendship; and also our friends the Tlaxcalans, as they already had gathered certain clothing and salt and other things from spoils and gold, and some of them wanted to return to their land, did not dare for lack of a safe road.

Seeing Cortés that to aid some towns that asked him for help and to go help those of Chalco so they would come to our friendship he could not give attention to one group or the other — because there in Texcoco we always had to keep our beards on our shoulders [very much on guard] and be very alert — what he decided was that everything be left behind and the first thing done be to go to Chalco and Tamanalco. And for that he sent Gonzalo de Sandoval and Francisco de Lugo with fifteen horsemen and two hundred soldiers, with arquebusiers and crossbowmen and our friends the Tlaxcalans, and that he should strive in every way to break up and destroy the Mexican garrisons and that they leave Chalco and Tamanalco, so the road to Tlaxcala would be very clear and they could go and come to Villa Rica without opposition from Mexican warriors.

And as soon as this was agreed very secretly with Indians of Texcoco, the Chalco people were told, so they would be very prepared to strike by day or by night at the Mexican garrisons; and the Chalco people, who were expecting nothing else, prepared themselves very well. And as Gonzalo de Sandoval went with his army, it seemed good to him to leave in the rear guard five horsemen and as many crossbowmen, with all the Tlaxcalans who were loaded with the spoils they had gotten. And as the Mexicans always had lookouts and spies and knew our people were bound for Chalco, they had newly prepared — besides those who were in garrison in Chalco — many squadrons of warriors who fell upon the rear where the Tlaxcalans were going with their load; and they treated them badly, so that the five horsemen and crossbowmen could not resist, because two crossbowmen were killed and the rest wounded — so that although Gonzalo de Sandoval very quickly turned back on them and routed them and killed ten Mexicans, and as the lagoon was near they crowded into the canoes they had come in — and because all those lands are very populated with subjects of Mexico — and after he had put them to flight and saw that of the five horsemen he had left with the crossbowmen and arquebusiers in the rear guard two crossbowmen were dead and the rest wounded, they and their horses — and even having seen all this, he did not fail to tell those he had left in his defense that they had been good for little, not having been able to resist the enemies and defend themselves and our friends; and he was very angry with them, because they were among the newly come from Castile, and told them it seemed to him they did not know what war was. And then he put safe all the Tlaxcalan Indians with their clothing; and he also dispatched some letters Cortés sent to Villa Rica, in which he told the captain who remained there everything that had happened concerning our conquests and the thought he had of putting siege to Mexico, and that they should always be very careful keeping watch, and that if there were soldiers disposed to take arms he should send them to Tlaxcala and that from there they should not pass until the roads were safer, because they would run risk. And the messengers dispatched and the Tlaxcalans set in their land, Sandoval turned back toward Chalco, which was very near there, and with great order his scouts ahead in the field, because he well understood that from all those towns and hamlets along his route he was going to have ambush from Mexicans.

And going on his road near Chalco, he saw many Mexican squadrons coming against him on a flat field, although there were great maize-field labors and maguey fields from which they draw the wine they drink; they gave him a good fight of bar and arrow and stones with slings and with long lances to kill the horses — so that Sandoval, when he saw so many warriors against him, encouraging his men, broke through them twice, and with the arquebuses and crossbowmen and with the few friends who had remained routed them. And although five soldiers and six horses and many friends were wounded, he pressed them so hard and with such fury that they paid him very well for the harm they had first done. And when the Chalco people, who were near, learned of it, they came out to receive Sandoval on the road and gave him great honor and celebration; and in that rout eight Mexicans were captured, and three very principal persons.

Then another day Sandoval said he wanted to return to Texcoco, and those of Chalco told him they wanted to go with him to see and speak to Malinche and bring with them two sons of the lord of that province, who had died a few days before of smallpox; and that before he died he had charged all his principals and elders to bring his sons to see the captain and that by his hand they should be lords of Chalco, and that all should strive to be subjects of the great king of the Teules, because certainly their ancestors had told them that men who would come with beards from where the sun rises would rule those lands; and by what they had seen, we were those men. And then Sandoval went with his whole army to Texcoco and brought in his company the sons of the lord and the other principals and the eight Mexican prisoners.

And after Cortés learned of his arrival he rejoiced greatly; and after Sandoval had given him account of his journey and how those lords of Chalco were coming, he went to his quarters, and the caciques soon came before Cortés; and after making him great obeisance they told him the will they brought to be vassals of His Majesty, according to and in the manner the father of those two youths had commanded them, and that by his hand he should make them lords. And after they had spoken their reasoning they presented rich jewels, worth two hundred pesos of gold. And when Captain Cortés had understood it well through our languages Doña Marina and Gerónimo de Aguilar, he showed them much love and embraced them and by his hand gave the lordship of Chalco to the elder brother, with more than half the towns his subjects; and Tamanalco and Chimalhuacan he gave to the younger brother, with Cuauhnáhuac and other subject towns. And after other much reasoning from Cortés to the principal elders and with the newly elected caciques, they said they wanted to return to their land and would in everything serve His Majesty and us in our royal name against the Mexicans, and that with that will they had always been, and that because of the Mexican garrisons that had been in their province they had not come before now to give obedience. And they also gave news to Cortés that two Spaniards he had sent to that province for maize before we were driven from Mexico — so the Culuas would not kill them — one night put them safe with our friends at Guaxozingo, and there they saved their lives, which we already knew days before, because one of them was he who went to Tlaxcala; and Cortés thanked him much and asked him to wait there two days, because a captain was to be sent for the wood and planking to Tlaxcala and would take them in his company and put them in their land so the Mexicans would not come out on the road against them; and they were very content and thanked him much.

Let us leave off speaking of this and say how Cortés agreed to send to Mexico those eight prisoners Sandoval took in that rout at Chalco, to tell the lord who had then been raised as king, called Guatemuz, that he greatly wished they would not be cause of their ruin nor of that so great city; and that they should come in peace and he would pardon them the deaths and damages they had done us in it, and that nothing would be demanded of them; and that he should consider that wars at the beginning are easy to remedy and in the middle and end difficult, and that in the end they would be destroyed; and that they well knew of the palisades and war stores and arsenals of arrows and lances and macanas and round stones and slings and all kinds of war that they are continually making and preparing — for what is it to spend time in vain doing it; and why does he want all his people to die and the city to be destroyed; and that he should look at the great power of our Lord God in whom we believe and adore, who always helps us; and also that he should see that we have all the neighboring towns on our side, since the Tlaxcalans desire nothing but the same war to avenge the treacheries and deaths of their people that have been done to them; and that they lay down arms and come in peace; and he promised always to do them much honor; and Doña Marina and Aguilar told him many other good reasons and counsels on the case.

And those eight Indians, our messengers, went before Guatemuz; but he would not send any answer, but rather make palisades and war stores and send throughout his provinces to order that if any of us were taken undisciplined they should be brought to Mexico to sacrifice; and that when he sent to call them they should come at once with their arms; and he sent to remit and forgive many tributes and even to promise great promises.

Let us leave off speaking of the war preparations being made in Mexico, and let us say how again many Indians from the towns of Guautinchon or Guaxuntlan came battered by the Mexicans because they had taken our friendship and for the dispute over the maize fields they used to sow for the Mexican priests in the time they served them, as I said elsewhere in the chapter that speaks of it; and how near the lagoon of Mexico every week they came to make war on them and even carried away certain Indians prisoners to Mexico. And after Cortés saw this he agreed to go again in person, with a hundred soldiers and twenty horsemen and twelve arquebusiers and crossbowmen, and had good spies so when they felt Mexican squadrons coming they would tell him; and as it was one or two leagues from Texcoco — on a Wednesday morning at dawn — the Mexican squadrons were where they were, and he fought them so that he quickly broke them and they got into the lagoon in their canoes, and there four Mexicans were killed and another three captured; and Cortés returned with his men to Texcoco; and from then on the Culuas came no more against those towns.

And let us leave off this and say how Cortés sent Gonzalo de Sandoval to Tlaxcala for the wood and planking of the brigantines, and what else he did on the road —
